title: Edge client proxy config sketch
preview:
Purpose:
Quick personal sketch for an "edge" client-facing proxy that translates lightweight client requests into Redwood inference routes while handling auth, caching, and graceful fallbacks. Meant as a starting point for a small shim that runs near customers (edge or local) to reduce tails and enforce routing policies.

Design goals (high level):
- minimal client SDK changes (thin edge library)
- local/pinned routing: prefer region-local Redwood Dedicated when available
- offline-safe: queue + backoff for transient network issues
- transparent telemetry: attach trace-ids + token usage metadata for cost attribution
- configurable policy surface (per-app or per-route)

Non-goals:
- full model orchestration (that stays on Redwood control plane)
- heavy stateful model caching beyond short-lived KV for prefixes

Quick ASCII diagram:

  [Client app] -> [Edge proxy shim (client)] -> HTTPS -> [Redwood edge-proxy endpoint] -> routing -> [model pool / fallback]

Core responsibilities for the shim/proxy:
- assemble request envelope with headers (client-id, app-version, trace-id)
- sign requests using short-lived key from customer (rotated)
- local cache: prefix cache for common prompts (configurable TTL)
- batching window (opt-in) for small requests to reduce per-token cost
- fallback policy: if primary route fails, switch to lower-cost or hosted route depending on SLA setting

Headers / envelope shape (suggestion):
- X-RW-Client-Id: <app-id>
- X-RW-Client-Name: <human-readable>
- X-RW-Trace: <trace-id>
- Authorization: Bearer <shr-token>  # short-lived token obtained via customer KMS or local auth
- X-RW-Route-Preference: "latency|cost|isolation"

Request payload sketch (from shim -> Redwood):
{
  "type": "inference.request",
  "model": "redwood-alpha-2.8b",
  "input": {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "summarize: ..."}]},
  "cache_key_hint": "user:123:prompt-hash-abc",
  "client_metadata": {"app":"notes-mobile","version":"1.2.0"},
  "batching": {"enable": true, "window_ms": 8}  
}

Config knobs (shim level):
- route_preference: [latency, cost, isolation]
- fallback_strategy: [retry-primary, route-to-hosted, degrade-to-embedding-only]
- cache: {enabled: true, ttl_ms: 60000, max_entries: 1024, key_expiration_policy: lru}
- batching: {enabled: false, window_ms: 10, max_batch_size: 16}
- local_queue: {max_inflight: 8, retry_backoff_ms: 200, max_retries: 4}
- telemetry: {sample_rate: 0.1, include_token_counts: true}
- security: {kms_key_id: null, token_rotation_seconds: 3600}

Routing logic (pseudo):
1) If route_preference==latency -> prefer dedicated in same region if healthy.
2) Else if cost and a cheaper quantized variant available -> route to quantized variant (cost tradeoff).
3) If primary fails (>=2 transient errors) -> apply fallback_strategy.
4) If token TTL < 60s -> block requests until rotated token available (avoid 401s mid-stream).

Caching semantics notes:
- Cache warm path should only short-circuit for the same input fingerprint and stable model+version.
- Use cache_key_hint to tie to prompt template + user persona. Avoid caching free-form user text unless explicitly allowed.
- Cache should have a freshness gate: if model version changes (version_id in metadata) invalidate.

Telemetry / observability:
- Add X-RW-Trace to every request, propagate sampler flag.
- Emit events: request.received, request.routed, request.cache_hit/miss, request.fallback, request.error
- Include token estimates (input_tokens, output_tokens_est) in metrics but not logs (privacy).

Instrumentation example (span tags):
- rw.client.app_id
- rw.client.route_pref
- rw.model.version
- rw.cache.hit
- rw.batch.size

Local config YAML (example):
---
route_preference: latency
fallback_strategy: route-to-hosted
cache:
  enabled: true
  ttl_ms: 30000
  max_entries: 2048
batching:
  enabled: true
  window_ms: 12
  max_batch_size: 8
telemetry:
  sample_rate: 0.05
  include_token_counts: true
security:
  token_rotation_seconds: 1800
  kms_key_id: projects/redwood/keys/edge-shim
---

Edge->Redwood auth flow (sketch):
- shim bootstraps with a long-lived bootstrap token (manual install step) OR uses customer KMS to sign requests.
- shim requests short-lived signer token from the control plane (using mTLS) and caches it for token_rotation_seconds.
- control plane enforces per-app quotas and scope (allowed models, rate limits).

Batching concerns: small window reduces latency but increases chance of partial ordering and retry complexity. For chat streaming we should keep batching off by default. Maybe enable batching only for non-streaming endpoints (embeddings, simple completion).

Failure modes to handle (notes):
- 401/403 returned mid-request -> should surface to user and trigger immediate token refresh.
- head-of-line blocking due to batching -> implement per-client per-route concurrency limits.
- inconsistent cache invalidation after rollout -> include model_version in cache keys.

Open questions / decisions:
- should the shim perform direct waveform post-processing (safety filtering) before returning to client? tradeoff: latency vs centralization.
- do we allow shim-local model checkpoints for offline inference (tiny on-device models)? scope creep.
- SDK API: async vs sync ergonomics; prefer async callbacks with optional promises.
- billing attribution: do we report token-level usage from shim or let Redwood deduplicate? need to guarantee no double-billing.

Short TODOs (next steps for me):
- [ ] sync with Maya on KMS flow design (ticket GH-issue-3142)
- [ ] prototype small shim in Go (single binary) with two endpoints: /infer and /metrics
- [ ] add tests for cache key collision (simulate version bump)
- [ ] outline SDK changes and a minimal migration guide for customers

References and tickets I keep thinking about:
- CONFLUENCE: EDGE-PROXY-PRD (link above)
- GH issues: gh-3142 (token rotation), gh-3321 (batching edge cases)
- Slack thread: #eng-serving-runtime "edge-shim-impl" (Dec 2025)

Notes to self (voicey):
- keep the shim as stateless as possible; state = pain when upgrading. Use local cache + controlled queue only.
- prioritize orthogonality: security/auth belongs in control plane; shim only gets rotated delegate tokens.
- keep streaming simple: proxy should stream raw tokens from Redwood to client when in streaming mode; do not buffer entire response.

Examples to flesh out later:
- sample SDK snippet (JS)
- end-to-end integration test harness (simulated network partitions)
- small perf test: cold-start vs warmed cache at 10 RPS

End of scratchpad.

(Most of the above is personal draft; file will be cleaned up before sharing with product.)

title: Audit logging gap analysis (WIP) — sensitive actions coverage inventory
preview:
LAST UPDATED: 2026-02-24 (Ethan)
STATUS: WIP inventory + owner proposals. Not yet GRC-approved evidence.

Purpose
- Working inventory of current audit event emission by service / endpoint / workflow.
- Identify gaps vs “sensitive actions” list for audit log schema v1 roll-out.
- Assign proposed owners and track dependencies (schema library, event_type registry, exporter normalization, CI lints, E2E coverage tests).

Quick notes / assumptions
- We are standardizing on audit schema v1 (schema_version: 1.0). Legacy events exist in a few places (not normalized, inconsistent actor semantics).
- “Coverage” means: for any sensitive action, at least one v1 event emitted with correct actor + target + tenancy + correlation IDs. (See PM-938 / ENG-4828 work.)
- Delivery semantics: best-effort at producer; pipeline aims at-least-once w/ buffering + retry; duplicates possible (customer guidance: de-dupe on event_id). (See exporter + SRE threads.)
- Multi-deploy parity: Hosted / Dedicated / Private should emit equivalent events. Private installer + private control plane are currently the biggest gaps.

Event taxonomy naming convention (draft)
- <domain>.<resource>.<action> (e.g., rbac.role.update, credential.api_key.rotate, policy.routing.update)
- Avoid embedding IDs in event_type. IDs go in *_id fields.

How to read this sheet
- “Current” = what’s in prod-ish codepaths today (as of late Feb), not necessarily v1.
- “v1-ready” means using shared schema library + registry + validator OR known to be in PR queue.
- “Gap type” codes:
  - G0 = no event emitted
  - G1 = event emitted but missing required fields (actor/source_ip/tenant/project/correlation)
  - G2 = wrong/inconsistent event_type naming
  - G3 = PII/secrets risk (payload too verbose)
  - G4 = Private parity missing
  - G5 = Exporter normalization issue / inconsistent outgoing fields

========================
SECTION A — Service inventory (table)
========================
Columns:
Service | Surface/workflow | Sensitive action | Current event(s) | v1-ready? | Gap type | Proposed owner | Dependencies/notes

1) Control Plane (tenant/config/policy/RBAC)
- control-plane | RBAC: create role | rbac.role.create | Legacy: "role_created" (missing actor.tenant_context sometimes) | In PR | G1/G2 | Benji Okafor | GitHub:redwood/pr-22005; needs request_id + trace_id propagation (PR-22047)
- control-plane | RBAC: update role perms | rbac.role.update | Emitted in some endpoints only | Partial | G0/G1 | Benji Okafor | Need diff summary (before/after) w/ cardinality constraints
- control-plane | RBAC: delete role | rbac.role.delete | Not consistent across internal/admin endpoints | No | G0 | Benji Okafor | Add to integration tests (PR-22058)
- control-plane | RBAC: assign role to user/service account | rbac.assignment.create | No event for bulk assignment | No | G0 | Ethan Park | Ensure “actor vs subject” semantics (admin edits someone else)
- control-plane | RBAC: remove role assignment | rbac.assignment.delete | Some events exist but missing subject identity | No | G1 | Ethan Park | Add target.* fields; avoid logging email if possible (use user_id)

- control-plane | API key: create | credential.api_key.create | PR adds event; currently logs key name + last4 only | Yes | - | Kaitlyn Nguyen | GitHub:redwood/pr-22012; confirm redaction rules (no secret material)
- control-plane | API key: rotate | credential.api_key.rotate | Not present in legacy | In PR | G0 | Kaitlyn Nguyen | Need idempotency + correlation with admin_action_id for UI action
- control-plane | API key: revoke | credential.api_key.revoke | Exists but inconsistent actor attribution for service-to-service revokes | Partial | G1 | Kaitlyn Nguyen | Ensure actor.type=service when automation triggers

- control-plane | SSO/SAML config update | auth.sso.update | Event missing entirely | No | G0 | Ethan Park | Naomi flagged as enterprise blocker; add in Phase 2
- control-plane | SCIM provisioning enable/disable | auth.scim.update | Not instrumented | No | G0 | Ethan Park | Confirm if in-scope for “sensitive actions” (likely yes)

2) API Gateway (admin endpoints)
- api-gateway | Quota override (tenant/project) | billing.quota.override | PR adds event; missing source_ip when called via internal job | Partial | G1 | Amara Diallo | GitHub:redwood/pr-22026; define source_ip rules for jobs (null + actor.service)
- api-gateway | Token issuance config change | auth.token_issuer.update | No event (today only config service logs) | No | G0 | Amara Diallo | Needs coordination w/ control-plane config write path
- api-gateway | Key validation policy update | auth.api_key_policy.update | Emits event but uses freeform “reason” string (high cardinality) | No | G1 | Amara Diallo | Replace reason w/ enum + separate “comment” (redacted/optional)

3) Console backend (admin actions)
- console-backend | Admin edits RBAC role | rbac.role.update | UI triggers control-plane endpoint; console does not emit distinct admin_action_id | No | G1 | Chloe Martin | Need admin_action_id + pass-through to control-plane (PR-22047 + ENG-4830)
- console-backend | Admin creates API key | credential.api_key.create | Covered via control-plane only | Partial | G1 | Chloe Martin | Ensure UI captures actor session + source_ip; avoid double-emitting duplicates (decide: emit once in control-plane)
- console-backend | Configure audit log export destination | audit.export_destination.update | Missing event entirely | No | G0 | Felix Schneider / Chloe Martin | Must be audited; ties to customer SIEM requirements; also add config validation + safe workflows (ENG-4837)
- console-backend | Update retention settings (logs/telemetry) | data.retention.update | Some events exist for telemetry retention, not for audit retention | No | G0/G4 | Chloe Martin | Align with data-residency work (ENG-4839; PR-22085)
- console-backend | Create/modify fallback policy | policy.routing.update | Covered if routed through policy-engine; console lacks correlation | Partial | G1 | Jordan Lee | Ensure diff summary, constrain cardinality (no raw JSON blobs)

4) Policy Engine / Routing
- policy-engine | Update routing policy (fallback rules) | policy.routing.update | PR exists w/ diff summary | Yes | - | Jordan Lee | GitHub:redwood/pr-22033; confirm event_type registry (PR-21991)
- policy-engine | Update allow-list/deny-list | policy.access_list.update | Not instrumented | No | G0 | Jordan Lee | Confirm taxonomy name; likely separate event type from routing.update
- policy-engine | Bind model to fleet pool / capacity tier | runtime.capacity_binding.update | Not fully decided if “sensitive” | TBD | TBD | Diego Santos | Slack eng-runtime thread suggests yes for enterprise; need PM decision

5) Private installer / upgrade tooling
- private-installer | Initial install | private.install.create | PR adds event but operator identity often missing | Partial | G1/G4 | Amina Yusuf | GitHub:redwood-private-installer/pr-611; clarify operator identity capture (kube user? email? “unknown”?)
- private-installer | Upgrade | private.upgrade.start / private.upgrade.complete | Partial instrumentation; doesn’t include target version consistently | Partial | G1 | Amina Yusuf | Add target.software_version, previous_version, outcome
- private-installer | Rollback | private.rollback.start / private.rollback.complete | Missing in older flows | Partial | G0/G4 | Amina Yusuf | Ensure parity w/ hosted change mgmt events
- private-installer | Apply config (incl. export endpoints, KMS) | private.config.apply | Often logged only in installer stdout (not audit pipeline) | No | G0/G4 | Amina Yusuf | Must route into audit pipeline in Private mode
- private-installer | Rotate credentials used by components | credential.rotation.execute | Not consistently emitted | No | G0/G4 | Amina Yusuf + Kaitlyn Nguyen | Also ensure secrets never logged (near-miss noted in Slack)

6) Audit log exporter
- audit-log-exporter | Normalization + schema_version tagging | audit.export.normalize | Exporter currently forwards legacy “flat” fields for some producers | In PR | G5 | Kevin Osei | GitHub:audit-log-exporter/pr-842; needs mapping doc (legacy->v1)
- audit-log-exporter | Splunk HEC target | audit.export.splunk.send | Implemented; customer saw inconsistent fields due to normalization gap | Partial | G5 | Kevin Osei | Jira SUP-18477; need outgoing payload validation against JSON schema
- audit-log-exporter | Retry/duplicates behavior | audit.export.retry | Customer complaint about duplicates | Known | G5 | Kevin Osei | Jira SUP-18510; guidance: de-dupe on event_id; ensure stable event_id on retry

7) Observability ingestion / telemetry
- observability-ingest | Audit pipeline drop/latency metrics | audit.pipeline.metric | Dashboards in observability-pack | Yes | - | Logan Wright / Sean Gallagher | observability-pack PR-433; ensure alerts link to runbook
- observability-ingest | Coverage dashboard “expected vs observed” | audit.coverage.report | Under development | No | G0 | Logan Wright | ENG-4828; needs “expected list” source of truth from taxonomy

========================
SECTION B — Highest-risk gaps (prioritized)
========================
P0 (blocks enterprise security reviews / SOC2 evidence)
1) Missing audit events for export destination config changes (Console + Private)
   - Gap: audit.export_destination.update not emitted anywhere reliably
   - Owner: Felix Schneider / Chloe Martin (Console), Amina Yusuf (Private)
   - Dep: Schema v1 + event_type registry; must include actor, tenant_id, project_id (if scoped), destination.type (enum), destination_id (not URL)

2) Private parity for RBAC changes (SUP-18421)
   - Gap: Private control-plane missing RBAC update/delete events, and/or not reaching exporter
   - Owner: Ethan Park + Amina Yusuf (coord)
   - Dep: Private routing of audit sink; test fixtures for private mode (ENG-4834)

3) Correlation IDs for investigations (admin_action_id)
   - Gap: UI action triggers multiple backend calls; hard to stitch without admin_action_id
   - Owner: Chloe Martin + Nadia Rahman
   - Dep: ENG-4830, PR-22047; ensure request_id + trace_id + admin_action_id in all events

P1 (important, but can ship behind flags if exception process is documented)
4) SSO/SAML + SCIM configuration changes (control plane)
   - Owner: Ethan Park
   - Notes: confirm “sensitive actions” list includes these (Naomi says yes for enterprise)

5) API gateway token issuance / key policy config changes
   - Owner: Amara Diallo
   - Notes: needs consistent actor attribution for internal automation

P2 (taxonomy/decision pending)
6) Runtime-level “sensitive” operations (model enable/disable, capacity binding)
   - Owner: Diego Santos + Jordan Lee
   - Notes: awaiting PM decision; if in-scope, define event types + required fields

========================
SECTION C — Owner map (proposed)
========================
- Control plane RBAC + core config writes: Ethan Park (lead), Benji Okafor (RBAC specifics)
- Credential lifecycle (API keys, rotations): Kaitlyn Nguyen
- API gateway admin endpoints: Amara Diallo
- Console admin flows + export config UX: Chloe Martin, Felix Schneider
- Policy engine/routing updates: Jordan Lee
- Private installer parity: Amina Yusuf
- Exporter normalization + SIEM targets: Kevin Osei
- Coverage dashboards + sampling: Logan Wright (+ Nadia Rahman for schema integration)
- Delivery SLO/alerts/runbooks: Sean Gallagher (+ Rafael Mendes)
- GRC mapping / evidence expectations: Olga Petrov

========================
SECTION D — Concrete next steps (2-week plan)
========================
Week 1
- [ ] Lock minimal “expected sensitive actions” list for Phase 2 instrumentation (Naomi + Marcus) (PM-938)
- [ ] Decide: single emission point per action (prefer producer-of-record emits; UI does not double-emit) (Marcus + Ethan + Chloe)
- [ ] Finalize correlation requirements: request_id + trace_id + admin_action_id + session_id (where applicable) (ENG-4830)
- [ ] Add audit.export_destination.update event type to registry + required fields (ENG-4822)

Week 2
- [ ] Implement export destination auditing in Console backend (ENG-4825/4837)
- [ ] Wire Private installer config apply -> audit sink (ENG-4826)
- [ ] Exporter validation: reject/flag outbound payloads failing schema v1 (ENG-4827)
- [ ] Add initial E2E tests: RBAC role update, API key revoke, export destination update (ENG-4834)

========================
SECTION E — Open questions / unresolved
========================
1) Actor identity in Private
- When operator runs installer from a bastion / workstation, what do we reliably capture?
  - Options discussed: kube apiserver user (if using kubectl), OS user, provided “operator_name” prompt, or “unknown”.
  - Need policy: do not capture personal emails by default; prefer opaque operator_id if SSO integrated; otherwise optional operator_label.

2) source_ip semantics
- For actions triggered by backend jobs (e.g., automation), do we set source_ip=null and actor.type=service?
- For console actions proxied through gateway, should source_ip be the real client IP (from XFF) or gateway IP? (enterprise expects real client when available)

3) Diff summaries
- For policy/routing changes and retention/residency changes: how to represent before/after without high cardinality or dumping raw JSON?
  - Proposal: diff_summary (short string, capped) + changed_fields (comma-separated list) + before_hash/after_hash (optional)

4) Dedupe + event_id
- Exporter retries can create duplicates in SIEM. We should standardize:
  - event_id generated at producer, stable across retries
  - customer guidance: de-dupe on event_id, not timestamp

========================
SECTION F — Notes from support/customer issues (for context)
========================
- SUP-18421: Private customer missing RBAC change events; likely because private control-plane path didn’t emit OR sink misconfigured; also highlights need for parity checklist + E2E tests.
- SUP-18477: SIEM export events not normalized; customer sees inconsistent field names across event types; exporter normalization PR in flight.
- SUP-18510: Duplicates due to retry semantics; we need to make event_id stable + document de-dupe.

Appendix: quick checklist for service owners (copy/paste)
- [ ] Use schema v1 library + validator
- [ ] Use registered event_type
- [ ] Populate: event_id, occurred_at, schema_version
- [ ] Populate: actor.*, target.*, resource.*, tenant_id (+ project_id when applicable)
- [ ] Populate correlation: request_id, trace_id, admin_action_id (if user-initiated admin workflow)
- [ ] Do NOT log secrets; check redaction rules
- [ ] Keep cardinality bounded (no raw payloads, no freeform reasons)
- [ ] Add unit test (shape) + E2E test (action -> event observed)

- Delivery semantics: best-effort at producer; pipeline aims at-least-once w/ buffering + retry; duplicates possible (customer guidance: de-dupe on event_id). (See exporter + SRE threads.)
- Multi-deploy parity: Hosted / Dedicated / Private should emit equivalent events. Private installer + private control plane are currently the biggest gaps.

Event taxonomy naming convention (draft)
- <domain>.<resource>.<action> (e.g., rbac.role.update, credential.api_key.rotate, policy.routing.update)
- Avoid embedding IDs in event_type. IDs go in *_id fields.

How to read this sheet
- “Current” = what’s in prod-ish codepaths today (as of late Feb), not necessarily v1.
- “v1-ready” means using shared schema library + registry + validator OR known to be in PR queue.
- “Gap type” codes:
  - G0 = no event emitted
  - G1 = event emitted but missing required fields (actor/source_ip/tenant/project/correlation)
  - G2 = wrong/inconsistent event_type naming
  - G3 = PII/secrets risk (payload too verbose)
  - G4 = Private parity missing
  - G5 = Exporter normalization issue / inconsistent outgoing fields

========================
SECTION A — Service inventory (table)
========================
Columns:
Service | Surface/workflow | Sensitive action | Current event(s) | v1-ready? | Gap type | Proposed owner | Dependencies/notes

1) Control Plane (tenant/config/policy/RBAC)

- control-plane | RBAC: delete role | rbac.role.delete | Not consistent across internal/admin endpoints | No | G0 | Benji Okafor | Add to integration tests (PR-22058)

- control-plane | RBAC: remove role assignment | rbac.assignment.delete | Some events exist but missing subject identity | No | G1 | Ethan Park | Add target.* fields; avoid logging email if possible (use user_id)

Week 2

1) Actor identity in Private
- When operator runs installer from a bastion / workstation, what do we reliably capture?

  - Need policy: do not capture personal emails by default; prefer opaque operator_id if SSO integrated; otherwise optional operator_label.

2) source_ip semantics
- For actions triggered by backend jobs (e.g., automation), do we set source_ip=null and actor.type=service?
- For console actions proxied through gateway, should source_ip be the real client IP (from XFF) or gateway IP? (enterprise expects real client when available)

3) Diff summaries
- For policy/routing changes and retention/residency changes: how to represent before/after without high cardinality or dumping raw JSON?

4) Dedupe + event_id
- Exporter retries can create duplicates in SIEM. We should standardize:
  - event_id generated at producer, stable across retries
  - customer guidance: de-dupe on event_id, not timestamp

SECTION F — Notes from support/customer issues (for context)

- SUP-18477: SIEM export events not normalized; customer sees inconsistent field names across event types; exporter normalization PR in flight.
- SUP-18510: Duplicates due to retry semantics; we need to make event_id stable + document de-dupe.

Appendix: quick checklist for service owners (copy/paste)
- [ ] Use schema v1 library + validator
- [ ] Use registered event_type
- [ ] Populate: event_id, occurred_at, schema_version
- [ ] Populate: actor.*, target.*, resource.*, tenant_id (+ project_id when applicable)
- [ ] Populate correlation: request_id, trace_id, admin_action_id (if user-initiated admin workflow)
- [ ] Do NOT log secrets; check redaction rules
- [ ] Keep cardinality bounded (no raw payloads, no freeform reasons)
- [ ] Add unit test (shape) + E2E test (action -> event observed)

title: Rollout controller + SLO monitoring integration (spike notes)
preview:
Context
- Spike goal: figure out where SLO evaluation belongs (controller vs separate service), draft an adapter interface to query SLO status for a rollout scope (route/model version/region/tier), and enumerate failure modes.
- Intended actions: pause rollout OR rollback (later) when breach confirmed, with guardrails to prevent flapping.
- Inputs from SRE: dual-window burn-rate, min sample size, staleness detection, and “don’t rollback during known incident” override.

Assumptions / terms (for this doc)
- “Rollout scope” = (service/route) + candidate model version + region + customer tier + rollout_id.
- Baseline = currently serving model version / control group.
- Candidate = new model version in canary or A/B.
- Monitor provider = slo-toolkit (burn-rate endpoint) + underlying metrics store.


Early architecture sketch (text)
Option A: Evaluation in rollout controller (preferred for MVP)
1) Rollout Controller state machine executes steps (shift traffic, wait, promote, etc).
2) At each “wait” / “verify” step, controller requests SLO status for candidate scope:
   - adapter.query(scope, signals, windows) -> status + metadata
3) Controller applies policy thresholds + debounce/holdoff + sample gates.
4) Controller decides action: continue | pause | rollback | suppress (if override).
5) Controller emits audit event + metrics for each evaluation tick.

Pros
- Minimal moving pieces; decision is co-located with rollout step transitions.
- Easier to reason about step idempotency: action and state transition in one place.

Cons
- Controller becomes more complex; needs caching/backoff and careful failure handling.
- Risk of controller load if we query too frequently per rollout.


Option B: Separate “SLO Evaluator” service (deferred)
- Controller registers rollout with evaluator; evaluator polls monitors and calls back with “breach confirmed”.
- Pros: isolates polling & caching; controller stays simple.
- Cons: distributed correctness; callback ordering; needs durable subscriptions + retries.

Decision (spike): Option A for MVP behind flag. Keep adapter boundary so we can lift polling out later if needed.


Integration points identified
1) Rollout controller state machine
- Hook point: the “verification” phase between traffic shifts (e.g., 1% -> 5% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%).
- Also for A/B: periodic evaluation while experiment runs.
- Needs: evaluation cadence + decision integration with existing “pause” mechanism.

2) Policy engine / config service
- Controller needs a resolved policy at evaluation time:
  - thresholds (latency, error)
  - windows (fast/slow)
  - min sample sizes
  - holdoff/debounce
  - action preference: pause vs rollback
  - suppression windows + admin override
- Note: policy must be versioned and included in audit trail.

3) Telemetry / metrics
- Emit metrics for (a) evaluation results and (b) decisions made.
- Add labels carefully (rollout_id is high cardinality; avoid in timeseries metrics; put rollout_id in logs/audit stream instead).

4) Audit event stream
- We need structured audit events for every action (pause/rollback/suppress) and a sampled set for evaluations (depending on noise).
- At minimum: on decision we include monitor ids, policy version, windows evaluated, and snapshot refs.


Monitor adapter interface draft
Goals
- Controller does not know about PromQL/Datadog/etc.
- Adapter returns normalized “burn rate” style evaluation + sample counts + timestamps.

Draft types (pseudocode-ish; not code)

Scope
- service: string (e.g. "redwood-inference")
- route: string (e.g. "/v1/chat/completions")
- model: string (model name)
- model_version: string (candidate)
- region: string
- customer_tier: string (self_serve | dedicated | private)
- rollout_id: string

Signal
- LATENCY_P95_MS
- LATENCY_P99_MS
- ERROR_RATE_5XX
- SATURATION (optional; maybe queue depth / GPU util proxy)

Windows
- fast: 5m
- slow: 30m

Query
adapter.get_slo_status(
  scope,
  signals=[...],
  windows={fast, slow},
  now=timestamp,
  include_baseline=true/false,
  comparison_mode=ABSOLUTE | RELATIVE_TO_BASELINE
)

Response
- status: OK | BREACH_FAST | BREACH_SLOW | BREACH_BOTH | INSUFFICIENT_DATA | STALE | MONITOR_MISSING | ERROR
- per_signal details:
  - burn_rate_fast, burn_rate_slow (float)
  - threshold_fast, threshold_slow (echoed if provider knows; otherwise controller owns thresholds)
  - sample_size_fast, sample_size_slow (requests)
  - last_data_timestamp
  - monitor_id / monitor_uri
  - notes (string)
- baseline (optional): same structure for baseline scope to support “relative” policies

Notes
- We likely want adapter to return raw numerators/denominators where possible (e.g., error_count, req_count) so controller can do min-sample gates itself.
- “STALE” semantics: last_data_timestamp older than X (SRE suggestion: >2x scrape interval OR >3m for fast window) OR provider explicitly says stale.


Policy resolution (controller-side) notes
- Policy engine should return a single “effective policy” for (service/route/model/region/tier) + rollout overrides.
- Safe bounds need to be enforced in policy layer (not just controller):
  - minimum windows (avoid 30s fast window)
  - max aggressiveness (avoid rollback on 1 request)
  - max evaluation frequency (avoid hammering monitor provider)

Open question: do we treat missing policy as disable-auto (safe) vs fallback to defaults?
- Tentative: if no explicit enablement, do not auto-rollback; allow auto-pause only for internal routes once defaults established.


Evaluation logic sketch (MVP)
Inputs
- adapter response per signal
- policy thresholds
- previous evaluation state (for debounce)

Steps
1) If admin suppression active OR “known incident override” active => return SUPPRESSED (no action) and emit audit w/ reason.
2) Validate data health:
   - if MONITOR_MISSING => (policy-dependent) either fail-open (no action) OR auto-pause rollout (safer) + alert "monitor missing".
   - if STALE => same treatment; do not rollback based on stale.
3) Sample gating:
   - require min_requests_fast and min_requests_slow (policy).
   - if insufficient => INSUFFICIENT_DATA, no action.
4) Breach decision per signal:
   - latency: compare burn rates OR percentile thresholds (TBD: burn-rate for latency is weird; might be simpler to use “latency SLO” from toolkit).
   - error: burn-rate more standard.
5) Rollup decision:
   - if any signal BREACH_BOTH => eligible for action.
   - else if BREACH_FAST only => wait for confirm (debounce).
6) Debounce / holdoff
   - require N consecutive evaluations over M minutes OR “fast + slow” confirmation.
   - introduce holdoff after any traffic shift before evaluating (avoid false positives during warmup / cache effects).
7) Action selection
   - prefer pause on first confirmed breach for early steps (1%/5%), rollback on later steps or if policy says “rollback always”.
   - for A/B: shift traffic back to baseline (i.e., reduce candidate split) before full rollback.


Integration points in controller code (where to hook)
- “Step transition” layer must be idempotent (we hit a race during spike where step advanced twice while evaluation was running).
- Proposed pattern:
  - Acquire rollout lock
  - Re-read rollout state + current step
  - If step has changed since evaluation started, discard evaluation result
  - Apply decision -> write state transition -> emit audit -> release lock


Edge cases discovered / noted during spike
Data/monitoring edge cases
1) Monitor missing for the exact rollout scope
- Common early on: service has global monitor but not labeled by model_version.
- Action: do not rollback. Optionally auto-pause + surface “monitor missing” in UI. Needs policy toggle.

2) Stale data / metric lag
- SLO toolkit can return last_data_timestamp; sometimes 2–5 min delay during telemetry hiccups.
- We should treat stale as “unknown” and avoid rollback. But still alert because it breaks safety rail.

3) Low traffic canaries
- Candidate at 1% traffic may not meet min sample size; will sit “INSUFFICIENT_DATA” indefinitely.
- Mitigation: policy can allow longer windows or require a min-duration before enabling evaluation.

4) False positives from warmup / cache effects
- First minutes after shifting traffic: cold KV cache, compilation warmup, GPU allocator jitter.
- Mitigation: holdoff after each shift (e.g., 3–5 min) before evaluating fast window.

5) Latency improvements (negative regressions)
- If candidate is faster, nothing to do. But some “relative to baseline” logic should not mis-trigger due to baseline degradation.

6) Baseline incident during rollout
- If baseline is already burning error budget, candidate comparisons get weird.
- Might need “absolute threshold” mode rather than relative.
- Or treat as suppressed due to incident.

7) Multi-region / partial outage
- Region A breaches; region B fine. Rollout might be region-scoped or global.
- MVP assumption: rollouts are region-scoped. If global, policy must specify whether to rollback globally on one region breach.

8) Multi-tenant tiers
- Dedicated customers may have different SLOs; tier label must be included in scope.
- Risk: if tier label missing in metrics, we accidentally use blended data.

9) Flapping due to traffic shifts
- Rollout action changes traffic distribution, which changes metrics; can induce oscillation.
- Debounce + minimum time between actions (cooldown) required.

10) Partial errors: some 5xxs expected
- Some customers send invalid requests; must ensure error rate is “server 5xx” not “all errors”.
- Also watch for upstream deps returning 429/503 during saturation.

11) Monitor provider errors/timeouts
- If adapter call times out, controller should not block state machine indefinitely.
- Use short timeout + retry w/ backoff; treat as “unknown” and continue waiting OR pause (policy).

Controller/action edge cases
12) Rollback idempotency
- Rollback executor must be safe if called multiple times (network retry, controller restart).

13) Double-execution due to leader failover
- If controller is HA, leader election changes can cause duplicated evaluation ticks.
- Need “decision dedupe” based on (rollout_id, step_id, decision_type, time_bucket).

14) Concurrent manual actions
- Human pauses/resumes while auto evaluator running.
- Must respect manual pause and not auto-resume.

15) Step transitions vs evaluation cadence
- If evaluation runs every 30s and step changes every 1–2m, we’ll often evaluate mid-transition.
- Need a “stable step” marker or only evaluate when step is “waiting”.

16) Audit logging size
- If we log every evaluation tick with full metric snapshots, event stream blows up.
- Suggest: emit audit only on decisions + on “missing/stale monitor” state changes; keep detailed evaluation traces in debug logs (sampled).

Security/privacy edge cases
17) Customer identifiers
- Audit events must avoid raw customer ids; use tier only, maybe hashed tenant id if truly needed.
- Console should show tier/region/route/model version; do not leak cross-tenant data.


Open questions
- Latency SLO representation: do we use burn-rate for latency SLOs (SRE prefers error budget style) or direct percentile threshold comparison?
- Failure mode for monitor missing/stale: fail-open (no action) vs fail-closed (pause rollout). Current leaning:
  - Hosted/self-serve: fail-open + alert
  - Dedicated/private (strict SLAs): fail-closed (pause) might be acceptable if rollout safety is required
- How to define “known incident override”? Source of truth (incident bot? status page? manual flag in policy engine?)
- Baseline comparison: is MVP absolute thresholds only? Relative-to-baseline can be added later.
- Cardinality: can we attach rollout_id to metrics as an exemplar/log-only and keep dashboards workable?


Next steps (captured during spike)
- Implement controller hook that calls evaluator only when step state is WAITING (Jada).
- Implement adapter with caching + staleness checks (Logan/Aisha).
- Policy schema draft with safe bounds + templates (Benji/Jordan + Paula for UX).
- Add decision audit schema proposal (Felix + Kevin for security review).
- Simulate edge cases in staging (Leila + Sahana): low volume canary, stale monitor, monitor missing.

Notes to self
- Keep adapter interface small and testable; we’re going to rewrite it once we learn how slo-toolkit endpoint behaves under high concurrency.
- Make sure “pause” semantics are reversible and don’t auto-resume.
- Don’t ship rollback-before-pause without rock solid idempotency + locking (race found in spike was scary).

Monitor adapter interface draft
Goals
- Controller does not know about PromQL/Datadog/etc.
- Adapter returns normalized “burn rate” style evaluation + sample counts + timestamps.

Draft types (pseudocode-ish; not code)

Scope
- service: string (e.g. "redwood-inference")
- route: string (e.g. "/v1/chat/completions")
- model: string (model name)
- model_version: string (candidate)
- region: string
- customer_tier: string (self_serve | dedicated | private)
- rollout_id: string

Signal
- LATENCY_P95_MS
- LATENCY_P99_MS
- ERROR_RATE_5XX
- SATURATION (optional; maybe queue depth / GPU util proxy)

Windows
- fast: 5m
- slow: 30m

Query
adapter.get_slo_status(
  scope,
  signals=[...],
  windows={fast, slow},
  now=timestamp,
  include_baseline=true/false,
  comparison_mode=ABSOLUTE | RELATIVE_TO_BASELINE
)

Response
- status: OK | BREACH_FAST | BREACH_SLOW | BREACH_BOTH | INSUFFICIENT_DATA | STALE | MONITOR_MISSING | ERROR
- per_signal details:
  - burn_rate_fast, burn_rate_slow (float)
  - threshold_fast, threshold_slow (echoed if provider knows; otherwise controller owns thresholds)
  - sample_size_fast, sample_size_slow (requests)
  - last_data_timestamp
  - monitor_id / monitor_uri
  - notes (string)
- baseline (optional): same structure for baseline scope to support “relative” policies

Notes
- We likely want adapter to return raw numerators/denominators where possible (e.g., error_count, req_count) so controller can do min-sample gates itself.
- “STALE” semantics: last_data_timestamp older than X (SRE suggestion: >2x scrape interval OR >3m for fast window) OR provider explicitly says stale.


Policy resolution (controller-side) notes
- Policy engine should return a single “effective policy” for (service/route/model/region/tier) + rollout overrides.
- Safe bounds need to be enforced in policy layer (not just controller):
  - minimum windows (avoid 30s fast window)
  - max aggressiveness (avoid rollback on 1 request)
  - max evaluation frequency (avoid hammering monitor provider)

Open question: do we treat missing policy as disable-auto (safe) vs fallback to defaults?
- Tentative: if no explicit enablement, do not auto-rollback; allow auto-pause only for internal routes once defaults established.


Evaluation logic sketch (MVP)
Inputs
- adapter response per signal
- policy thresholds
- previous evaluation state (for debounce)

Steps
1) If admin suppression active OR “known incident override” active => return SUPPRESSED (no action) and emit audit w/ reason.
2) Validate data health:
   - if MONITOR_MISSING => (policy-dependent) either fail-open (no action) OR auto-pause rollout (safer) + alert "monitor missing".
   - if STALE => same treatment; do not rollback based on stale.
3) Sample gating:
   - require min_requests_fast and min_requests_slow (policy).
   - if insufficient => INSUFFICIENT_DATA, no action.
4) Breach decision per signal:
   - latency: compare burn rates OR percentile thresholds (TBD: burn-rate for latency is weird; might be simpler to use “latency SLO” from toolkit).
   - error: burn-rate more standard.
5) Rollup decision:
   - if any signal BREACH_BOTH => eligible for action.
   - else if BREACH_FAST only => wait for confirm (debounce).
6) Debounce / holdoff
   - require N consecutive evaluations over M minutes OR “fast + slow” confirmation.
   - introduce holdoff after any traffic shift before evaluating (avoid false positives during warmup / cache effects).
7) Action selection
   - prefer pause on first confirmed breach for early steps (1%/5%), rollback on later steps or if policy says “rollback always”.
   - for A/B: shift traffic back to baseline (i.e., reduce candidate split) before full rollback.


Integration points in controller code (where to hook)
- “Step transition” layer must be idempotent (we hit a race during spike where step advanced twice while evaluation was running).
- Proposed pattern:
  - Acquire rollout lock
  - Re-read rollout state + current step
  - If step has changed since evaluation started, discard evaluation result
  - Apply decision -> write state transition -> emit audit -> release lock


Edge cases discovered / noted during spike
Data/monitoring edge cases
1) Monitor missing for the exact rollout scope
- Common early on: service has global monitor but not labeled by model_version.
- Action: do not rollback. Optionally auto-pause + surface “monitor missing” in UI. Needs policy toggle.

2) Stale data / metric lag
- SLO toolkit can return last_data_timestamp; sometimes 2–5 min delay during telemetry hiccups.
- We should treat stale as “unknown” and avoid rollback. But still alert because it breaks safety rail.

3) Low traffic canaries
- Candidate at 1% traffic may not meet min sample size; will sit “INSUFFICIENT_DATA” indefinitely.
- Mitigation: policy can allow longer windows or require a min-duration before enabling evaluation.

4) False positives from warmup / cache effects
- First minutes after shifting traffic: cold KV cache, compilation warmup, GPU allocator jitter.
- Mitigation: holdoff after each shift (e.g., 3–5 min) before evaluating fast window.

5) Latency improvements (negative regressions)
- If candidate is faster, nothing to do. But some “relative to baseline” logic should not mis-trigger due to baseline degradation.

6) Baseline incident during rollout
- If baseline is already burning error budget, candidate comparisons get weird.
- Might need “absolute threshold” mode rather than relative.
- Or treat as suppressed due to incident.

7) Multi-region / partial outage
- Region A breaches; region B fine. Rollout might be region-scoped or global.
- MVP assumption: rollouts are region-scoped. If global, policy must specify whether to rollback globally on one region breach.

8) Multi-tenant tiers
- Dedicated customers may have different SLOs; tier label must be included in scope.
- Risk: if tier label missing in metrics, we accidentally use blended data.

9) Flapping due to traffic shifts
- Rollout action changes traffic distribution, which changes metrics; can induce oscillation.
- Debounce + minimum time between actions (cooldown) required.

10) Partial errors: some 5xxs expected
- Some customers send invalid requests; must ensure error rate is “server 5xx” not “all errors”.
- Also watch for upstream deps returning 429/503 during saturation.

11) Monitor provider errors/timeouts
- If adapter call times out, controller should not block state machine indefinitely.
- Use short timeout + retry w/ backoff; treat as “unknown” and continue waiting OR pause (policy).

Controller/action edge cases
12) Rollback idempotency
- Rollback executor must be safe if called multiple times (network retry, controller restart).

13) Double-execution due to leader failover
- If controller is HA, leader election changes can cause duplicated evaluation ticks.
- Need “decision dedupe” based on (rollout_id, step_id, decision_type, time_bucket).

14) Concurrent manual actions
- Human pauses/resumes while auto evaluator running.
- Must respect manual pause and not auto-resume.

15) Step transitions vs evaluation cadence
- If evaluation runs every 30s and step changes every 1–2m, we’ll often evaluate mid-transition.
- Need a “stable step” marker or only evaluate when step is “waiting”.

16) Audit logging size
- If we log every evaluation tick with full metric snapshots, event stream blows up.
- Suggest: emit audit only on decisions + on “missing/stale monitor” state changes; keep detailed evaluation traces in debug logs (sampled).

Security/privacy edge cases
17) Customer identifiers
- Audit events must avoid raw customer ids; use tier only, maybe hashed tenant id if truly needed.
- Console should show tier/region/route/model version; do not leak cross-tenant data.


Open questions
- Latency SLO representation: do we use burn-rate for latency SLOs (SRE prefers error budget style) or direct percentile threshold comparison?
- Failure mode for monitor missing/stale: fail-open (no action) vs fail-closed (pause rollout). Current leaning:
  - Hosted/self-serve: fail-open + alert
  - Dedicated/private (strict SLAs): fail-closed (pause) might be acceptable if rollout safety is required
- How to define “known incident override”? Source of truth (incident bot? status page? manual flag in policy engine?)
- Baseline comparison: is MVP absolute thresholds only? Relative-to-baseline can be added later.
- Cardinality: can we attach rollout_id to metrics as an exemplar/log-only and keep dashboards workable?


Next steps (captured during spike)
- Implement controller hook that calls evaluator only when step state is WAITING (Jada).
- Implement adapter with caching + staleness checks (Logan/Aisha).
- Policy schema draft with safe bounds + templates (Benji/Jordan + Paula for UX).
- Add decision audit schema proposal (Felix + Kevin for security review).
- Simulate edge cases in staging (Leila + Sahana): low volume canary, stale monitor, monitor missing.

Notes to self
- Keep adapter interface small and testable; we’re going to rewrite it once we learn how slo-toolkit endpoint behaves under high concurrency.
- Make sure “pause” semantics are reversible and don’t auto-resume.
- Don’t ship rollback-before-pause without rock solid idempotency + locking (race found in spike was scary).

title: Tier config API proposal notes (batching SLO tiers)
preview:
Context
- Project: workload-aware batching defaults with 3 latency SLO tiers: fast / standard / cost-efficient.
- Platform question: how do customers (and internal routing/policy) select a tier, and how does it interact with existing config knobs and Dedicated overrides.
- This doc is a working set of notes for API surface + policy evaluation order. Not a final spec.

Goals for the API/config surface
- Simple for Hosted API: customers can choose a tier without learning scheduler knobs.
- Safe default: if customer does nothing, we pick a tier (likely “standard”) and can change the underlying knob set over time.
- Clear override precedence: avoid “why didn’t my setting take effect?” tickets.
- Backwards compatible: existing customers with manual batching configs should behave the same until they opt into tier defaults (or we migrate with a flag + explicit comms).
- Works across deployment modes:
  - Hosted API (self-serve)
  - Dedicated (per-tenant and/or per-cluster overrides; stricter change-control)
  - Private (customer-controlled control plane; may pin versions)

Non-goals (for initial release)
- Real-time per-tenant auto-tuning.
- Exposing all scheduler internals publicly.

Terminology (proposed)
- “Tier” (customer-facing): fast | standard | cost-efficient.
- “Profile” (internal): concrete scheduler knob set + guardrails; may be model-family + hardware-class specific.
- “Effective tier” (what runtime ran with): after applying overrides / constraints / overload mode.

API surface options

Option A: Request-level tier selection (header or request field)
- Example (REST):
  - Header: x-redwood-batching-tier: fast
  - Or body: "batching_tier": "fast" (where supported)
- Pros:
  - Flexible for multi-tenant apps (e.g., same route serves realtime chat + async summarization).
  - Allows A/B experimentation by caller without route config changes.
  - Fast path for support: “try fast tier for this endpoint” can be done client-side.
- Cons / concerns:
  - High cardinality if not constrained (metrics, logs). Needs normalization.
  - Risk of per-request tier thrash affecting batching stability (scheduler sees mixed tiers interleaved).
  - Potential for abuse: customers might always set “fast” and then complain about cost/throughput.
  - Harder to reason about SLO commitments if tier is caller-controlled.
- Guardrails if we do this:
  - Only allow request-level tier if route policy allows it (allowlist).
  - Enforce “max_tier” per API key / project (e.g., cannot exceed standard unless explicitly enabled).
  - Rate limit or reject invalid values early (SDK validation + server-side schema validation).

Option B: Route-level tier selection (policy/config)
- Example:
  - Route config: /v1/chat -> batching_tier: fast
  - Model policy: model=llama-3.1-70b -> default_tier: standard
- Pros:
  - Predictable; easy to explain and to publish in docs.
  - Stable scheduler behavior; better for caching/batching efficiency.
  - Easier to set SLO expectations (“this route runs as fast tier”).
- Cons:
  - Less flexible for mixed workloads on same route.
  - Customers with multi-mode traffic must split routes or use request metadata for routing.

Option C: Both, with precedence + constraints (current leaning)
- Route defines default tier; request may optionally override within bounds.
- Dedicated can clamp/force tier at tenant/cluster level.

Where should the tier live?

1) Control plane config (route / model policy)
- Stored with other policy artifacts (routing, fallback, quotas).
- Benefits: uniform across Hosted/Dedicated; works with Console.
- Needs: migration story for existing route configs.

2) Runtime-only knob (legacy batching config)
- Existing “manual batching” knobs are runtime config; tiers should not require customers to understand these.
- Proposed: tiers map to internal profiles; manual knobs remain but become “advanced/legacy.”

Backwards compatibility & migration plan

Current state (simplified)
- Many routes have implicitly default batching behavior (runtime defaults).
- Some Dedicated tenants have explicit knob overrides (max_batch_tokens, max_wait_ms, etc.).
- Some customers set request-level parameters for streaming behavior but not batching.

Key decision: how to interpret existing manual batching knobs when tier is introduced
- Proposed behavior:
  - If any explicit manual batching knob is set, treat as “manual mode” and do not apply tier defaults (unless customer explicitly opts into tiers).
  - If no manual knobs are set, apply tier defaults.
- Rationale: avoids silent behavior changes.

Opt-in vs default
- Hosted API: start opt-in via feature flag and route setting in Console; later we can default new routes to “standard” tier.
- Dedicated: opt-in only initially; requires clear precedence and a rollback switch.

Policy evaluation order (draft)

We need a single, deterministic order that can be surfaced as “effective tier” in Console and in reason codes.

Proposed precedence (highest wins)
1) Safety/compat constraints (runtime-enforced)
   - Model/hardware constraints may disallow certain profiles (e.g., quantization compatibility, max batch tokens ceiling).
   - Overload mode may override tier-specific targets to preserve system health (e.g., enable backpressure, clamp wait).
   - Note: these should not silently change tier label; instead, we can keep tier label but emit “constrained_profile” reason code, OR set effective tier to “standard” if we truly downgrade. Needs decision.

2) Dedicated tenant/cluster forced setting (control plane)
   - Example: tenant forces “standard” for all routes; request cannot override.
   - Primary use case: enforce cost controls or meet contracted SLO behavior.

3) Route-level explicit tier (control plane)
   - Set in Console or config API.

4) Request-level override (if allowed)
   - Only permitted if route policy allows request override.
   - Must be validated (enum) and potentially clamped (cannot exceed route’s allowed_max_tier).

5) Default tier (system)
   - If nothing specified, default = standard (current assumption; PM to confirm).

6) Manual batching knobs (legacy)
   - Conflict: manual knobs are not a “tier.”
   - Suggestion: manual knobs take precedence over tiers only if present; but that’s effectively an alternate branch rather than a step.
   - Revised structure:
     - Determine “mode” first: manual vs tier.
     - If manual knobs present and not explicitly overridden, run manual mode.
     - Else run tier mode with precedence above.

Reason codes / observability requirements (so support can explain behavior)
- Need a compact set of reason codes emitted by runtime and shown in Console.
- Draft codes:
  - tier_default_standard (no config)
  - tier_route_config
  - tier_request_override
  - tier_dedicated_forced
  - tier_clamped_by_policy (requested > allowed)
  - profile_constrained_by_model (compat constraint)
  - profile_constrained_by_hardware
  - overload_mode_backpressure
  - manual_batching_config_detected
  - feature_flag_disabled_fallback
- Note: keep cardinality bounded; values must be small enums.

HTTP/SDK specifics (Hosted API)

Header vs body field
- Header is easiest to add without breaking existing request schemas and SDK type generation.
- Body field is more discoverable in API docs (OpenAPI), but it touches more surfaces (chat/completions/embeddings).
- Proposed compromise:
  - Start with header for experimental/preview.
  - Add first-class SDK parameter later that sets the header.

Naming
- “batching_tier” is explicit but long.
- “slo_tier” is ambiguous (customers will ask if it affects other things).
- Proposed external name: “latency_tier” (but PM worries: implies guaranteed latency).
- Current leaning: keep “batching_tier” externally to scope expectations.

Validation behavior
- Invalid tier value -> 400 with message listing allowed values.
- If request override not allowed -> ignore vs error?
  - Strong preference: do NOT silently ignore. Return 400 unless we’re worried about breaking traffic.
  - Potential staged approach:
    - Phase 1: ignore with warning header + metric (to avoid breaking existing clients during preview)
    - Phase 2: enforce 400.

Policy API / config schema notes
- Route schema additions:
  - batching_tier_default: fast|standard|cost_efficient
  - batching_tier_allow_request_override: true|false
  - batching_tier_max_allowed: fast|standard|cost_efficient (optional)
- Dedicated overrides schema additions:
  - tenant_forced_batching_tier (optional)
  - tenant_max_batching_tier (optional)
- Need to ensure the schema composes with existing “model routing policy” objects (precedence rules must be explicit).

Console UX implications (brief)
- Must show:
  - Configured tier (route)
  - Whether request override is allowed
  - Dedicated forced tier (if applicable)
  - Effective tier observed (from telemetry) + reason code breakdown
- Avoid implying “fast tier = SLA.” Use wording like “optimized for lower tail latency; may reduce throughput.”

Edge cases / risks

Mixed-tier batching interaction
- If request-level override exists, scheduler may see interleaved “fast” and “cost-efficient” for same model pool.
- Potential mitigation:
  - Separate queues per tier within a model replica set.
  - Or keep single queue but apply tier-aware admission/wait budgets per request.
- Need runtime input from Sofia/Lena on how expensive it is to maintain tier-separated queues.

Streaming vs non-streaming
- Streaming endpoints are more sensitive to time-to-first-token.
- Is tier selection even allowed on embeddings/rerank? (probably yes, but value is mostly irrelevant).
- Proposed: allow parameter across all generation endpoints; runtime may ignore for non-generation routes but still label metrics consistently.

Security / compliance
- Tier selection should not affect logging/retention/data handling.
- However, request header values may appear in edge logs; ensure no customer PII is included (tier is enum, safe).
- Confirm with Security review: header name allowed through existing allowlists.

Telemetry cost/cardinality
- Must not allow arbitrary tier strings.
- Tag set should be bounded: tier (3 values), effective_tier (3), reason_code (small enum), model_family (bounded), region (bounded).

Open questions
1) Default tier choice: standard vs cost-efficient for some routes? (PM: likely standard as safe, but Optimize wants cost-efficient recommendations surfaced.)
2) Should we allow request-level override in GA? If yes, do we require explicit route opt-in?
3) When a profile is constrained (compat), do we change effective tier label or keep tier label and annotate reason_code?
4) How do we represent “manual mode” in Console and telemetry? (e.g., effective_tier=manual)
5) For Dedicated: do we need both tenant_forced_tier and tenant_max_tier, or just one?

Action items
- Ethan: draft schema changes for route policy + dedicated overrides and circulate to Priya/Jordan (by 2026-01-24).
- Jordan: confirm where in policy engine we evaluate tier vs model selection; propose implementation order + tests (ties into ENG-18422).
- Benji: ensure feature flag + kill switch can disable request-level override separately from route-level tier (ties into ENG-18431).
- Henry/Nikhil: decide on error vs ignore semantics for disallowed request overrides during preview.
- Sofia/Lena: feedback on feasibility of tier-separated queues vs per-request wait budget only.

Notes from quick sync (2026-02-18)
- Priya: strong preference for deterministic precedence and for always emitting reason codes; support load is the biggest risk.
- Lauren: Dedicated needs a tenant-level clamp at minimum; “forced standard” is a common request for cost predictability.
- Marissa: rollout UX needs to show what will happen before applying a tier change (preview of expected cost/latency deltas is later; for now show warnings and links to benchmark methodology).

Provisional recommendation (for ADR input)
- Ship route-level tier as the primary selection mechanism.
- Allow request-level override only when route explicitly enables it, and clamp to a maximum tier.
- Treat presence of manual batching knobs as manual mode that supersedes tiers unless the customer explicitly clears/opts out.
- Implement and surface a strict precedence order (Dedicated forced > route > request > default), with runtime constraints applied last but always explained via reason codes.

Edge cases / risks

Mixed-tier batching interaction
- If request-level override exists, scheduler may see interleaved “fast” and “cost-efficient” for same model pool.
- Potential mitigation:
  - Separate queues per tier within a model replica set.
  - Or keep single queue but apply tier-aware admission/wait budgets per request.
- Need runtime input from Sofia/Lena on how expensive it is to maintain tier-separated queues.

Streaming vs non-streaming
- Streaming endpoints are more sensitive to time-to-first-token.
- Is tier selection even allowed on embeddings/rerank? (probably yes, but value is mostly irrelevant).
- Proposed: allow parameter across all generation endpoints; runtime may ignore for non-generation routes but still label metrics consistently.

Security / compliance
- Tier selection should not affect logging/retention/data handling.
- However, request header values may appear in edge logs; ensure no customer PII is included (tier is enum, safe).
- Confirm with Security review: header name allowed through existing allowlists.

Telemetry cost/cardinality
- Must not allow arbitrary tier strings.
- Tag set should be bounded: tier (3 values), effective_tier (3), reason_code (small enum), model_family (bounded), region (bounded).

Open questions
1) Default tier choice: standard vs cost-efficient for some routes? (PM: likely standard as safe, but Optimize wants cost-efficient recommendations surfaced.)
2) Should we allow request-level override in GA? If yes, do we require explicit route opt-in?
3) When a profile is constrained (compat), do we change effective tier label or keep tier label and annotate reason_code?
4) How do we represent “manual mode” in Console and telemetry? (e.g., effective_tier=manual)
5) For Dedicated: do we need both tenant_forced_tier and tenant_max_tier, or just one?

Action items
- Ethan: draft schema changes for route policy + dedicated overrides and circulate to Priya/Jordan (by 2026-01-24).
- Jordan: confirm where in policy engine we evaluate tier vs model selection; propose implementation order + tests (ties into ENG-18422).
- Benji: ensure feature flag + kill switch can disable request-level override separately from route-level tier (ties into ENG-18431).
- Henry/Nikhil: decide on error vs ignore semantics for disallowed request overrides during preview.
- Sofia/Lena: feedback on feasibility of tier-separated queues vs per-request wait budget only.

Notes from quick sync (2026-02-18)
- Priya: strong preference for deterministic precedence and for always emitting reason codes; support load is the biggest risk.
- Lauren: Dedicated needs a tenant-level clamp at minimum; “forced standard” is a common request for cost predictability.
- Marissa: rollout UX needs to show what will happen before applying a tier change (preview of expected cost/latency deltas is later; for now show warnings and links to benchmark methodology).

Provisional recommendation (for ADR input)
- Ship route-level tier as the primary selection mechanism.
- Allow request-level override only when route explicitly enables it, and clamp to a maximum tier.
- Treat presence of manual batching knobs as manual mode that supersedes tiers unless the customer explicitly clears/opts out.
- Implement and surface a strict precedence order (Dedicated forced > route > request > default), with runtime constraints applied last but always explained via reason codes.

title: Audit Log Telemetry Snapshot — Customer Review Guide
preview:
Purpose:
This snapshot is a concise customer-facing reference describing the shape, retention, and export options for Redwood Inference audit telemetry. It is intended for security reviewers and customers completing security questionnaires who want a single-page technical summary of: what events we capture, which fields are available, how RBAC v2 decisions are recorded, and how telemetry can be exported to customer SIEMs or used with our evidence automation tooling.

High-level summary:
- What we record: authentication events, authorization checks (RBAC v2), model invocation metadata (model id, version, runtime variant), request/response metadata (status, latency, token counts), quota and rate-limit hits, deployment lifecycle events (rollouts, instance restarts), and system-level alerts (OOM, GPU faults). We do NOT log customer prompt text by default in hosted deployments; request/response payload logging is opt-in and covered by contractual, encryption, and retention controls.
- Formats: logs are emitted as structured JSON and can be exported in either JSON lines or Common Event Format (CEF) depending on customer SIEM requirements.
- Retention & residency: default retention for audit logs is 90 days in hosted accounts; customizable for Dedicated/Private deployments. Logs for Private deployments remain in-customer network by default. All logs in transit are TLS-encrypted; at-rest storage uses AES-256 with KMS-managed keys.

Core audit log schema (representative fields):
- timestamp: ISO-8601 UTC
- tenant_id: obfuscated tenant GUID (or raw id for Private per contract)
- request_id: global request UUID
- actor: {type, id, source_ip}  <-- flattened into actor_type, actor_id, actor_ip in our JSON logs
- action: canonical action name (e.g., model.generate, model.embed, admin.update_policy)
- resource: resource_type/resource_id (e.g., model/model-7b-v2)
- rbac_decision: allow | deny | conditional
- rbac_policy_id: id of evaluated RBAC policy (if applicable)
- decision_explanation: short reason (e.g., role-mismatch, expired-key)
- http_status: numeric
- latency_ms: integer
- token_counts: {prompt: n, completion: n}  <-- flattened to prompt_tokens, completion_tokens
- bytes_in / bytes_out: integers
- outcome: success | failure | throttled
- host_instance: host id or pool id (for Dedicated visibility)
- export_marker: boolean (true when log was included in an evidence export)
- pii_hash: SHA-256 hash of any PII fields included (when enabled)

Note: In the actual exported JSON lines each key is a top-level field; the above outlines semantic names customers will see.

RBAC v2 specific telemetry:
- Every authorization evaluation emits a compact authorization event with: evaluated_policy_ids (comma list), matched_rule_id, context_tags (e.g., environment=prod, route=api/v1/generate), and pre/post attribute set for principals when attribute-based checks are used.
- Denies include an audit reason code and a linkable policy fingerprint that maps back to the policy bundle in our policy store (useful for evidence).
- Policy rollout actions (canary/promotion/rollback) generate deployment events that include targeted tenant lists and timestamped rollout windows.

SIEM integration patterns and examples:
- Push integrations: we support direct HTTPS event delivery to customer endpoints, Splunk HEC collector, and S3-compatible buckets for ingestion pipelines. Delivery is batched; batching size and interval are configurable per customer.
- Format choices:
  - JSON Lines (default): each log is a single JSON object per line.
  - CEF: we provide a CEF mapping layer that converts a selected subset of fields into standard CEF fields. CEF exports contain a mapping manifest.
- Example Splunk query (JSON ingestion):
  index=redwood_logs tenant_id=\"T-abc123\" rbac_decision=deny | stats count by decision_explanation, actor_id
- Example Elastic/KQL query:
  event.dataset: \"redwood.audit\" and rbac_decision.keyword: \"deny\" and host_instance: \"pool-eu-01\"

Evidence automation & exports:
- Triggered exports: customers can request ad-hoc exports for time ranges via the Console or via an authenticated API. Exports can be: JSONL bundle, CSV summary, or pre-packaged evidence ZIP (includes manifest, sample logs, and policy fingerprints).
- Scheduled SIEM syncs: for enterprise customers we can configure near-real-time forwarders to push telemetry into customer SIEMs with delivery guarantees and replay semantics for missed windows.
- Evidence manifest: every export includes a manifest.json (or manifest.txt) listing the export_query, time window, included fields, and KMS key_id used for encryption. For Private deployments the manifest points to local storage paths.

Sample audit log lines (JSONL - representative):
{"timestamp":"2026-02-18T21:03:12Z","tenant_id":"T-********","request_id":"req-9f3b","actor_type":"api_key","actor_id":"key-42","actor_ip":"18.205.3.12","action":"model.generate","resource":"model-7b-v2","rbac_decision":"allow","rbac_policy_id":"pol-211","http_status":200,"latency_ms":132,"prompt_tokens":24,"completion_tokens":58,"outcome":"success","host_instance":"pool-us-03","export_marker":false}",{"timestamp":"2026-02-19T03:17:45Z","tenant_id":"T-********","request_id":"req-afe2","actor_type":"user","actor_id":"user-998","actor_ip":"3.121.11.90","action":"admin.update_policy","resource":"policy/pol-211","rbac_decision":"deny","rbac_policy_id":"pol-211","decision_explanation":"role-mismatch","http_status":403,"latency_ms":12,"outcome":"failure","export_marker":true}",
Security controls & privacy knobs:
- Prompt redaction: by default, hosted accounts do not persist prompt text. If a customer enables request/response logging we provide field-level redaction and a PII hashing option.
- Encryption & key management: export bundles can be encrypted with customer-supplied KMS keys (Dedicated/Private) or Redwood-managed keys for hosted.
- Minimization & retention: customers can configure retention windows per log class (authz, admin, model-invocation) and request immediate deletion for compliance cases (subject to contractual SLAs).

FAQ for questionnaires (short answers customers can copy):
Q: Do you capture authorization decisions for access control?
A: Yes — every RBAC v2 evaluation emits an authorization event with policy id and decision reason. Logs contain sufficient artefacts to reproduce the authorization context for audits.

Q: Can we stream logs into our SIEM?
A: Yes — supported targets: Splunk HEC, HTTPS endpoints, S3-compatible buckets, and syslog (via forwarder). Delivery can be near-real-time with replay for missed windows.

Q: Is prompt text stored by default?
A: No — prompt text is not persisted by default for hosted customers. Customers may opt-in to payload logging under strict controls.

Q: How do you handle PII in logs?
A: PII is either redacted, hashed, or omitted depending on customer settings. When hashing is used we surface a deterministic SHA-256 hash field and do not store the cleartext.

Operational notes for reviewers (internal):
- Priority open items for completion before finalizing customer-facing version: include canonical field mapping doc to CEF schema, add example Splunk HEC configuration snippet, and finalize retention SLA language with legal.
- Known limitation: current CEF mapping omits token_counts (by design to reduce payload). We plan a v2 CEF mapping to include token summaries.

Next steps / contact:
- For customers requesting a tailored export or SIEM integration, contact the Security Integrations team: security-integrations@redwood.ai (request: SIEM sync, include tenant_id and preferred target).
- Document owner: Ethan Park. For product/infra questions loop in Priya Khatri (RBAC lead) and Liam O'Connell (telemetry infra).

Appendix: checklist for attaching to a security questionnaire response (copyable):
- We provide structured audit logs in JSON/CEF formats: yes
- Authorization (RBAC v2) decisions logged: yes (policy id + reason)
- Prompt text persisted by default: no
- Export options to customer SIEM: Splunk HEC, HTTPS, S3, syslog
- Retention configurable: yes (90d default)
- Exports encryptable with customer KMS: yes

(End of snapshot)

title: Throughput Repro Lab Journal and Experiment Matrix
preview:
Purpose and quick summary:
- Running a compact lab notebook that prioritizes reproducible throughput numbers while uncovering brittle failure modes (thermal throttling, KV cache pressure, batching edge cases).
- This doc is a running journal + compact experiment matrix to capture configurations that consistently reproduce behavior across infra (devbox -> staging -> dedicated pool).

Top-level hypothesis to test:
- Variability in peak throughput often correlates with: client-side pacing jitter, tokenization variance in sample corpus, and priming order for KV caches. If we attack all three systematically we can reduce run-to-run 95th percentile throughput variance to <4%.

Core definitions used in experiments:
- Steady-state window: 3 minute sliding window after warmup ends used for baseline metrics.
- Warmup phase: traffic pattern that ramps from 0 to target qps over a configurable duration (default 120s).
- Anchor run: a baseline run using canonical dataset, canonical seed, and no client jitter; used daily to detect drift.
- Seed hygiene: documented RNG seeds for all components (client, loadgen, tokenizer) included in the run metadata.

Experiment matrix (columns we vary):
- model_variant: fp16 | int8-llm | int8-llm-kv-optimized
- batch_strategy: fixed-batch(1..N) | auto-batching | occupancy-aware-batching
- client_pattern: poisson | bursty-microburst | steady-ramped | coalesced-burst
- tokenization_profile: short-mean(8) | mixed(8..64) | long-tail(64..512)
- rng_seed: explicit numeric value or "random"
- warmup_policy: gentle-ramp-120s | staircase-prime | seedful-kv-prefix-prime
- client_jitter_ms: 0 | 5 | 20 | 100

Notebook entries (experiments and notes):
- EXP-001 (2025-11-10): baseline anchor run. model_variant=fp16, batch_strategy=auto-batching, client_pattern=poisson, tokenization_profile=mixed, rng_seed=1337, warmup_policy=gentle-ramp-120s, client_jitter_ms=0. Observations: steady-state throughput 18.4k toks/s, run-to-run drift ~7% over three repeats. Tail latency occasionally spikes at 99.9th when concurrent requests hit KV flush boundary. Action: add KV-prefix priming in next run.

- EXP-007 (2025-11-25): added staircase priming (3 small priming requests with representative prompts). result: throughput median unchanged, run-to-run drift reduced to ~3.6%, 99.9th tail spikes reduced but not eliminated. Noted that priming must match tokenization_profile; mismatched priming (short primes for long-tail corpus) worsened variance.

- EXP-015 (2026-01-12): introduced occupancy-aware-batching on int8-llm-kv-optimized. client_pattern=bursty-microburst, warmup_policy=staircase-prime, client_jitter_ms=5. Result: peak sustained throughput increased by 12% vs auto-batching; tail latency improved on 95th but 99.9th still sensitive under backpressure. Hypothesis: kernel scheduling occasionally starves threads handling long-sequence requests. Need trace sampling.

- EXP-021 (2026-02-03): seed hygiene audit. Ran three anchor runs with explicit seeds for tokenizer and client. Observed deterministic artifacts in initial latency slope when seeds aligned; good for debugging but masks real-world variability. Recommendation: keep seeded runs for CI debugging and unseeded runs for production realism.

Warmup and priming playbook (practical checklist):
- Choose warmup_policy that mirrors expected production arrival patterns. For bursty usage, use staircase-prime to populate KV cache and CUDA contexts. For steady traffic, gentle ramp is enough.
- Always record the warmup end timestamp and exclude first X seconds (default 30s) from steady-state window to avoid transient tail effects.
- When using prefix priming, ensure priming prompts are tokenization-profile-matched to the target corpus. Document primers in run metadata.
- If using seedful warmup, store RNG seeds alongside artifacts (tokenizer seed, client seed, replay seed).

Loadgen configs and snippets (examples we used):
- poisson pattern: mean_qps=1200, sample function=expovariate(1/mean_qps), client_concurrency=80, request_timeout=10s
- bursty-microburst: bursts_per_minute=8, burst_size_mean=200, intra_burst_interarrival=1..5ms, core_sleep_between_bursts=10s
- ramped-steady: ramp_seconds=120, plateau_qps=900, jitter=uniform(0,client_jitter_ms)

Observability signals to capture for each run (must be included in automated artifacts):
- per-request latency histogram (track 50/90/95/99/99.9)
- tokenized length distribution and sequence-length occupancy heatmap
- GPU metrics: SM utilization, memory utilization, memory allocator churn, temperature, power draw
- host metrics: CPU steal, context-switches, interrupts, net tx/rx drops
- runtime internals: KV cache hits/misses, batch size distribution, kernel selection log per step
- loadgen-side: request timestamps, client-side queuing, retry counts, observed qps series
- OS-level traces (perf/ebpf) for suspect runs when tail spikes occur

Repeatability best practices (procedural):
- Lock infrastructure config: same driver version, CUDA/cuDNN, kernel args. Record AMI or image digest.
- Run at least 3 anchor runs separated by 30 minutes to check thermal drift. If drift >5% investigate cooling/clocking.
- Use deterministic workloads (seeded) for debugging and fuzzed/unseeded for production representativeness.
- Capture entire system snapshot: git commit for bench runner, docker image digests, loadgen args, RNG seeds, and observability export paths. Attach to artifact storage.
- Automate a metadata.json in artifact s3 for each run (see linked GH PR for schema).

Failure modes we encountered and quick mitigations:
- Symptom: throughput drop after ~10 minutes, GPU temp rises then throttles. Mitigation: schedule long-run experiments in cooled pool and run anchor before and after.
- Symptom: sudden 99.9th spikes correlated with long requests (>=256 tokens) causing batch fragmentation. Mitigation: enforce request bucketing by sequence-length or set max_batch_latency lower to ensure long requests do not block microbatches.
- Symptom: run-to-run difference tied to tokenizer library update. Mitigation: pin tokenizer package versions and include tokenization_profile artifacts in run logs.

Automation and reproducibility checklist for CI integration:
- Implement two nightly jobs:
  1) Development anchor: quick anchor run (10m) against fp16 model_variant to detect regressions.
  2) Canary long-run: 1hr run for int8-llm-kv-optimized in dedicated pool to validate long-term stability.
- Both jobs must publish run metadata and artifact links, and post a summary to #bench-results with tags.
- Add an approval gate that prevents rolling model variants to production if anchor drift >8% or 99.9th latency regresses by >25%.

Open questions and ideas to explore next:
- Can we build a small synthetic prefix corpus that provably exercises KV cache locality patterns? (Idea: generate clusters of prompts that share prefix tokens in different ratios.)
- Investigate runtime kernel telemetry: add a lightweight telemetry hook that records per-step kernel selection decisions with sample rate 0.1% to correlate with tail events.
- Explore client-side pacing controllers that adapt to instantaneous backend batch occupancy instead of fixed pacers.

Appendix: run metadata template (fields we store for each run)
- run_id, created_by, model_variant, image_digest, cpu_affinity, gpu_affinity, driver_version, cuda_version, bench_runner_commit, loadgen_commit, tokenizer_version, seed_map (client, tokenizer, replay), warmup_policy, client_pattern, batch_strategy, observed_qps_timeseries_path, steady_state_window, artifacts_bucket_path

Quick notes to self:
- Keep this doc as lab notebook, copy finalized procedures into the canonical playbook once we stabilize the anchor behavior.
- Share latest anchor artifact link in sprint demo for visibility.

End of journal entry.
