- Always record the warmup end timestamp and exclude first X seconds (default 30s) from steady-state window to avoid transient tail effects.
- When using prefix priming, ensure priming prompts are tokenization-profile-matched to the target corpus. Document primers in run metadata.
- If using seedful warmup, store RNG seeds alongside artifacts (tokenizer seed, client seed, replay seed).

Loadgen configs and snippets (examples we used):
- poisson pattern: mean_qps=1200, sample function=expovariate(1/mean_qps), client_concurrency=80, request_timeout=10s
- bursty-microburst: bursts_per_minute=8, burst_size_mean=200, intra_burst_interarrival=1..5ms, core_sleep_between_bursts=10s
- ramped-steady: ramp_seconds=120, plateau_qps=900, jitter=uniform(0,client_jitter_ms)

Observability signals to capture for each run (must be included in automated artifacts):
- per-request latency histogram (track 50/90/95/99/99.9)
- tokenized length distribution and sequence-length occupancy heatmap
- GPU metrics: SM utilization, memory utilization, memory allocator churn, temperature, power draw
- host metrics: CPU steal, context-switches, interrupts, net tx/rx drops
- runtime internals: KV cache hits/misses, batch size distribution, kernel selection log per step
- loadgen-side: request timestamps, client-side queuing, retry counts, observed qps series
- OS-level traces (perf/ebpf) for suspect runs when tail spikes occur

Repeatability best practices (procedural):
- Lock infrastructure config: same driver version, CUDA/cuDNN, kernel args. Record AMI or image digest.
- Run at least 3 anchor runs separated by 30 minutes to check thermal drift. If drift >5% investigate cooling/clocking.
- Use deterministic workloads (seeded) for debugging and fuzzed/unseeded for production representativeness.
- Capture entire system snapshot: git commit for bench runner, docker image digests, loadgen args, RNG seeds, and observability export paths. Attach to artifact storage.
- Automate a metadata.json in artifact s3 for each run (see linked GH PR for schema).

Failure modes we encountered and quick mitigations:
- Symptom: throughput drop after ~10 minutes, GPU temp rises then throttles. Mitigation: schedule long-run experiments in cooled pool and run anchor before and after.
- Symptom: sudden 99.9th spikes correlated with long requests (>=256 tokens) causing batch fragmentation. Mitigation: enforce request bucketing by sequence-length or set max_batch_latency lower to ensure long requests do not block microbatches.
- Symptom: run-to-run difference tied to tokenizer library update. Mitigation: pin tokenizer package versions and include tokenization_profile artifacts in run logs.

Automation and reproducibility checklist for CI integration:
- Implement two nightly jobs:
  1) Development anchor: quick anchor run (10m) against fp16 model_variant to detect regressions.
  2) Canary long-run: 1hr run for int8-llm-kv-optimized in dedicated pool to validate long-term stability.
- Both jobs must publish run metadata and artifact links, and post a summary to #bench-results with tags.
- Add an approval gate that prevents rolling model variants to production if anchor drift >8% or 99.9th latency regresses by >25%.

Open questions and ideas to explore next:
- Can we build a small synthetic prefix corpus that provably exercises KV cache locality patterns? (Idea: generate clusters of prompts that share prefix tokens in different ratios.)
- Investigate runtime kernel telemetry: add a lightweight telemetry hook that records per-step kernel selection decisions with sample rate 0.1% to correlate with tail events.
- Explore client-side pacing controllers that adapt to instantaneous backend batch occupancy instead of fixed pacers.

Appendix: run metadata template (fields we store for each run)
- run_id, created_by, model_variant, image_digest, cpu_affinity, gpu_affinity, driver_version, cuda_version, bench_runner_commit, loadgen_commit, tokenizer_version, seed_map (client, tokenizer, replay), warmup_policy, client_pattern, batch_strategy, observed_qps_timeseries_path, steady_state_window, artifacts_bucket_path

Quick notes to self:
- Keep this doc as lab notebook, copy finalized procedures into the canonical playbook once we stabilize the anchor behavior.
- Share latest anchor artifact link in sprint demo for visibility.

End of journal entry.

title: KV Lease Epochs and Priority Lanes — Throttling & Prefill/Decode Coordination (Draft)
preview:
Summary:
This note proposes an "epochal lease" model for KV residency combined with explicit priority lanes for streams to better coordinate overlapping prefill and decode work. The goal is to reduce tail latency regressions during mixed workloads (short chat vs long prefill-heavy requests) while minimizing throughput loss from conservative throttling.

Motivation / problem statement:
- Current prefilling + decode overlap sometimes causes long-running prefill work to evict hot KV entries or grab GPU kernels for extended periods, increasing tail latency for streaming decodes.
- Existing heuristics (simple TTLs, token-count quotas) are brittle as request mixes shift. We need a more adaptive mechanism that encodes intent (short interactive vs long batch) and gives the serving runtime levers to schedule microbatches, KV residency, and kernel affinity without global freezes.

High-level idea:
1) Lease epochs: allocate short-duration "leases" on KV residency per stream group. Each lease is an epoch id + expiry timestamp; renewals are explicit and cheap. Leases express the stream's desire to keep KV state resident for the next N tokens.
2) Priority lanes: map streams to lanes (interactive, background-prefill, burst-batch). Lanes carry QoS profiles: preferred kernel families, prefetch aggressiveness, renewal politeness.
3) Throttling & admission: implement per-lane soft budgets and a fast-path reclaim that yields to interactive lanes when contention spikes.

Lease model details:
- Lease token: 64-bit epoch id + lease_ttl_ms. Small per-stream metadata kept on CPU.
- Default TTLs: interactive=250ms, streaming-decode=500ms, prefill-heavy=2000ms (configurable). Leases are renewed on key events: new tokens buffered for decode, prefetch complete, or explicit keepalive from admission layer.
- Renewal policy: exponential backoff for noisy renewals to avoid thundering at scale. If a stream fails to renew, KV residency is demoted to "evictable" and will be reclaimed opportunistically.
- Lease hierarchy: per-slot leases (fast, cheap) + per-model-shard tokens (coarser). This lets a large request hold lightweight slot-level leases while not monopolizing shard-level memory.

Priority lanes and mapping:
- Lanes: interactive (SLO-critical small outputs), streaming (medium length, prefers steady emit), prefill (long context, heavy KV use), batch (throughput-optimized offline jobs).
- Mapping heuristics: request metadata (max_new_tokens, predicted prefix size), route config, and past behavior. Agents can override via rollout tags.
- Lane properties: kernel_affinity_hint, renewal_ttl, max_parallel_prefills, preemptibility_score.

Admission, throttling and reclaim strategy:
- Soft budgets: each lane has a soft concurrent-prefill budget and a token-emission window. When a lane exceeds soft budget, it enters degraded mode (slower prefetch, higher microbatch thresholds).
- Fast-path reclaim: on urgent pressure (interactive queue build-up), runtime signals prefill lanes to yield: immediate stop of non-critical prefetches, delayed KV writes, and accelerated lease expiration. This uses an in-memory priority bitmap and per-worker epoch checks (cheap).
- Graceful fallback: evicted prefill streams get a small cached summary of KV (partial prefix) and can either retry prefill opportunistically or fall back to a smaller context runtime. Application-level fallback policies are supported in route config.

Microbatch windows & overlap rules:
- Windowing: define short microbatch windows for interactive/streaming lanes (e.g., 10–30ms) and larger windows for prefill (50–200ms). Windows are adaptive based on observed token-service times and kernel run durations.
- Overlap rules: allow decode to proceed concurrently with prefill only if: (a) prefill's lease is not holding critical kernels (kernel_affinity_hint allows shared kernels), (b) KV residency chief indicates low eviction risk, and (c) global tail-latency signal is below threshold. Otherwise, prefill yields or pauses.
- Microbatch packing: prefer decode microbatches to be opportunistically batched across slots while maintaining lane ordering. Prefill microbatches may be split into smaller chunks in degraded mode.

Kernel affinity and lane-to-kernel mapping:
- Kernels will be classified by resource shape: low-latency small-window kernels vs long-lived heavy-token kernels (e.g., kv-heavy matmul sequences). Each lane receives an affinity score for kernel types.
- Reservation vs opportunistic: interactive lanes may reserve a small number of low-latency kernels per worker; prefill lanes use opportunistic kernels but with longer time slices. Reservation sizes are adaptive and controlled by autoscaler signals.
- Eviction policy: prefer to preempt opportunistic kernels before reserved ones; when preempting, ensure in-flight KV is checkpointed minimally to avoid decode stalls.

Fairness, staleness and correctness tradeoffs:
- Staleness window: when a KV shard becomes evictable, reads return slightly stale values for a configurable window to avoid consistency stalls. We accept bounded staleness for latency gains; critical routes can opt-out.
- Fairness heuristics: slot-level round-robin across lanes with weighted priorities; use decay factors for repeated renewals.
- Correctness: leases do not change model semantics; they only affect residency and scheduling. Applications that require strict KV consistency must mark routes as "pinned" to prevent eviction.

Telemetry and metrics to measure success:
- Per-lane P50/P95/P99 latency, prefill throughput, eviction rate, lease-renewal failure rate, kernel preemptions/sec.
- New composite metric: Effective Prefill Yield (EPY) = successful prefill token / attempts adjusted by latency impact on interactive lane P99.
- Dashboards: lane heatmaps, lease churn timelines, KV residency occupancy by lane. Alerts for lease_renewal_failures > 0.5% and interactive_p99_regression > baseline + 5ms.

Experiment plan / rollout:
1) Canary: implement passive lease bookkeeping without enforcement (collect signals for 2 weeks).
2) Soft enforcement: apply soft budgets and telemetry-driven gentle reclaim on low-traffic clusters.
3) Hard enforcement: enable fast-path reclaim and kernel reservations on dedicated pools.
4) Route opt-ins: provide rollout flags for customers to pin behaviors.

Short-term implementation checklist (MVP):
- [ ] Lease token structure + APIs (renew, expire, mark-evictable).
- [ ] Per-stream lane tagging and route-level config mapping.
- [ ] Lightweight per-worker priority bitmap + reclaim signal.
- [ ] Microbatch window config knobs and adaptive controller.
- [ ] Telemetry events for lease lifecycle and prefill yield.

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- TTL sizing: static defaults are a crude start; can we learn TTLs per-customer or per-route cheaply?
- Kernel reservation size: too large wastes capacity; too small causes tail regressions. Tie to autoscaler metrics?
- Staleness semantics: what are minimal invariants for customer-facing KV semantics?
- Interoperability with existing KV-sharding / checkpointing pipeline; need to avoid doubling memory.

Notes and references:
- Related PR: runtime#987 (experimental k/v lease bookkeeping).
- Jira: RT-4512 (observe lease churn), RT-4589 (interactive tail regressions).
- Followups: bench microbatch sizes under mixed traffic and simulate with synthetic traces from prod replay.

Appendix (quick pseudo flow):
- On request arrival: classify lane -> try to acquire slot-lease with ttl -> schedule prefetch microbatch if lane allows -> mark kernels with affinity hints -> push to run-queue.
- On pressure event: set reclaim-bit -> prefill lanes see reclaim and stop non-essential prefetch -> interactive lanes use reserved kernels or get promoted by fast-path.

This is a working draft. Feedback wanted on: lease TTL heuristics, eviction fallback UX, and kernel-affinity taxonomy.

title: Real-world Inference: Mission & Positioning Brief
preview:
Purpose and audience:
This one-pager is for internal GTM, field, and product teams who need a concise, pragmatic story to explain why Redwood matters to engineers, platform teams, and technical buyers. Use this to align talk tracks for demos, early pitch decks, and onboarding for sales/solutions engineers.

Core mission statement (single sentence):
Make inference predictable, affordable, and operable so engineering teams can ship features rather than fight infrastructure.

Thesis (the why, in plain terms):
As models proliferate and hardware diversity explodes, competitive advantage moves from model selection to how reliably and cheaply you can serve model outputs at scale. Customers don’t buy models — they buy predictable latency, unit economics, and the ability to iterate without production regressions. Redwood is built around that reality: a unified runtime, observability, and policy layer that treats serving as the product, not the plumbing.

Why this matters to buyers (three core promises):
- Predictable latency and cost: teams can set SLOs and predictable budgets across hosted, reserved, and private deployments.
- Seamless operational controls: rollout policies, routing, caching, and fallbacks that reduce incidents and make releases safe.
- Developer-forward ergonomics for ops objectives: simple APIs + first-class telemetry so engineers can iterate quickly without sacrificing reliability.

Positioning pillars (how to talk about Redwood without jargon):
1) Serving-as-a-product: Redwood abstracts the messy parts of inference (kernel choices, KV cache, batching behavior) and exposes clear knobs and metrics. Frame the conversation around outcomes (requests/sec, p99 latency, $/1M tokens), not internals.
2) Economics-first engineering: talk in concrete tradeoffs — compression/quantization profiles, cache hit uplift, batch sweet spots — and show how they move the cost needle. Buyers care about dollars per MAU or per session.
3) Uniformity across ops modes: compare to the friction of stitching cloud endpoints, on-prem servers, and ad-hoc caching. Redwood is the same control plane across hosted, dedicated, and private.
4) Safety net for production: emphasize fallbacks, canary rollouts, and automatic model variant routing to avoid outages when capacity shifts or a model quality regression slips into prod.

Short competitive frame (what to say about competitors):
- General cloud model endpoints: They give raw compute but not the opinionated defaults, batching heuristics, and cost meters that make inference efficient at scale. Distinguish Redwood on outcomes and tooling for long-lived production use, not just model access.
- API-first LLM vendors: Many are easiest to start with but bake cost and vendor lock-in into product decisions. Our pitch: start easily with hosted, then grow into Dedicated or Private without rewriting your stack.
- Self-hosted runtimes / OSS stacks: They can be cheaper in theory but require expertise and ongoing maintenance. Redwood sells the operational experience and SLAs that teams want when they don’t want to hire a separate inference ops org.

Key proof points and evidence to include in field materials: 
- Measured p99 improvements and $/1M token comparisons on representative workloads (search, chat, embeddings).
- Case vignette: a mid-market SaaS company cut inference cost by ~38% after enabling caching + quantization profiles while maintaining 95th-percentile quality marks on critical prompt sets.
- Reliability story: two customers who avoided major outages by using Redwood’s model fallback routing during a regional capacity event.

Primary buyer personas and tailored hooks: 
- Platform/Infrastructure Lead (Platform Teams): Hook -> "Make inference repeatable across product teams and reduce toil." Speak in commits, SLAs, and multi-tenant cost allocation.
- ML Engineer / Applied ML (Builders): Hook -> "Ship features, not infra." Emphasize fast iteration, model pinning, and eval/regression alerts.
- Product/Engineering Manager: Hook -> "Deliver predictable UX and unit economics for conversational features." Show business metrics (latency -> engagement, cost -> margin).

Top-line messaging options (short soundbites to A/B test):
- "Predictable inference. Predictable product."
- "Ship LLM features without the infrastructure tax."
- "Inference that scales — on your terms."

Tactical talking points for demos and discovery calls: 
- Discovery: ask about traffic patterns, p50/p95/p99 targets, acceptable cost per session, and whether teams have run into model regressions in prod.
- Demo: show deploy-time profile selection (quantization + batching), live cost/latency dashboard, and a canary rollback in under 90 seconds.
- Objection handling: for cost objections, point to specific dial (cache TTL, batching config) and show a modeled cost delta. For security/regulatory asks, surface Private deployment options and KMS/HSM integrations.

Open questions we should resolve before finalizing copy: 
- Standardized benchmark set for our "cost per workload" claims — which five workloads and what prompts do we publish?
- One coherent customer vignette with numbers and permission to share.
- Finalize canary rollback UI copy so SEs can demo it verbatim.

Next steps and owners: 
- GTM content: produce a one-slide quant economics visual (owner: Priya Singh, due: 2026-03-05).
- Benchmarks: finalize benchmark dataset and run across representative models (owner: Eng applied-ml, due: 2026-03-12).
- Field play: equip SEs with a 90s demo script and rebuttal card (owner: Sales Enablement, due: 2026-03-10).

Tone guidance: lean pragmatic and measurable. Avoid hype about model size or proprietary model claims; emphasize operational outcomes, economics, and clear migration paths.

Notes to self / rough phrases for future assets:
- "Batch smarter, not bigger."
- "Pin on day one, roll safely forever."
- "Operate inference like a service feature, not a research experiment."

End of brief.

title: capacity-blend-cost-optimizer-onprem-vpc-draft
preview:
Purpose and scope
- Quick working draft: build a compact, parameterized spreadsheet and narrative that helps sales/product recommend a capacity-blend (steady-state on‑prem + burst VPC) and pick a licensing/support bundle to minimize blended unit cost while preserving SLA.
- Audience: sales engineering, product leads, solutions eng, finance.
- Status: exploratory model + sensitivity knobs; not final pricing.

Top-line hypothesis
- For sustained baseline throughput (>40% of peak) an amortized on‑prem footprint with a modest enterprise support bundle gives lower unit cost after ~18–30 months.
- For spiky or unpredictable workloads, hybrid (on‑prem baseline + VPC for peaks) reduces required on‑prem overprovisioning and often yields lower blended TCO when accounting for ops FTE and refresh risk.

Model structure (spreadsheet layout notes)
- Sheet tabs: assumptions, capacity-sizing, hardware-capex, ops-and-support, licensing-bundles, vpc-burst-costs, blended-scenarios, sensitivity-analysis, summary-charts.
- All base parameters single-sourced on "assumptions" tab so sales/engineers can change: HW unit price, GPU type, utilization, refresh years, power/PUE, datacenter cost per kWh, network egress $/GB, onprem-rack-density, ops-FTE salary + burden, support-bundle-multipliers, reserved-vpc-discount.

Key formulas (put as human-readable formula text in assumptions tab)
- Annualized hardware cost = (HW_capex + install_costs + one_time_network) / refresh_years + (HW_capex * annual_hw_maintenance_rate)
- Ops cost (annual) = sum(ops_headcount * fully_burdened_salary) + managed-services-fee
- Licensing annual = base_license_fee * license_multiplier(bundle) + per-instance-seat * seat_count
- VPC burst hourly = sum( instance_hourly * count * (1 - reserved_pct) * (1 - spot_pct) ) + egress_estimate + data-store IOPS estimates
- Unit cost per 1k tokens = (annual_total_cost / annual_tokens_processed) * 1000

Representative baseline numbers (editable):
- Target steady QPS baseline: 200 qps (avg), 650 qps peak
- GPU: A100-80GB equivalent; unit price (rack-level) $320k including networking & chassis
- Rack GPU density: 8 GPUs per rack after cooling/power constraints
- Refresh cycle: 4 years; annual maintenance rate: 8% of capex
- Ops: 1.0 infra engineer per 10 GPUs fully-burdened $220k; 0.25 infra ops per rack for DC ops $150k
- Support bundles (examples): Standard (SLAs up to 24h, 12% license uplift), Enterprise (4h, 22% uplift), Enterprise+ (SRE on-call, 35% uplift)
- Network: DC bandwidth reserved $0.02 / GB; cloud egress $0.09 / GB

Mini example calculation (editable cells referenced as A1.. for clarity)
- HW_capex_total = 4 racks * $1,280,000 = $5,120,000
- Annualized_hw = 5,120,000 / 4 + (5,120,000 * 0.08) = 1,280,000 + 409,600 = $1,689,600 / year
- Ops_annual = (8 GPUs => 0.8 FE * $220k + 0.5 DC ops * $150k) ~ $286,000 / year (rounded)
- Licensing_example = $250,000 base + 22% enterprise uplift = $305,000 / year
- Total_onprem = 1,689,600 + 286,000 + 305,000 + misc_network(60k) = $2,340,600 / year
- Annual_tokens_processed estimate = baseline_throughput_tokens_per_sec * 31.5M secs/year (adjust for utilization) -> produce units for unit cost
- Unit cost ex: if tokens/year = 30B -> unit cost per 1k tokens = (2,340,600 / 30,000,000) * 1000 = $0.078 / 1k tokens

VPC burst slice example
- Assume peak bursts consume additional 450 qps for 8 hours/day for 120 days/year -> hours = 8*120 = 960 hours
- VPC instance mix: 10 x p4d equiv at $40/hr discounted -> gross = $384k; after 35% reserved/spot mix -> $249.6k
- Egress for burst data: 120 TB * $0.09 = $10,800
- VPC-burst-annual ~ $260k

Blended scenario
- Onprem sized for 60% of peak => smaller racks (2 racks) annualized_onprem ~ $844,800; licensing can be prorated or seat-based; ops headcount reduced
- Blended total = onprem (partial baseline) + VPC for predictable burst = example $844,800 + $260k + licensing(200k) = $1,304,800 -> unit cost materially lower for bursty workloads vs pure-onprem overprovisioning

Support & bundling tradeoffs (narrative)
- Standard bundle: lower $ uplift but longer mean time to mitigation for high-risk customers (finance/legal heavy). Good for internal-only or low-SLA customers.
- Enterprise bundle: recommended default for Dedicated customers; includes 24/7 pager, faster RPOs and runbook support. Uplift covers an SRE resource + priority queueing.
- Enterprise+ (SRE embedding): cost is high but reduces ops FTE uncertainty; best for highly regulated customers who need strict uptime + SLOs.
- Pricing approach: show 3 columns for each scenario: (A) pure onprem (full peak), (B) blended (baseline onprem + vpc burst), (C) pure-vpc (reserved commit). For each, show annualized hardware, ops, licensing, network, cloud-burst, margins.

Sensitivity knobs to include in model
- GPU spot/reserved mix in VPC (0–80%)
- Hardware price delta (±20%)
- Refresh period (3–5 years)
- Utilization windows (baseline % of peak 30–80%)
- Network egress assumptions (±50%)
- Support bundle uplift (from 8% to 40%)

Sales play recommendations (initial)
- For customers with >18 months runway and predictable baseline (>40% peak), recommend onprem with Enterprise bundle; prepare blended option if legal/regulatory prefer not to go full cloud.
- For customers with unpredictable growth or severe seasonal spikes, propose blended baseline with aggressive VPC-reserved for forecasted peaks and spot for unpredictable spikes. Use contractual burst credits to control cost.
- Offer a 12–18 month "starter foot print": 2-rack onprem with Enterprise+ for first 6 months, then right-size and switch to Enterprise after stabilizing. This reduces time-to-value and near-term capex sticker shock.

Open questions / data needed to finalize spreadsheet
- Precise customer tokenization profile (tokens per inference) and request size distribution.
- Preferred SLA targets (p50/p90/p99 latency requirements) vs budget constraints.
- Whether licensing is per-host, per-GPU, or per-seat for target accounts (changes uplift math).
- Discount bands for multi-year dedicated commits and partner discounts.

Next steps (short backlog)
- Build canonical spreadsheet with named cells and a scenario toggler (checkboxes for blended vs pure options). -> owner: Priya Nair; due 2026-02-28
- Run 3 customer example cases (SaaS mid-market, regulated enterprise finance, AI-native startup) and store outputs in /shared_drives/product/private-deployments/cases/
- Collect real purchase invoices for last 6 deployments to benchmark DC hardware + install line items (ops: Liam)
- Draft 1-pager decision flow (sales cheat-sheet) that maps workload profile -> recommended deployment + bundle (owner: Sofia)

Notes (scratch)
- Consider adding an "insurance"/risk premium line for onprem because of supply-chain/refresh uncertainty.
- Look into offering a "burst credit" product: pre-purchased VPC burst hours at committed discount tied to Dedicated contract.

End of draft content.
text:

- Audience: sales engineering, product leads, solutions eng, finance.
- Status: exploratory model + sensitivity knobs; not final pricing.

Top-line hypothesis
- For sustained baseline throughput (>40% of peak) an amortized on‑prem footprint with a modest enterprise support bundle gives lower unit cost after ~18–30 months.
- For spiky or unpredictable workloads, hybrid (on‑prem baseline + VPC for peaks) reduces required on‑prem overprovisioning and often yields lower blended TCO when accounting for ops FTE and refresh risk.

title: Latency + Economics: Claim Stack and Hero Variants
preview:
Purpose
---
Short brief to align on homepage and product landing copy that ties latency assurances to unit-economics claims. We need a compact claim stack that is credible to engineers and persuasive to procurement—fast to read on the site and easy to use in sales decks. This doc is intentionally lightweight: pick 2 hero variants and 3 product page claims to iterate in the next sprint.

Primary audiences
- Developer/ML engineers evaluating APIs (want predictable latency, easy SDKs, honest benchmarks)
- Platform/infra buyers (need capacity guarantees, cost modeling, ops controls)
- Product leaders who care about end-user latency impact and predictable spend

Positioning statement (single sentence)
Redwood Inference makes latency predictable and cost-efficient for production LLMs, so teams can ship features with predictable performance and predictable bills.

Why this angle?
Engineers choose platforms that reduce unknowns. The most credible lever Redwood has: observable latency guarantees + concrete unit-econ levers (batching, caching, quant profiles). Combining these into a short claim stack lets us speak to both SRE/ML engineering risk and procurement ROI.

Problem framing (2–3 lines for hero subhead)
- Modern LLMs vary widely by token cost, throughput, and tail latency. That variability makes planning and SLOs hard.
- Teams either overprovision (waste money) or accept poor UX for spikes.
- Redwood puts the levers in the customer’s hands: predictable tail latency, cost controls, and operational fallbacks.

Claim stack (top-level bullets for site and product pages)
1) Predictable tail latency: SLA-backed latency tiers with regional routing and automatic fallback.
2) Cost controls that matter: intelligent batching, KV/prefix caching, and per-route quantization suggestions that cut per-token spend without blind quality loss.
3) Unified runtime across modes: same API and tooling whether hosted, dedicated, or private—so you can migrate without refactoring.
4) Observability that ties to dollars: token-level breakdowns and attackable regressions (alerts by eval set).

Hero copy variants (short, punchy—the headline + one-sentence subhead each)
Variant A (engineer-first):
Headline: "Make inference predictable — not guesswork."
Subhead: "SLA-backed latency tiers, per-route cost controls, and the runtime that scales from dev to dedicated clusters."

Variant B (economics-first):
Headline: "Lower token cost without slowing users."
Subhead: "Smart batching, cache-aware serving, and model routing that balance latency and spend for production traffic."

Variant C (enterprise assurance):
Headline: "High-performance inference with enterprise guarantees."
Subhead: "Regional routing, private control planes, and rollback-safe rollouts for regulated workloads."

Variant D (developer velocity):
Headline: "Ship LLM features that behave in production."
Subhead: "Same API across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments — plus tooling to keep latency steady as you scale."

Variant E (concise):
Headline: "Predictable latency. Predictable cost."
Subhead: "Run production LLMs with SLAs, insights, and controls that reduce surprises."

Microcopy and CTA candidates
- Primary CTA options: "Start with predictable latency", "Try latency tiers", "Estimate monthly token cost"
- Secondary microcopy under CTAs: "No infra changes. SDKs in minutes.", "Free latency audit with sample traffic", "See per-route cost breakdowns"

Product page claim breakdown (three focused blocks to live on product pages)
1) Serving runtime + optimizations (techproof):
Headline: "A runtime built for production tokens"
Bullets: "continuous batching, KV-aware caching, quantization profiles, kernel selection per-hardware"
Proof cue: "Benchmarks available: 95th percentile latency vs alternatives (internal github benchmarks link)."

2) Economics toolkit (operator-facing):
Headline: "Cut per-token spend without sacrificing UX"
Bullets: "Adaptive batching policies, cache-first routing, auto-suggest quantization per workload"
Proof cue: "Customer case: 40% unit cost reduction on chat workload with <5% degradation to metric X (internal case study)."

3) Deployment parity (ops proof):
Headline: "Same controls across hosted, dedicated, and private"
Bullets: "Pin model versions, canary and A/B rollouts, network isolation / KMS integrations"
Proof cue: "Compliance-ready checklist for SOC2 / FedRAMP-readiness (internal doc link)."

Competitive notes (plain-language observations, for copy & rebuttals)
- Hyperscalers: competing on price and scale, but often lack turnkey latency SLAs per-customer and the fine-grained per-route tooling. Emphasize predictable tail latency and model routing as differentiators.
- LLM API players: ease-of-use is strong; some have better model catalogs. Our counter: we provide the same UX plus billing/latency visibility and on-prem parity.
- Self-host/open-source runtimes: cheaper at pure infra marginal cost but higher engineering ops and slower time-to-reliability. Call out the hidden engineering cost of owning serving for production.

Objection handling (short lines for FAQ or sales snippets)
- "We can self-host for cheaper." 
Response: "Maybe — but total cost of ownership, time-to-reliability, and incident risk typically exceed marginal infra savings. We quantify the tradeoffs and offer Dedicated options to match procurement needs."
- "How do you measure latency in mixed workloads?"
Response: "We publish per-route latency distributions and provide traffic replay tools to validate in your environment before commit."

A/B test matrix (priority experiments for next 4 weeks)
1) Hero headline A vs E (engineer clarity vs concise economic hook) — metric: click-to-signup and demo requests.
2) Subhead with numeric proof vs qualitative proof (e.g., "95th p latency < X ms" vs "SLA-backed latency tiers") — metric: trial conversion in high-traffic segments.
3) CTA phrasing: "Estimate monthly token cost" vs "Start free trial" in paid-up funnel — metric: lead quality and MQL to SQL conversion.

Measurement plan and signals
- Primary signals: demo request rate, trial activation rate, and signups from engineering-targeted pages.
- Secondary signals: time-on-page for product pages, scroll depth to claim stack, click heatmap on metric proof links.
- Sales signal: demo-to-contract conversion for leads originating from pages with latency proofs.

Quick content hygiene (copy rules for writers)
- Use concrete numbers where available; flag when numbers are estimates.
- Avoid vague superlatives without proof (e.g., "fastest"). Prefer "95th percentile latency under X ms on GPU Y".
- Prefer engineer-oriented language on core product pages; use more outcome-oriented language on pricing/enterprise pages.

Next steps and owners
- Freeze two hero variants by EOD next Tuesday: Ethan to pick 2, Aisha to finalize microcopy.
- Create simple benchmark snippet for site (Samir): anonymized 95th p numbers + test parameters.
- Implement A/B test scaffold (growth): link to experiment JIRA JIRA-GTM-3301.

Notes to reviewers
This is a pragmatic, test-first brief: we expect to iterate after we see early signal. The goal is to reduce ambiguity between engineering claims and procurement value, not to produce one definitive narrative on first pass. Please add specific objections or missing proof points in the Slack thread linked above.

title: Vendor inference security onboarding checklist (draft)
preview:
Purpose:
This checklist is a working draft intended for early-stage vendor security and privacy assessment when the vendor will host or operate LLM inference (hosted, dedicated, or private). Focus is on inference-specific risks: in-flight and cached context (KV/prefix caches), prompt/response logging, model provenance, and edge or private-hosted deployments.

Scope:
- Applies to third-party vendors and partners that will handle live inference traffic or provide managed inference runtime components.
- Not a replacement for full legal DPA negotiation; use as intake + evidence request to feed legal and procurement.

How to use:
1) Run intake call with vendor and fill the intake section below.
2) Request evidence items in parallel (technical screenshots, config excerpts, architecture diagrams).
3) Score risks and determine negotiation items.

INTAKE (to capture in first 48 hours)
- Vendor name / legal entity: __________________
- Point of contact (technical and legal): __________________
- Deployment mode proposed: hosted-api | dedicated-pool | private-vpc | on-prem
- Supported model types and versions (open weights / licensed / proprietary): __________________
- Expected traffic pattern: median seq_len, peak tpm, concurrency
- Is the vendor storing prompts/outputs beyond the request lifecycle? (yes/no/ephemeral) — explain: __________________
- Does vendor use prefix/KV caching? If yes, describe eviction/expiration policy and whether cache is persisted to disk.

TECHNICAL CONTROL CHECKLIST (evidence required where marked)
A. Network & Infrastructure - Is customer traffic segmented from other tenants at the network level? (VPCs, subnets, tenant-specific routing) [evidence: network diagram]
- Are control-plane and data-plane separated (config/API vs inference path)? [evidence: deployment diagram]
- Support for customer-managed VPC peering, private link, or dedicated tenancy?

B. Model & Weights Handling - Model provenance: Provide license/TOU evidence for any non-open models and provenance chain for fine-tuned artifacts. [evidence: licenses, S3 manifests]
- Are model weights treated as customer data if customers upload fine-tuning artifacts? If yes, describe retention and deletion flows.

C. KV/Prefix Cache and Ephemeral State (critical for inference) - Describe KV/prefix cache lifecycle: in-RAM only, snapshot to disk, or persisted object store?
- Eviction policy and maximum TTL for cached entries (document defaults and per-tenant overrides).
- Is cached state scrubbed of PII before being cached? If not, what controls are used?
- Evidence requested: cache config screenshot, cache encryption settings, sample cache-flush API call.

D. Logging, Observability & Residual Data - What logging is enabled by default for inference requests (request+response, request IDs, token-level traces)?
- Are logs redacted by default; if not, provide redaction pipeline and retention policies. [evidence: log retention policy, sample redacted log]
- Can customers opt-out of request retention or enable ephemeral-only mode? If so, how (API flag / contract clause)?

E. Encryption & Key Management - Encryption in transit: TLS 1.2+ required for API endpoints?
- Encryption at rest: which layers are encrypted (model weights, KV cache snapshots, logs, attachments)?
- KMS/HSM integration options (customer-managed keys via KMS or Cloud HSM). [evidence: KMS config guide]

F. Access Controls & Admin Operations - Role-based access control for platform operators and support staff (RBAC) — provide roles matrix.
- Is there privileged access for vendor engineers to customer inference data? If yes, describe justifications and safeguards (just-in-time access, recorded sessions). [evidence: access review logs]

G. Privacy & Data Minimization - Does vendor perform telemetry sampling on inference data? If yes, what sampling rate and was customer consent assumed?
- Policy for customer-provided PII in prompts: recommended best practices and available tooling (PII scrubber, token filters).

H. Incident Response & Forensics - RTO/RPO for inference infra incidents and customer notification windows.
- Evidence: runbook excerpt for data exposure incidents, contact matrix.

CONTRACT / DPA REDLINES (suggested draft language to propose to vendor legal)
- Ephemeral data retention: "Vendor shall not persist inference request or response content beyond the configured ephemeral TTL (default XX hours) unless explicitly authorized in writing by Customer. Ephemeral caches must be flushable via API or by vendor within 24 hours of request."
- Cache persistence prohibition: "Vendor will not snapshot or persist KV/prefix cache data to non-volatile storage unless Customer has provided explicit written consent and the storage is subject to equivalent contractual encryption and access controls."
- Logging opt-out: "Customer may elect an 'no-retention' mode where full request/response bodies are not written to persistent logs; only request identifiers and minimal telemetry will be retained for billing and metrics."
- KMS requirement: "Customer shall have the option to provide and control cryptographic keys (via [cloud KMS]/HSM) for encryption of model artifacts and persisted inference caches."
- Model weight licensing covenant: "Vendor represents that any model weights served under this Agreement are properly licensed and that no customer data has been used to train models without Customer's express consent."

EVIDENCE PACKET (request in parallel)
- Architecture diagram showing inference path, cache, log sinks, and control plane.
- Cache config/export demonstrating TTL, eviction, and whether persisted to disk.
- Sample IAM/RBAC policy documents and privileged access audit for last 90 days.
- Log retention policy and a redacted example of an inference log entry.
- DCO/Runbook excerpt for data exfiltration incident.

RISK SCORING (quick heuristic for intake)
- Data retention risk (0-5): 0 = explicit no-persistence, 5 = persistent full request logs retained indefinitely.
- KV-cache risk (0-5): 0 = RAM-only ephemeral flushable, 5 = persisted multi-tenant caches with long TTLs.
- Access risk (0-5): 0 = no vendor operator access; 5 = broad operator access without JIT or recording.
- Compliance match (0-5): 0 = supports required controls (KMS/HSM, VPC), 5 = cannot meet basic compliance asks.

NEGOTIATION NOTES / TACTICS - Prioritize contractual commitments on cache persistence and log retention as non-negotiable for regulated customers.
- For vendors refusing KMS, consider limited-scope pilot with strong logging and short TTLs as mitigation.
- If vendor requires model-hosted caching for performance, require customer-visible toggle and documented acceptable use cases.

OPEN QUESTIONS / DECISIONS REQUIRED - What TTL can product accept for aggressive caching before customer risk is unacceptable? (ops/product to decide)
- Will legal accept 'no-retention' as a paid feature or require it be standard for enterprise tiers?

SIGN-OFF CRITERIA (recommendation for procurement)
- All technical evidence items received and validated by eng-security.
- Risk scoring aggregated <= 6 for non-regulated customers; <=3 for regulated or enterprise with strict data residency.
- Contractual redlines for cache persistence, logging opt-out, and KMS included and accepted (or alternative mitigations documented).

APPENDIX: quick sample clause for DPA insertion (editable)
\"Vendor acknowledges that inference request content and generated outputs may constitute Customer Personal Data. Vendor shall: (1) treat such data as Confidential; (2) not persist request/response content beyond transient processing unless documented and authorized; (3) support Customer request to purge cached inference state within 24 hours via API or vendor operations; (4) provide documented evidence of cache purge on completion.\"

Notes to reviewers:
- This is intentionally prescriptive on caching and logging because inference workloads expose new classes of residual data not captured by standard DPA templates.
- Engineering: please attach any microbenchmarks that justify caching TTLs and trade-offs for latency vs risk.
- Legal: suggested redline options included above; please iterate on precise time windows (24h vs 72h) and acceptable evidence types.

History / comments (work-in-progress):
- 2025-11-04 created by Ethan Park (initial draft)
- 2025-12-12 added KV-cache evidence section (Sophia Li)
- 2026-01-28 added contract clause language and risk scoring heuristics (Marcus Reed)
- 2026-02-15 minor edits and clarified sign-off matrix (Ethan Park)
text:
Purpose:
This checklist is a working draft intended for early-stage vendor security and privacy assessment when the vendor will host or operate LLM inference (hosted, dedicated, or private). Focus is on inference-specific risks: in-flight and cached context (KV/prefix caches), prompt/response logging, model provenance, and edge or private-hosted deployments.

Scope:
- Applies to third-party vendors and partners that will handle live inference traffic or provide managed inference runtime components.
- Not a replacement for full legal DPA negotiation; use as intake + evidence request to feed legal and procurement.

How to use:
1) Run intake call with vendor and fill the intake section below.
2) Request evidence items in parallel (technical screenshots, config excerpts, architecture diagrams).
3) Score risks and determine negotiation items.

INTAKE (to capture in first 48 hours)
- Vendor name / legal entity: __________________
- Point of contact (technical and legal): __________________
- Deployment mode proposed: hosted-api | dedicated-pool | private-vpc | on-prem
- Supported model types and versions (open weights / licensed / proprietary): __________________
- Expected traffic pattern: median seq_len, peak tpm, concurrency
- Is the vendor storing prompts/outputs beyond the request lifecycle? (yes/no/ephemeral) — explain: __________________
- Does vendor use prefix/KV caching? If yes, describe eviction/expiration policy and whether cache is persisted to disk.

TECHNICAL CONTROL CHECKLIST (evidence required where marked)
A. Network & Infrastructure - Is customer traffic segmented from other tenants at the network level? (VPCs, subnets, tenant-specific routing) [evidence: network diagram]
- Are control-plane and data-plane separated (config/API vs inference path)? [evidence: deployment diagram]
- Support for customer-managed VPC peering, private link, or dedicated tenancy?

B. Model & Weights Handling - Model provenance: Provide license/TOU evidence for any non-open models and provenance chain for fine-tuned artifacts. [evidence: licenses, S3 manifests]
- Are model weights treated as customer data if customers upload fine-tuning artifacts? If yes, describe retention and deletion flows.

C. KV/Prefix Cache and Ephemeral State (critical for inference) - Describe KV/prefix cache lifecycle: in-RAM only, snapshot to disk, or persisted object store?
- Eviction policy and maximum TTL for cached entries (document defaults and per-tenant overrides).
- Is cached state scrubbed of PII before being cached? If not, what controls are used?
- Evidence requested: cache config screenshot, cache encryption settings, sample cache-flush API call.

D. Logging, Observability & Residual Data - What logging is enabled by default for inference requests (request+response, request IDs, token-level traces)?
- Are logs redacted by default; if not, provide redaction pipeline and retention policies. [evidence: log retention policy, sample redacted log]
- Can customers opt-out of request retention or enable ephemeral-only mode? If so, how (API flag / contract clause)?

E. Encryption & Key Management - Encryption in transit: TLS 1.2+ required for API endpoints?
- Encryption at rest: which layers are encrypted (model weights, KV cache snapshots, logs, attachments)?
- KMS/HSM integration options (customer-managed keys via KMS or Cloud HSM). [evidence: KMS config guide]

title: Forge Sentry — Implementation Sprint Jot
preview:
Daily scratchpad for Forge Sentry kickoff + sprint implementation. Very rough — only for me + core SEs.

Context: customer is an early-stage fintech (sandbox -> prod in 6 weeks). Must run in their VPC, HSM for keys, strict egress controls. Primary workload: policy QA + short-form generation (avg seq 180 tokens), peak concurrency ~220 rps expected in month 3.

Goals this sprint (week 1):
- final infra diagram for VPC peering + NAT flow (Priya to validate with infra)
- confirm model pin and fallback model family (quantized 4-bit candidate)
- initial perf baseline on dedicated pool (1xA10G, 2xA10G tests)
- create canary rollout route in Console (route: forge-sentry-canary)

Immediate TODOs (quick):
- [ ] get customer KMS details (contact: amanda@forge) — ask about HSM vs KMS
- [ ] add security checklist to onboarding Confluence page (Jorge)
- [ ] run quick ak-run to measure p50/p95 for seq=180 (local script: ./scripts/p95-smoke.sh --model rwd-llama-7b-q4)
- [ ] pin model in config: model: "rwd-llama-7b-q4a"; fallback: "rwd-llama-7b-q8"

Config notes / snippets to copy into customer repo:
- autoscale: min_replicas: 2, max_replicas: 12, target_rps_per_replica: 40
- batching: preferred: latency_slo_ms: 120, max_batch_size: 64, allow_delay_ms: 8
- kv_cache: enabled: true, prewarm: true

Open questions / risks:
- Will their egress rules block kernel upload during initial bundle? -> Need offline bundling option (deliver tar + sha256)
- HSM latency: call pattern will add ~12–20ms; must adjust SLOs
- Cost sensitivity: finance asked for 30% of RU target; propose aggressive prefix caching to reduce per-token spend

Notes from call 2026-02-12:
- Amanda: legal pushing for no logs outside customer VPC; want server-side encryption + no telemetry.
- Recommended approach: Private deployment with control plane in Redwood, but customer insists on self-host control plane (air-gapped). Escalate to product for private packaging timeline.

Next actions for me:
- Draft runbook for air-gapped install (skeleton in this doc)
- Schedule 1:1 with Lina to scope dedicated pool sizing math (Thu 11am)

title: incremental-output-latency-fingerprint-journal
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This is a working lab journal capturing an incremental, empirical fingerprint of per-stage contributions to end-to-end response latency for streaming/incremental-output requests. Goal: record representative trace shapes (p50/p95/p99), coarse per-stage breakdowns, hypotheses for observed long-tail behaviors, and low-risk mitigations to iterate in the next sprint. This doc is intentionally informal — notes-to-self + triage trail for the perf triage meeting.

Context and motivation:
- Recent customer reports (ENG-2124) showed sporadic p99 spikes for chat-style streaming on a medium-sized model (1.3B-ish open model) when average sequence lengths were > 600 tokens.
- Baseline change: release v0.9.7 introduced a micro-optimization in KV cache metadata handling and expanded async-batcher windowing default from 20ms -> 30ms (PR 1125). Suspect interaction between larger batch windows and KV-PAGE eviction pacing.

Test environment (short):
- Cluster: dedicated-dev-pool-6 (A100-sim nodes), 16 GPU pods, model-sharded 2-way.
- Workload: 2 steady drivers generating mixed chat traffic; 60% short-turn (<60 tokens), 30% medium (60-600), 10% long (>600).
- Instrumentation: per-token trace spans: request-receive, tokenization, routing, batch-wait, kv-lookup, model-forward, serialize, network-egress. Collected 10k end-to-end traces per run.

Representative aggregated numbers (run on 2026-01-26):
- P50 end-to-end: 85ms
- P95 end-to-end: 220ms
- P99 end-to-end: 1,050ms

Per-stage median and tail highlights (approx, numbers are ms):
- tokenization: p50 2  | p95 6   | p99 12
- routing (policy evaluation): p50 1 | p95 4 | p99 10
- batch-wait: p50 10 | p95 45 | p99 310
- kv-lookup (cache read + lock): p50 8 | p95 24 | p99 180
- model-forward (per-token average): p50 40 | p95 85 | p99 220
- serialize+egress: p50 6 | p95 18 | p99 60

Notes on distribution patterns:
- The p99 is dominated by a combination of batch-wait spikes and kv-lookup stalls. ~60% of p99 traces have batch-wait > 200ms OR kv-lookup > 150ms (often both).
- Model-forward contributes heavily to p95 for long sequences (pipeline backpressure), but rarely the dominant cause of p99 when kv/batch issues present.
- Two recurring trace phenotypes observed:
  * Phenotype A: batch-window amplification — single request waits for a large batch that then faces KV contention; timeline: request-receive -> wait 220ms -> kv-lookup 170ms -> model-forward 80ms -> egress 20ms -> total ~550ms. Root cause appears to be large window + sudden QPS microburst.
  * Phenotype B: kv-page-eviction stall — small batch but KV access serializes on an eviction lock (eviction takes ~120ms); timeline shows kv-lookup as the long pole.

Trace excerpts (anonymized IDs):
- trace 6b1f3a: seq_len 720, turn_type: assistant-stream
  spans: receive(3ms), batch-wait(260ms), kv-lookup(190ms), forward(95ms), egress(15ms) => total 563ms
- trace 3c9d7f: seq_len 42, turn_type: user-followup
  spans: receive(2ms), batch-wait(12ms), kv-lookup(6ms), forward(39ms), egress(6ms) => total 65ms
- trace d2a4e1: seq_len 1500, turn_type: long-completion
  spans: receive(4ms), batch-wait(18ms), kv-lookup(230ms), forward(420ms spread across tokens), egress(30ms) => total 750ms

Hypotheses (ranked):
1) Increased default batch window (30ms) interacting with bursty QPS creates opportunities for larger batches that then contend on KV lookup locks/evictions when many requests target similar context sizes.
2) KV cache metadata eviction path holds a coarse-grained lock while writing metadata to persistent index in some failure paths (observed via stack traces). Eviction occasionally spikes due to a background compaction task.
3) Network serialization/backpressure is amplifying model-forward queueing under certain shard placement choices; less frequently observed but correlated with long sequences.

Quick experiments performed:
- A: Reduced async-batcher window to 12ms (local): p99 fell from 1,050ms -> 420ms; p95 dropped to 150ms. Batch size distribution shifted: median batch size -1 token, p95 batch size -3 tokens. Observation: tail improved but model-forward p95 slightly increased in some runs (more small batches -> less compute efficiency).
- B: Forced KV cache to use optimistic-read path (disabled eviction compaction) for a single pod: p99 reduced ~35% on that pod; but saw higher memory usage and transient GC events in background.
- C: Instrumented eviction path to add finer-grained timing (PR: 1125-add-eviction-timing): found periodic compaction spikes every ~22s that coincided with worst p99 windows.

Actionable mitigations (short term):
- Roll async-batcher window back to 12ms for customer-facing fleets where tail latency SLAs are strict (can be feature-flagged per pool). Rationale: empirically cuts p99 by >50% in our tests.
- Add rate-limiter bucketing for eviction compaction tasks so they avoid running concurrently with high QPS bursts. (Config knob + follow-up Jira: ENG-2178).
- Add optimistic-read fallback for KV lookups on hot keys for 0.9.x patch (monitor memory).

Medium-term engineering tasks (sprint+):
- Split eviction metadata lock into per-shard locks to avoid global serialization (design doc draft: linked PR discussion).
- Improve async-batcher adaptive algorithm: target batch-size caps for high-seqlen requests and prefer compute-packed batches to avoid kv-contention amplification.
- Add selective early-flush for streams where the first N tokens are latency-sensitive (heuristic: if response is streaming and user-facing, flush smaller early batches).

Operational notes and caveats:
- Reducing batch window increases per-token cost by ~3-7% in our microbenchmarks; need finance/price impact estimate if we roll globally.
- Optimistic-read increases memory pressure; monitor host memory + GC. In one experiment we saw a 12% mem bump on the affected pod.
- Some customers prefer predictable throughput; document rollout options for Dedicated: per-pool config and SLA tradeoffs.

Follow-ups and owners:
- Maya: drive a canary that rolls async-batcher-window -> 12ms for low-latency fleet (due 2026-02-01).
- Carlos: prototype per-shard eviction locks (design by 2026-02-08).
- Priya: track memory + GC impact of optimistic-read patch in PR 1125 (monitor dashboard update).

Questions for triage meeting:
- What p99/SLA threshold should trigger auto-switching of batch-window per-pool?
- Is per-shard eviction lock acceptable complexity vs. a stopgap (rate-limiter for compaction)?

Raw attachments and pointers:
- Local perf graphs: /mnt/perf-runs/2026-01-26/incremental-fingerprint/*.png (attached to ENG-2124 ticket).
- PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/1125 (async-batcher changes), https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/1133 (eviction timing).
- Related tickets: ENG-2124 (customer p99 reports), ENG-2178 (eviction rate-limiter), ENG-2180 (batching-adaptive-algo).

Short summary (standing):
- Observed p99 tail largely driven by a batch-window amplification effect + KV eviction stalls. Simple short-term mitigation (reduce batch-window to ~12ms) yields large p99 reductions at modest cost to throughput. Longer-term fix: reduce KV eviction serialization and make batching adaptive to request shape.

Notes-to-self: persist this fingerprint after the canary and retest with production traffic shape (esp. heavier 90th-seqlen). Keep this doc as the running lab journal for this line of investigation.

---
Change log (mini):
- 2026-01-28 (Ethan): initial capture and baseline runs appended.
- 2026-02-02 (Maya): added canary results and updated p95/p99 after window rollback.
- 2026-02-14 (Carlos): appended eviction timing discovery and recommended next steps.

- Roll async-batcher window back to 12ms for customer-facing fleets where tail latency SLAs are strict (can be feature-flagged per pool). Rationale: empirically cuts p99 by >50% in our tests.
- Add rate-limiter bucketing for eviction compaction tasks so they avoid running concurrently with high QPS bursts. (Config knob + follow-up Jira: ENG-2178).
- Add optimistic-read fallback for KV lookups on hot keys for 0.9.x patch (monitor memory).

Medium-term engineering tasks (sprint+):
- Split eviction metadata lock into per-shard locks to avoid global serialization (design doc draft: linked PR discussion).
- Improve async-batcher adaptive algorithm: target batch-size caps for high-seqlen requests and prefer compute-packed batches to avoid kv-contention amplification.
- Add selective early-flush for streams where the first N tokens are latency-sensitive (heuristic: if response is streaming and user-facing, flush smaller early batches).

Operational notes and caveats:
- Reducing batch window increases per-token cost by ~3-7% in our microbenchmarks; need finance/price impact estimate if we roll globally.
- Optimistic-read increases memory pressure; monitor host memory + GC. In one experiment we saw a 12% mem bump on the affected pod.
- Some customers prefer predictable throughput; document rollout options for Dedicated: per-pool config and SLA tradeoffs.

Follow-ups and owners:
- Maya: drive a canary that rolls async-batcher-window -> 12ms for low-latency fleet (due 2026-02-01).
- Carlos: prototype per-shard eviction locks (design by 2026-02-08).
- Priya: track memory + GC impact of optimistic-read patch in PR 1125 (monitor dashboard update).

Questions for triage meeting:
- What p99/SLA threshold should trigger auto-switching of batch-window per-pool?
- Is per-shard eviction lock acceptable complexity vs. a stopgap (rate-limiter for compaction)?

Raw attachments and pointers:
- Local perf graphs: /mnt/perf-runs/2026-01-26/incremental-fingerprint/*.png (attached to ENG-2124 ticket).
- PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/1125 (async-batcher changes), https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/1133 (eviction timing).
- Related tickets: ENG-2124 (customer p99 reports), ENG-2178 (eviction rate-limiter), ENG-2180 (batching-adaptive-algo).

Short summary (standing):
- Observed p99 tail largely driven by a batch-window amplification effect + KV eviction stalls. Simple short-term mitigation (reduce batch-window to ~12ms) yields large p99 reductions at modest cost to throughput. Longer-term fix: reduce KV eviction serialization and make batching adaptive to request shape.

Notes-to-self: persist this fingerprint after the canary and retest with production traffic shape (esp. heavier 90th-seqlen). Keep this doc as the running lab journal for this line of investigation.

---
Change log (mini):
- 2026-01-28 (Ethan): initial capture and baseline runs appended.
- 2026-02-02 (Maya): added canary results and updated p95/p99 after window rollback.
- 2026-02-14 (Carlos): appended eviction timing discovery and recommended next steps.

title: Priming lag and breadcrumb rollout notes
preview:
Purpose: quick personal scratchpad for a breadcrumb-style canary that accounts for priming/lag in KV + cache layers and client SDK warmup. Not for copy-paste ops doc but to capture heuristics, signals, and open questions before formalizing into product playbook.

Background/context:
- Observed: immediate small canary traffic often shows degraded p95/p99 until prefix KV/caches hit. Some clients -> large initial tokens (long prompts) cause cold-run tail.
- Goal: reduce noisy false positives during early minutes; ensure canary growth only when steady-state metrics stable.

High-level plan (breadcrumb approach):
- Step 0: preflight checks (infra/telemetry) -> run smoke tests in staging (latency, errors, tokens)
- Step 1: anchor cohort (shadowing) -> 0.1% production traffic with responses only logged (no client routing) for 20m
- Step 2: breadcrumb cohort -> 0.5% routed, split across low-QPS tenants, stagger start times (+5m between cohorts)
- Step 3: primed cohort -> 2% with explicit cache-warm requests (synthetic short prefixes) for first 10m, then normal traffic
- Step 4: steady expansion -> 5%, 10%, 25% with checks at each step

Priming details (what to send and why):
- For models with KV caches: run 200 synthetic prefix requests per replica to populate KV shards. Use short prompts (2-6 tokens) to get KV warm.
- For client SDK warm: push a small cohort of SDK ping requests that exercise streaming and function calls (if used) so SDK connection/JIT paths exercised.
- For embedding-heavy routes: precompute small sample embeddings for common prefixes to populate embedding cache layer.

Safety signals & thresholds (quick heuristics):
- Latency SLOs: p50 < 120ms, p95 < 350ms, p99 < 700ms (per-route baseline must be known). If any p95 or p99 increases >+25% vs baseline for 5m -> HOLD.
- Error rate: total error rate > 0.5% absolute increase vs baseline for 3m -> ROLLBACK.
- Quality regressions: monitoring small eval prompt-set (50 probes) run continuously; if mean quality score drops >0.03 -> investigate (do not auto-rollback unless paired with latency/errors).
- Cost delta: if 1-token unit cost increases >15% for a sustained 10m window -> HOLD and consult Optimize.
- Telemetry gaps: missing key traces (model id, request id, route) -> abort rollout and return to preflight.

Rollback & escape hatches:
- Immediate route pin: route 100% of newly affected tenants back to pinned model version (automated by console routing rule).
- Circuit breaker: if region-specific capacity overwhelmed, fallback to regional model variant or fallback small-model path.
- Manual rollback: support engineers get runbook with exact Linear/GH links and rollout ID; time-to-rollback target <5m after decision.

Operational checklist (pre-rollout):
- [ ] Verify canary infra (dedicated minimal pool exists and autoscaling disabled for canary).
- [ ] Traces annotated (rollout id + experiment id).
- [ ] Baseline snapshots captured (last 24h p50/p95/p99, error rate, tokens per request).
- [ ] Small eval set uploaded and scheduled to run automatically.
- [ ] Notify on-call (SRE) + product owner + growth (if customer-facing change).
- [ ] Slack channel created with integrations for Grafana, PagerDuty.

During rollout monitoring checklist:
- Check telemetry every minute for first 15m of each step.
- Validate that prefix cache hit-rate climbs (target >70% after priming window).
- Verify noop-slow-start: initial client requests should not be >2x median latency.

Notes about cohort selection (practical heuristics):
- Prefer low-traffic, non-critical tenants (internal, beta customers) for earliest breadcrumbs.
- Avoid tenants with heavy long-prompt usage during first priming window.
- If client uses SDK older than X.Y.Z, exclude until warm path validated.

Open questions / items to formalize:
- How to quantify "primed" state in a robust way across multi-tenant KV sharding? (per-replica counter? global)
- Should we create a synthetic-traffic feature in console to run priming jobs per pool (instead of synthetic callers)? Pros/cons.
- Can optimize suggest number of priming requests based on historical prefix distribution per route? Worth implementing in Optimize?
- SLA language: do we need to warn customers about warmup tail for large-version flips?

Quick rollback decision tree (for my brain):
- If error rate spike alone: rollback immediately.
- If latency spike alone but client quality unaffected: HOLD and reduce breadcrumb traffic to 0.2% to see if tail recovers.
- If quality + latency: rollback + notify customers.

Postmortem notes to capture if rollback happens:
- exact rollout id, cohort ids, start times, per-region telemetry dumps (attach)
- who made the call, who executed rollback, time-to-rollback, time-to-stable
- artifacts: linear ticket, PR that shipped, console rollout config export

Short-term experiments to try next week:
- Run a micro-experiment: 0.1% vs 0.5% vs 1% breadcrumbs with same priming budget to see priming efficiency curve.
- Measure per-replica priming time: instrument to report minutes until replica hit-rate >50%.

To-dos for me (personal):
- [ ] Convert this into a short internal playbook with prettier checklists.
- [ ] Add a Grafana dashboard panel that compares baseline vs canary p95 side-by-side for quick visual diff.
- [ ] Drop a note to Optimize team about auto-priming suggestion prototype.

Random notes / brain-dump lines (keep raw):
- "breadcrumb" naming feels right — small, spaced-out cohorts that leave 'crumbs' of primed state behind.
- need to avoid over-priming — wasted GPU cycles vs risk of noisy false positives. Balance.
- reminder: tell growth & sales team how priming works so they can set expectations for beta customers.

-- ephs (scratch)

title: All-Staff 30/60/90 slate + rapid deck notes
preview:
Context and intent:

This is a working prep doc for the upcoming all-staff (target date: Mar 4) and the compact 30/60/90 slate that execs and managers can use for follow-ups. The goal: align the company on a tight set of priorities for the next 90 days, provide the exec update narrative, and surface risks + contingency plays. Use this for the quick deck (10 slides) and the run-of-show notes we hand to hosts.

High-level narrative (one-liner):
- Keep product momentum while investing in predictability: deliver Dedicated runway + Private onboarding, reduce per-token operating cost by 18% through batching/quantization playbooks, and raise SLOs for hosted API reliability to 99.95%.

Topline metrics to show (latest):
- Hosted API 7-day p50 latency (completion): 190ms (target 170ms)
- Token rate (hosted): +42% QoQ; unit cost trending down 8% QoQ after early batching changes
- Dedicated committed utilization: 68% (goal 80% after capacity reshuffle)
- Customer NPS (inference product): 46 (benchmark 50)

Draft OKRs (compact; these are the ones we push in the all-staff):
Objective A: Make Redwood the predictable inference platform customers can trust
- KR A1: Raise hosted API uptime SLO to 99.95% measured across 30 days (currently 99.88%)
- KR A2: Ship first-phase Dedicated autoscaling policy and burst capacity feature (GA in E2E test) - rollout 50% of customers
- KR A3: Release Private onboarding checklist and automated preflight (remove manual step X) to reduce onboarding time 30%

Objective B: Lower real per-token cost for production workloads while preserving quality
- KR B1: Ship two optimized quantization profiles and recommend flow in Redwood Optimize that reduce median cost/token by 12% for seq<=512 workloads
- KR B2: Improve caching effectiveness: increase prefix reuse hit rate from 11% to 26% on top-10 customers
- KR B3: Publish cost transparency report and per-route token cost breakdown in Console (beta)

Objective C: Increase product-led growth velocity with better dev experience
- KR C1: Shorten time-to-first-successful-request (signup -> first inference) from 22m to 8m (docs + SDK sample improvements)
- KR C2: Grow self-serve paid conversion 35% QoQ across targeted use-cases (chat + embeddings combo)
- KR C3: Ship 3 reference app templates for search, summarization, and personalization with full starter prompts and evals

All-hands run-of-show (10:00 - 11:00) - 60 minutes compact
- 10:00 - 10:05 Welcome + quick scoreboard (CEO) - 3 bullets: growth, uptime, cost improvement
- 10:05 - 10:15 Product & Roadmap snapshot (Head of Product) - top 3 launches: Dedicated autoscaler, Console cost-beta, Private preflight
- 10:15 - 10:25 Customer spotlight (CS lead) - 5 minute clip + 5 minute QA
- 10:25 - 10:35 Engineering deep-dive (serving-runtime lead) - where latency wins came from (kernels, batching), remaining hotspots
- 10:35 - 10:45 Ops & Reliability (SRE) - SLOs, incident response improvements, runbook adoption
- 10:45 - 10:55 Org priorities and 30/60/90 ask (Exec synthesis) - hiring hotspots, resourcing asks, one comms/ask per team
- 10:55 - 11:00 Rapid Q&A / close (CEO + COO)

Slides: quick deck notes (each slide 30-90s)
- Slide 1: Cover + all-hands theme: "Predictability > unpredictability"
- Slide 2: Scoreboard (metrics above) with small trend charts
- Slide 3: Customer story highlight (one quote + thumb video still)
- Slide 4: Product roadmap bullets (hosted + dedicated + private) - 3 rows: What, Who, Timing
- Slide 5: Reliability story: recent incident remediation + SLO progress
- Slide 6: Cost and Optimize: headline numbers + next actions for teams
- Slide 7: Org asks (hiring, shifts) - list of 6 fast asks with owners
- Slide 8: 30/60/90 compact plan: top 3 priorities per function (ENG, PRODUCT, CS, SRE, G2M)
- Slide 9: Risk scenarios + fallback ladder (capacity shortage, model-quality regression, major outage)
- Slide 10: Closing: what to do after (team huddles, post-all-hands doc + feedback form link)

30/60/90 compact slate (what each timeslice means, for managers to cascade)
- 30-day focus (stabilize): finish Dedicated autoscaler MVP, close two highest-severity infra bugs, ship Console cost-beta to beta customers, complete Private preflight automation spec
- 60-day focus (scale): onboard first wave Dedicated customers with autoscale, roll cost-beta to GA customers, run two cross-team load tests, finish reference app templates
- 90-day focus (optimize): measure and report unit economics improvements, iterate on quantization flows, bake SLOs into routing policy and fallback ladder

Decision points we need before the all-staff (owner and deadline):
- Do we show the real hosted API p99 latency numbers (sensitive) or p95 plus note? (Owner: Priya, due: 2026-02-18)
- Finalize customer clip and permissions for the all-staff (Owner: Maya, due: 2026-02-19)
- Approve the 6 org asks and who will own comms (Owner: Carlos, due: 2026-02-20)

Open risks and contingency plays (quick list):
- Risk: Dedicated capacity hit unexpectedly high (holiday burst) -> Play: enable short-term burst pool + auto-fallback to hosted variant with explicit consumer cost note
- Risk: Quantization causes generation quality regressions -> Play: gated rollout with eval hooks, automatic rollback on regressions detected via prompt test-suite
- Risk: Console beta surfaces incorrect cost allocation -> Play: revert to previous cost breakdown and push hotfix to fix token attribution

Communications checklist (pre and post all-hands):
- Pre: short teaser email (Exec comms) + RSVP form + invite top 100 customers to watch recording (CS)
- During: Slack #company-townline live thread + pinned transcript + ops channel standby
- Post: publish deck in Confluence, create short FAQ, request team huddles to document 30/60/90 commitments in team spaces

Follow-up actions (first pass):
- Ethan: finalize draft deck + hand to CEO for edits (due 2026-02-22)
- Priya: run p99 sensitivity review and prepare wording options (due 2026-02-18)
- Maya: final customer consent and clip ready (due 2026-02-19)
- Carlos: consolidate org asks and owners (due 2026-02-20)
- Lena: QA Console cost-beta demo and record video (due 2026-02-24)

Notes to self / rough ideas (informal):
- Keep slides VERY visual; ops loves numbers but front-load them to avoid long monologue
- Avoid jargon: "quantization" needs a one-line plain-English explanation + link to deeper doc for folks who want it
- Consider a 2-question pulse at end of all-hands: "Do you have enough clarity to act?" and "What single roadblock should execs triage?"

Appendix: short draft exec script bullets (CEO)
- Opening: thanks, quick score (growth + reliability + cost), one story about a customer who switched because of reliability
- Middle: product momentum (top 3 wins), engineering note on where latency came down, what remains
- Close: concrete asks (6 asks), where to find follow-ups and how we will measure impact, 2-week check-in cadence on all-hands commitments

Quick links (internal):
- Confluence draft deck: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ALL/All-Staff+Mar+2026
- Ops ticket for autoscale: https://jira.redwood.ai/browse/PD-919
- Cost-beta checklist: https://docs.redwood.ai/cost-beta/checklist

Unresolved questions (log):
- Should we surface org-level hiring numbers in the all-staff or keep it to teams? (Decision pending from People Ops)
- Which customers should be in the beta cohort list? (CS to propose top 12)

If you edit this doc: leave short notes at the top with initials and date. This is intended to be the single source of truth for the compact all-staff run-of-show and the 30/60/90 asks that teams will cascade.

End.
