title: Cost Model API v1 — Proto/JSON Shape Outline (WIP)
Research: Time skew in air-gapped environments (impact on offline bundle signature verification)
preview:
Last updated: 2026-01-18
Owner: Ethan Park

Context / why this doc exists
- We’re adding cryptographic signing + verification for Redwood Private offline bundles (air-gapped installs). Multiple pilot customers reported install failures caused by system clock drift ("certificate not yet valid" / validity checks failing even though the bundle is legitimate).
- Air-gapped environments often have strict time-source policies (no outbound NTP, sometimes no inbound NTP, sometimes "time is local responsibility" with manual changes).
- This doc collects what we’ve learned about time sources/constraints, proposes verification behavior (fail/allow/warn), and recommends defaults + diagnostics.

Scope
- Time skew realities in air-gapped / restricted networks.
- NTP/PTP restrictions and common time architectures.
- How signature validity windows should be handled for signed offline bundles.
- UX + support diagnostics recommendations.

Non-goals
- Not choosing the overall signature scheme (see ADR-0147).
- Not designing full customer-managed signing roots (future).


1) What we’ve observed in customer environments (pilots + discovery)
1.1 Typical constraints
- No outbound internet (hard air-gap). Some environments also disallow any inbound connections from "less trusted" segments.
- NTP often blocked by default (UDP/123) and treated as "network service" requiring explicit approval.
- Even if NTP is allowed, the approved NTP servers are internal only (DC time appliances).
- Some customers have strict change-control: updating time configuration can take weeks.
- Virtualization layers: hypervisor time sync policies vary; sometimes VM time drifts under load.

1.2 Common failure symptoms we’ve seen / expect
- Installer preflight fails with messages equivalent to:
  - signature/cert not yet valid
  - signature/cert expired
  - system time appears incorrect
- Customer response is often: "we cannot change system time quickly" or "we can’t enable NTP".

1.3 Pilot anecdote (sanitized)
- Helios Defense pilot: clocks differed by ~45–90 minutes between bastion host and kube nodes. Bundle was signed recently; verifier on a skewed node evaluated notBefore/notAfter incorrectly.
- AsterBank: cluster nodes sync to an internal Windows time hierarchy; a subset of nodes were misconfigured (domain controller reachability). Outcome was intermittent verification errors depending on which node ran the installer job.


2) Primer: Time sources in air-gapped environments
2.1 "Good" time sources we should expect customers may have
- Internal NTP stratum (time appliance or dedicated servers)
  - e.g., Meinberg, Spectracom, or custom hardened NTP servers.
  - Often GPS-backed, sometimes radio time (WWVB/DCF77), sometimes PTP grandmaster.
- AD / Windows Time Service (common in enterprise)
  - Often good enough but can drift if hierarchy is broken.
- PTP (Precision Time Protocol, IEEE 1588)
  - More common in low-latency trading / industrial environments; sometimes used in private datacenters.
- Hardware RTC (real-time clock) and manual operator setting
  - High risk of drift; depends on process rigor.

2.2 "Poor" or risky time sources we should plan for
- VM host time that drifts under resource contention.
- Manual time setting without formal procedure.
- Nodes that are intentionally isolated from internal time servers.
- Clusters where not all nodes have consistent time configuration.

2.3 Time sync architecture patterns we see
- Pattern A: Centralized internal NTP (ideal)
  - Installer host + all k8s nodes can reach internal NTP servers.
  - Drift usually <1s to a few seconds.
- Pattern B: Bastion has time, cluster nodes don’t
  - Bastion can reach time source; nodes isolated.
  - Installer may run on bastion (good) but workloads may validate on nodes later (risk if we ever validate inside cluster).
- Pattern C: No network time; controlled manual changes
  - The hardest case; must support a safe, auditable workflow.


3) Why time matters for our verification
3.1 Verification checks that may involve time
Depending on the final envelope, we may rely on one or more of:
- Certificate validity (X.509 notBefore/notAfter) if we ship a cert chain with the signature.
- Signature metadata validity (envelope "issued_at" / "expires_at").
- Downgrade/replay protections tied to versions/build timestamps.

3.2 What can go wrong
- If verifier time is behind, anything "recently issued" can appear "not yet valid".
- If verifier time is ahead, older bundles may appear expired prematurely.
- If we use a short validity period (e.g., 30–90 days) we make time skew and "install later" scenarios worse for air-gapped customers.
- If we use a very long validity period (years) we reduce the security benefit of expiry and key rotation pressure, but improve offline usability.

3.3 Threat model tie-in
- Time confusion can be exploited if we accept arbitrary "signing time" from untrusted input.
- However, strict time enforcement can cause operational outages (denial of service) when clocks are wrong.
- We need a balanced approach: fail closed for integrity; be deliberate about time-based policy so legitimate installs don’t get bricked by clock drift.


4) Recommendation: How to handle time in offline bundle verification
4.1 Principles
- Integrity must fail closed: manifest hash mismatch, signature mismatch, unknown key -> block by default.
- Time-based failures should be: (a) diagnosable, (b) remediable without internet, and (c) policy-controllable.
- Avoid requiring "accurate time" for core integrity checks unless absolutely necessary.

4.2 Suggested validity strategy (default)
- Keep cryptographic validity independent of local time when feasible.
  - Prefer detached signatures over schemes that require online freshness checks.
- If we must include time validity:
  - Use a long validity window for the signer certificate/envelope (e.g., 3–5 years) to avoid routine expiry blocking air-gapped installs.
  - Combine long validity with:
    - key rotation via trust store (multiple allowed keys)
    - revocation via out-of-band trust store updates (see separate key rotation docs)
- Treat time checks as policy gates, not the primary assurance mechanism.

4.3 Proposed installer behavior (preflight)
- Always verify:
  - manifest canonicalization + hash list
  - signature over canonical manifest
  - signing key is in trust store
- Then evaluate time-related checks:
  - If signature/cert is "not yet valid" or "expired":
    - Default: block install (because we cannot distinguish skew from actual invalidity) BUT with a targeted message and an explicit diagnostic flow.
    - Allow an admin-only diagnostic override to supply a reference time (or to ignore validity bounds) with loud audit warnings.

4.4 Diagnostic-only time override (strongly recommended)
- Add a flag to installer preflight:
  - --time-override=RFC3339 (or epoch)
  - Purpose: allow Support/SE to prove the bundle is valid and that time skew is the cause, without changing system clock.
  - Not intended for long-term operation; should emit an audit event and big console warnings.
- Alternative: --ignore-validity-window (even riskier). Prefer an explicit time override because it leaves a paper trail of what time was used.

4.5 Break-glass (rare)
- If customer cannot resolve time and must proceed:
  - require break-glass flag (policy-gated) + explicit confirmation prompt + local audit log entry containing:
    - bundle build id
    - manifest digest
    - reason code
    - operator identity (if available)
    - timestamp as observed (even if wrong) + override time if used
  - recommended approval path documented separately (see break-glass policy draft).


5) UX requirements: error messages that work offline
What Support needs in the error output (copy/paste friendly):
- Observed system time (UTC) on the machine running installer.
- Signature envelope validity bounds (notBefore/notAfter or issued_at/expires_at) in UTC.
- Drift estimate if we can compute it (only if we have a trusted reference time; we usually don’t).
- Suggested next steps:
  1) Verify system time/RTC settings
  2) Check if NTP/chrony is configured and reachable
  3) If policy allows, run preflight with --time-override to confirm skew
  4) If still blocked, collect logs + run bundle inspect


6) Proposed defaults (to validate with Security + Release Eng)
- Envelope/cert validity duration: 3–5 years
  - Rationale: air-gapped upgrades may lag by quarters; also time skew issues become less frequent when notBefore is far enough in the past.
- Set notBefore/issued_at with a safety backdate buffer at signing time (e.g., -24h)
  - Rationale: prevents "not yet valid" due to small skew.
  - Security note: backdating increases acceptance window by that amount; 24h seems reasonable. 7d feels too permissive.
- Installer allowed time skew tolerance before calling it out explicitly: 5 minutes (warning only), 30 minutes (strong warning), >24h (treat as likely misconfigured).
  - Important: this is messaging only unless we have a trusted time source.


7) NTP/PTP details: what to tell customers / what to ask in discovery
7.1 Discovery questions (SE/PS checklist)
- Do you have internal NTP servers? If yes: addresses, allowed clients, firewall rules.
- Are nodes allowed UDP/123 to those servers?
- Is chrony/ntpd allowed on the install host and cluster nodes?
- Is time managed via AD Windows time? Is Linux joined to domain or using separate NTP?
- Is PTP in use? If yes: is it only for certain NICs/hosts?
- Is there a formal procedure for manual time setting and audit?

7.2 Common Linux implementation notes
- chrony is common in locked-down environments (better at handling intermittent connectivity).
- Some hardened baselines disable all NTP services.
- VMs may require enabling host-guest time sync (but security teams sometimes disable this).

7.3 What we should document (docs/runbook)
- Minimum expectation: system time must be "approximately correct" to validate signer validity checks unless using time override.
- Provide commands:
  - date -u
  - timedatectl
  - chronyc tracking / sources
  - ntpq -p (if ntpd)
- Provide guidance on "preferred time architecture" (internal NTP) but keep it optional.


8) Open questions / decisions needed
- Do we enforce time validity at all in v1, or do we rely only on key trust + signature integrity and skip notBefore/notAfter checks?
  - Pro: fewer operational failures.
  - Con: loses defense-in-depth around stale credentials.
- If we keep time validity, can we avoid x509 certificate validity checks by using an Ed25519 public key in a trust store + envelope fields?
  - That shifts time checks from X.509 to our envelope; still needs local time if we enforce expires_at.
- Where should we document and gate --time-override vs break-glass?
  - Preference: time override allowed for preflight/diagnostics; break-glass needed to proceed with install.


9) Action items (as of last edit)
- [Ethan] Align installer behavior with Security on time override + audit logging requirements.
- [Hanae] Confirm feasibility of backdating notBefore/issued_at in signing pipeline (and what evidence we need for auditors).
- [Sean] Update day-2 runbook with time skew troubleshooting steps + example outputs.
- [Fatima] Add discovery questions to air-gapped customer requirements library.
- [Marcus] Confirm threat model stance: acceptable tolerances and how to message customers.


Appendix A: Example troubleshooting flow (Support)
1) Customer runs: redwood-private-installer bundle inspect /path/to/bundle --verify
2) If error indicates validity window:
   - collect output showing system time + notBefore/notAfter
3) Ask customer to run:
   - redwood-private-installer preflight --bundle /path/to/bundle --time-override 2026-01-15T12:00:00Z
4) If succeeds with override:
   - conclude time skew
   - recommend internal NTP or manual correction procedure
5) If still fails:
   - treat as real signature issue (tampering, wrong bundle, wrong trust store)

Appendix B: Notes from internal threads
- Error taxonomy thread: we need distinct codes for NOT_YET_VALID vs EXPIRED vs UNKNOWN_KEY vs SIG_INVALID.
- Release thread: long-lived keys + trust store rotation is the core mechanism; expiry should not be the main rotation lever for air-gapped.

Appendix C: References
- ADR-0147: Offline artifact signing scheme (canonicalization + envelope)
- Threat model: tampering + replay + downgrade; includes time skew as an operational risk
- PR-24961: initial fix for "not yet valid" failures; this doc is intended to guide final policy

title: Cross-team handshake inventory and lightweight tracker - Jan 2026
preview:
Purpose: quick inventory of cross-team handoffs, one-off asks, and small decisions that need owner/time estimates. Lightweight—use this to escalates blockers and to make sure small dependencies don't fall through cracks.

Summary notes:
- Snapshot date: 2026-01-12 (last scrub 2026-01-14).
- Quick counts: 12 items total, 3 blocked, 4 awaiting sign-off, 5 on-boarding/ops work.
- Primary escalation channel: #ops-handshakes and weekly 15-min sync Fridays at 09:30 PST.

Table (columns): ID | Topic / short description | Initiator | Responsible | Stakeholders | Due | Status | Blockers / notes | Quick next step | Slack thread / ticket

1 | Dedicated region config for ACME pilot — confirm routing to eu-west | Maya Chen | Platform Infra (Liam) | Sales (Priya), SE team | 2026-01-20 | in_progress | Need region IP whitelist from customer; infra waiting on network team | Ask customer for prod CIDR by 2026-01-15, set tentative mapping | #acme-pilot / JIRA-3247

title: Larkspur Telemed onprem sizing and TF cutover notes
preview:
Overview:

Quick working notes for Larkspur Telemed private deployment proof-of-concept and cutover. This doc captures sizing assumptions, a proposed phased upgrade/cutover plan, Terraform module callouts, networking/KMS considerations, and open questions. Intended audience: Solutions eng + infra + Larkspur infra lead (Ben K.).

Customer problem summary:
- Larkspur needs low-latency on-prem inference for clinical triage (chat + structured output) with strict PHI controls (no model telemetry offsite).
- Expected steady-state: 120 QPS text-gen (95th latency target < 120ms for short prompts), peak bursts up to 350 QPS for scheduled clinic hours.
- Throughput must support multi-model fallback (base model + smaller fallback for cost).

Constraints & hard requirements:
- Air-gapped deployment option required for production environment; control plane managed in customer VPC via private connectivity (VPN/Direct Connect equivalent).
- FIPS-compliant KMS/HSM for key storage.
- Maintenance window: weekday 02:00-04:00 local time; rolling updates preferred.

Sizing assumptions (working):
- Model mix: two served variants: 'telemed-30b-q8' (primary) and 'telemed-7b-q4' (fallback). Primary used ~85% of requests during clinic hours, fallback used for degraded capacity or cheaper routings.
- Model token distribution: median 64 tokens/user, 95th 280 tokens.
- Serving instance: NVIDIA A10G equivalent for cost-efficient 30B q8 inference; H100 is optional for latency-critical pilot tests.
- Node config (initial cluster): 6 x A10G nodes with 80GB GPU RAM (assume 2 hosts reserved as warm spares). Estimated sustained throughput per node: ~25 QPS (mixed seq lengths) for primary model with batching configured. Total sustained ~150 QPS.
- Redis KV prefix cache: 3-primary/2-replica cluster; 128GB total memory (estimate).
- NVMe local scratch for model repo and local cache: 1TB per node (encrypted).
- CPU/memory for host: 64 vCPU, 512GB RAM per node to account for model process + OS + observability agents.

Network and VPC layout notes:
- VPC design: 3 AZs, each AZ has 2 public subnets (load balancer) and 3 private subnets (serving nodes, infra).
- Private control plane endpoints (Redwood control-plane connector) via customer-managed proxy in a management subnet. No internet egress for inference hosts.
- NAT: Only for controlled outbound to patch repository via signed proxy; otherwise blocked.
- Peering/express: customer prefers direct private link to monitoring ingestion (SQS-like) — design to use SFTP push at night for logs to auditing cluster to avoid live egress.

KMS/HSM + secrets:
- Integration with customer's Thales/KMS recommended; Redwood runtime requires envelope encryption keys-- propose KMS key alias redwood/inference/larkspur-prod.
- HSM for signing image attestations in cutover. Terraform must not store plaintext secrets in state; state backend: S3-compatible with server-side encryption + IAM policies scoped to service account.

Terraform module notes (practical snippets & callouts):
- modules/network: expects variables: az_count, private_subnet_cidr_list, mgmt_subnet_cidr, enable_flow_logs. Add tunable mtu and mtu exceptions for SGX islands.
- modules/serving_pool: variables: instance_type, gpu_count_per_host, node_pool_size, tf_gpu_image_id, local_nvme_size. Outputs: asg_name, instance_profiles.
- modules/kms_integration: wrapper to bind customer KMS key to service principal; must conditionally create ephemeral service token resource when HSM present (use var.use_hsm boolean).
- modules/storage: create encrypted object bucket (model-repo), lifecycle rules to purge staging artifacts after 14d. Must enable versioning for safe rollbacks.
- modules/monitoring: push metrics to customer prometheus stack via remote_write; include firewall rules only to prometheus collector IPs.

Terraform gotchas we've observed in similar customers:
- Avoid using dynamic block for instance profiles in modules/serving_pool; it complicates state when ASG scaling policies are updated by SRE. Prefer explicit count-based submodules per AZ.
- State locking: customer's backend doesn't have native DynamoDB locking; use remote S3 with advisory locking via consul or add an admin step to coordinate TF applies for major changes.
- Provider versions: pin aws provider to 5.x in root module. Nonsensical provider upgrades have broken rolling-recreate strategies in past.

Autoscaling and runtime policies:
- Recommend proactive autoscaling with schedule-based scale-up for clinic hours + dynamic scale based on GPU utilization and queue length.
- Warm-pool: keep 1 host warm per AZ during business hours.
- Fallback policy: when queue > 400 requests or average latency exceeds 200ms for 3 consecutive minutes, route new sessions to 7B fallback or to hosted fallback (if customer allows egress).

Cutover/Upgrade plan (Phased):
Phase 0 — Lab PoC (2 weeks):
- Stand up 1 AZ mini-cluster (2 A10G nodes) inside customer dev VPC. Validate model load, KV caching, and streaming responses.
- Deliver a short runbook and bench numbers.

Phase 1 — Pilot (4 weeks):
- Expand to 2 AZs (4 nodes), enable autoscaler, deploy ingress LB with sticky sessions for session affinity.
- Run synthetic traffic and small subset of real traffic (5% traffic mirroring).
- Validate KMS sign/verify flows, audit logging to customer SIEM.

Phase 2 — Production cutover (3 days window):
- Pre-cutover: replicate model repo to prod bucket, pre-warm cache, freeze schema changes.
- Day 1: Turn on traffic routing by weight (gradual 10% -> 50% -> 100% over hours) with rollback gates every 30 minutes.
- Day 2: Monitoring and full traffic validation; if errors > 1% or P95 latency > 200ms, rollback to hosted endpoint or fallback group.

Phase 3 — Post-cutover hardening (2 weeks):
- Run disaster recovery drills: AZ failover, node reboot, KMS key rotation test.
- Formalize runbook and ops playbooks.

Rollback plan (quick):
- Maintain DNS TTLs low (30s) for LB entrypoint for quick re-pointing.
- Keep hosted fallback route ready; pre-configure routing policies to flip to fallback by flipping a single feature flag in control plane.
- Snapshot TF state and AMIs before mass-replace operations.

Risk matrix (top items):
- Risk: KV cache thrash under burst -> Mitigation: increase cache memory and tune prefix eviction policy; pre-warm hot keys.
- Risk: Network egress policy blocks telemetry -> Mitigation: provide offline log export path and enable periodic signed uploads.
- Risk: Terraform drift during manual ops -> Mitigation: tag immutable resources and disallow manual changes to ASG tags via policy.

Open questions (must resolve before prod cutover):
- Does customer allow any outbound connections to Redwood-managed control plane for health checks? (Owner: Ben K., ticket JIRA-INF-3482)
- Confirm preferred HSM vendor and KMS key rotation cadence. (Owner: Sofia M.)
- SLA expectations for onprem nodes (what uptime percentage will customer contract require?). (Owner: Ankit R.)

Short list of immediate TODOs / action owners:
- Ankit: Produce final TF module interface doc that maps customer inputs to variables (due 2025-11-20). (JIRA-INF-3520)
- Priya: Run two bench runs on A10G vs H100 in lab to validate latency delta for 30B q8 (due 2025-11-14).
- Maya (security reviewer): Review KMS/HSM steps and approve network egress exceptions (due 2025-11-17).
- Ethan: Draft cutover rollback playbook and DNS TTL change script (due 2025-11-10).

Notes to self / rough ideas:
- Consider creating a small TF module 'serving_warm_pool' that manages warm instances separately from ASG to avoid conflict with autoscaler.
- Evaluate NVLink benefits vs cost for multi-GPU hosting of 30B models — likely unnecessary for q8 but test in pilot.
- Add a nightly job to snapshot local NVMe model caches to central encrypted bucket to speed restarts.

Appendix: quick bench numbers from lab PoC (raw):
- 2 x A10G hosts, batch size 4, median latency 95ms, P95 210ms for 64-token median workloads.
- 1 x H100 host, batch size 8, median latency 58ms, P95 120ms.
- Memory footprint primary model (quantized): ~28GB GPU FP footprint + 6GB CPU pinned memory for kv cache per model shard.

If anything in here should be elevated into a formal proposal doc for the customer, flag and we'll convert to a one-pager with costs and SOW language. This is intentionally informal — fill in with more bench runs and TF variable name proposals in the next pass.

-- end --

title: Cross-region fallback safety margin scenarios and activation checklist — Aug 2026
preview:
Purpose:
This working sheet captures a set of distinct cross-region fallback and safety-margin scenarios for GPU fleet capacity. The goal is pragmatic: drive a single operational rubric for when to (a) activate region-to-region failover, (b) bring up reserve pools, and (c) trigger customer-visible routing/degradation policies. This doc blends simple threshold rules with Monte Carlo–style scenario outcomes and a short activation checklist for on-call and SRE operators.

Executive summary (short):
- We recommend holding an operational buffer equivalent to 8–12% of steady-state peak GPU slots per cloud region as the baseline safety margin.
- For multi-region DR where cross-region failover is required, plan for an absorbable burst of up to 1.8x regional peak for up to 2 hours without service degradation by leveraging prioritized pre-warming + elastic burst pools.
- Activation is tiered: automated soft-failover at 95% regional util for non-critical routes; manual hard-failover if region-level outage > 3 availability zones or sustained > 10 minutes of 99.9th-percentile latency spikes.

Assumptions and definitions used in scenarios:
- GPU slot: 1 slot ≈ one p3.2xlarge-equivalent inference container. We use normalized slot units to compare GPU type families across clouds.
- Peak: observed 95th percentile 30-day concurrent slot usage.
- Safety margin: spare slots kept unallocated in a region for immediate absorbable demand.
- Reserve pool: dedicated pre-provisioned slots (can be cross-region) allocated for DR/failover activation.
- Elastic burst pool: warm nodes not attached to production routing, can be brought up in minutes via autoscaling runbooks and pre-pulled images.
- Acceptable degradation: routing teardown to L3 model variant (smaller model) for non-enterprise tiers; enterprise routes must maintain baseline model but can accept extended latency up to +150ms.

Scenario matrix (summary outputs):
- Scenario A: Short AZ degradation (one AZ goes down for 30–45 min).
  - Likely impact: regional capacity drops ~30%.
  - Recommended response: shift intra-region traffic to remaining AZs + enable AZ affinity relaxation; do NOT activate cross-region reserves unless remaining capacity < 85% of allocations.
  - Exercise result (simulation): with 10% safety margin, 92% of requests served within SLO with L3 degradation on 12% of non-critical traffic.

- Scenario B: Region-wide network partition (traffic rerouting possible but higher latency; partial control plane available).
  - Likely impact: effective capacity 40–60% for 45–180 min depending on cloud.
  - Recommended response: trigger Tier-1 manual incident, activate cross-region reserve pool to absorb 30% of critical routes. Begin progressive DNS cutover for enterprise customers after 10 minutes if metrics trending negative.
  - Exercise result: need 1.3x of baseline reserve capacity in neighboring region to keep enterprise routes within SLO for 90 minutes.

- Scenario C: Full region outage (cloud provider region down).
  - Likely impact: 100% capacity loss for region; customer routing must be redirected.
  - Recommended response: activate hard failover runbook; use pre-provisioned dedicated reserve pools in two partner regions (primary failover and secondary) and run accelerated model spin-up. Throttle non-critical traffic immediately.
  - Exercise result: with 12% safety margins + 40% reserved cross-region capacity, system maintains enterprise-level models for 60 minutes; non-critical suffers degradation unless additional burst capacity is accepted (costly).

Monte Carlo summary (500 runs) — quick notes:
- Inputs: historical hourly demand, random correlated AZ failures, lead time to spin-up (uniform 2–12 min for elastic burst, 6–25 min for model cold-start on new GPU types), cross-region network latency multipliers.
- Outcome: 8% safety margin yields 87% of runs where critical SLOs preserved without cross-region activation.
- Outcome: 12% safety margin + a reserve pool equivalent to 25% regional peak yields 96% success across runs for outages <= 120 minutes.
- Diminishing returns beyond 15% margin — cost per avoided-minute increases sharply.

Operational playbook (step-by-step condensed):
1) Detection and pre-eval (automated):
   - Alert triggers: region_utilization > 90% sustained for 5m; AZ_loss detected; control-plane heartbeats lost > 30s.
   - Automated checks: routing health, model queue lengths, 99th percentile latency, percent of requests served from cache.
2) Soft actions (automated):
   - Relax AZ affinity; enable request-level graceful degradation for non-critical tokens (L3 fallback models); shed best-effort background jobs.
   - Publish incident marker and update internal routing dashboard.
3) Manual escalation (on-call SRE + infra lead):
   - Decide whether to recruit cross-region reserve: check reserve pool availability and cost-bucket.
   - If decision = yes: run cross-region activation script (ops/activate-reserve.sh) that flips route weights by priority list and starts pre-warmed containers in target region.
4) Hard failover (manual):
   - Criteria: region down OR sustained SLO breaches after 10 minutes of soft actions.
   - Actions: execute DNS cutover plan for enterprise customers (staged), enable rate limits for non-enterprise traffic, and invoke customer comms template.
5) Post-incident:
   - Triage: collect model-level latency histograms, costing delta, and missed-SLO counts.
   - Retrospective: update threshold parameters and runbook timing.

Activation checklist (quick for pager):
- [ ] Confirm region metrics: utilization, AZ failures, control-plane errors.
- [ ] Verify reserve pool capacity and hitless spin-up scripts exist for target region.
- [ ] Notify Product/SRE: enterprise customer list and escalation contacts posted.
- [ ] Execute traffic redistribution in 10% increments (monitor error and latency).
- [ ] If spinning up new model variants, validate KV cache warming plan to avoid cold-start spikes.
- [ ] Keep finance on standby if activation crosses > $10k/hr burn threshold.

Reserve sizing heuristics (practical rule-of-thumb):
- Baseline safety margin (operational buffer): 8–12% of regional peak.
- Short-term reserve pool for cross-region failover: 20–35% of regional peak (sized smaller when symmetric regions available).
- Long-duration reserve (sustained DR contract): contract for 0.5x of peak in a partner region if RTO expectations are > 4 hours.

Cost vs reliability tradeoff (short note):
- Holding a 12% safety margin across 3 major regions increases idle GPU-hour cost by ~9–13% in modeling runs; but reduces cross-region activation frequency by ~60%.
- Leaner strategy (8% margin + rapid elastic burst) reduces baseline cost but increases customer-facing failover risk for incidents > 40 minutes.

Open questions & decisions needed by leadership:
- Acceptable enterprise exposure window before customer-visible routing (default suggested: 10 minutes).
- Budget bucket for on-demand burst beyond reserved pools (threshold and approval flow).
- Whether to convert part of the elastic burst pool into paid reserved capacity to lower cold-start time variance.

Appendix: quick commands and runbook pointers (refs)
- ops/activate-reserve.sh — script to flip route weights and start pre-warmed instances.
- ops/pre-warm-cache.py — helper to warm model KV caches across target nodes.
- dashboards: infra/region-capacity and infra/failover-playbook (links in linked artifacts).
- Suggested drills: quarterly table-top and biannual full failover exercise covering minimum 60-min sustained outage.

Notes to reviewers (internal):
- This is a working doc: please add concrete numbers from the latest cloud invoices (Dec 2025) and the most recent regional utilization CSV (s3://redwood-infra/metrics/region-usage-2025.csv).
- Priority for next revision: integrate latest synthetic load tests (ticket JIRA-ENGINFRA-4599) and update spin-up latency distributions for the new A800-based pool.

End of document.

- We recommend holding an operational buffer equivalent to 8–12% of steady-state peak GPU slots per cloud region as the baseline safety margin.
- For multi-region DR where cross-region failover is required, plan for an absorbable burst of up to 1.8x regional peak for up to 2 hours without service degradation by leveraging prioritized pre-warming + elastic burst pools.
- Activation is tiered: automated soft-failover at 95% regional util for non-critical routes; manual hard-failover if region-level outage > 3 availability zones or sustained > 10 minutes of 99.9th-percentile latency spikes.

Assumptions and definitions used in scenarios:
- GPU slot: 1 slot ≈ one p3.2xlarge-equivalent inference container. We use normalized slot units to compare GPU type families across clouds.
- Peak: observed 95th percentile 30-day concurrent slot usage.
- Safety margin: spare slots kept unallocated in a region for immediate absorbable demand.
- Reserve pool: dedicated pre-provisioned slots (can be cross-region) allocated for DR/failover activation.
- Elastic burst pool: warm nodes not attached to production routing, can be brought up in minutes via autoscaling runbooks and pre-pulled images.
- Acceptable degradation: routing teardown to L3 model variant (smaller model) for non-enterprise tiers; enterprise routes must maintain baseline model but can accept extended latency up to +150ms.

Scenario matrix (summary outputs):
- Scenario A: Short AZ degradation (one AZ goes down for 30–45 min).
  - Likely impact: regional capacity drops ~30%.
  - Recommended response: shift intra-region traffic to remaining AZs + enable AZ affinity relaxation; do NOT activate cross-region reserves unless remaining capacity < 85% of allocations.
  - Exercise result (simulation): with 10% safety margin, 92% of requests served within SLO with L3 degradation on 12% of non-critical traffic.

- Scenario B: Region-wide network partition (traffic rerouting possible but higher latency; partial control plane available).
  - Likely impact: effective capacity 40–60% for 45–180 min depending on cloud.
  - Recommended response: trigger Tier-1 manual incident, activate cross-region reserve pool to absorb 30% of critical routes. Begin progressive DNS cutover for enterprise customers after 10 minutes if metrics trending negative.
  - Exercise result: need 1.3x of baseline reserve capacity in neighboring region to keep enterprise routes within SLO for 90 minutes.

- Scenario C: Full region outage (cloud provider region down).
  - Likely impact: 100% capacity loss for region; customer routing must be redirected.
  - Recommended response: activate hard failover runbook; use pre-provisioned dedicated reserve pools in two partner regions (primary failover and secondary) and run accelerated model spin-up. Throttle non-critical traffic immediately.
  - Exercise result: with 12% safety margins + 40% reserved cross-region capacity, system maintains enterprise-level models for 60 minutes; non-critical suffers degradation unless additional burst capacity is accepted (costly).

Monte Carlo summary (500 runs) — quick notes:
- Inputs: historical hourly demand, random correlated AZ failures, lead time to spin-up (uniform 2–12 min for elastic burst, 6–25 min for model cold-start on new GPU types), cross-region network latency multipliers.
- Outcome: 8% safety margin yields 87% of runs where critical SLOs preserved without cross-region activation.
- Outcome: 12% safety margin + a reserve pool equivalent to 25% regional peak yields 96% success across runs for outages <= 120 minutes.
- Diminishing returns beyond 15% margin — cost per avoided-minute increases sharply.

Operational playbook (step-by-step condensed):
1) Detection and pre-eval (automated):
   - Alert triggers: region_utilization > 90% sustained for 5m; AZ_loss detected; control-plane heartbeats lost > 30s.
   - Automated checks: routing health, model queue lengths, 99th percentile latency, percent of requests served from cache.
2) Soft actions (automated):
   - Relax AZ affinity; enable request-level graceful degradation for non-critical tokens (L3 fallback models); shed best-effort background jobs.
   - Publish incident marker and update internal routing dashboard.
3) Manual escalation (on-call SRE + infra lead):
   - Decide whether to recruit cross-region reserve: check reserve pool availability and cost-bucket.
   - If decision = yes: run cross-region activation script (ops/activate-reserve.sh) that flips route weights by priority list and starts pre-warmed containers in target region.
4) Hard failover (manual):
   - Criteria: region down OR sustained SLO breaches after 10 minutes of soft actions.
   - Actions: execute DNS cutover plan for enterprise customers (staged), enable rate limits for non-enterprise traffic, and invoke customer comms template.
5) Post-incident:
   - Triage: collect model-level latency histograms, costing delta, and missed-SLO counts.
   - Retrospective: update threshold parameters and runbook timing.

Activation checklist (quick for pager):
- [ ] Confirm region metrics: utilization, AZ failures, control-plane errors.
- [ ] Verify reserve pool capacity and hitless spin-up scripts exist for target region.
- [ ] Notify Product/SRE: enterprise customer list and escalation contacts posted.
- [ ] Execute traffic redistribution in 10% increments (monitor error and latency).
- [ ] If spinning up new model variants, validate KV cache warming plan to avoid cold-start spikes.
- [ ] Keep finance on standby if activation crosses > $10k/hr burn threshold.

Reserve sizing heuristics (practical rule-of-thumb):
- Baseline safety margin (operational buffer): 8–12% of regional peak.
- Short-term reserve pool for cross-region failover: 20–35% of regional peak (sized smaller when symmetric regions available).
- Long-duration reserve (sustained DR contract): contract for 0.5x of peak in a partner region if RTO expectations are > 4 hours.

Cost vs reliability tradeoff (short note):
- Holding a 12% safety margin across 3 major regions increases idle GPU-hour cost by ~9–13% in modeling runs; but reduces cross-region activation frequency by ~60%.
- Leaner strategy (8% margin + rapid elastic burst) reduces baseline cost but increases customer-facing failover risk for incidents > 40 minutes.

Open questions & decisions needed by leadership:
- Acceptable enterprise exposure window before customer-visible routing (default suggested: 10 minutes).
- Budget bucket for on-demand burst beyond reserved pools (threshold and approval flow).
- Whether to convert part of the elastic burst pool into paid reserved capacity to lower cold-start time variance.

Appendix: quick commands and runbook pointers (refs)
- ops/activate-reserve.sh — script to flip route weights and start pre-warmed instances.
- ops/pre-warm-cache.py — helper to warm model KV caches across target nodes.
- dashboards: infra/region-capacity and infra/failover-playbook (links in linked artifacts).
- Suggested drills: quarterly table-top and biannual full failover exercise covering minimum 60-min sustained outage.

Notes to reviewers (internal):
- This is a working doc: please add concrete numbers from the latest cloud invoices (Dec 2025) and the most recent regional utilization CSV (s3://redwood-infra/metrics/region-usage-2025.csv).
- Priority for next revision: integrate latest synthetic load tests (ticket JIRA-ENGINFRA-4599) and update spin-up latency distributions for the new A800-based pool.

End of document.

title: Owner mapping config schema (control plane) — draft v0.3
preview:
Owner mapping config schema (control plane) — draft v0.3
Owner: Ethan Park (Platform)
Last updated: 2026-01-06

Context
- Reliability Program deliverable: “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping” requires a single source of truth that maps (incident_type, subtype) -> primary owner team, plus secondary/consulted teams and runbook links.
- This config must be:
  - Versioned + auditable (who changed what, when)
  - Safe to roll out/roll back (canary + validation)
  - Low-cardinality and aligned to taxonomy v1 (avoid freeform)
  - Usable by incident-bot, alert routing, Console/ops views

Goals
1) Define config schema: types/subtypes -> owners + metadata
2) Define validation rules and review workflow
3) Define rollout mechanics (staging/canary/prod, pinning, emergency override)

Non-goals (v1)
- Modeling complex multi-owner logic (ex: “depends on region/provider”). We will pick a single primary owner and capture others as secondary/consulted.
- Auto-classification logic (that belongs in incident-bot heuristics); this config is for “given a classification, resolve owners + links”.
- Replacing on-call rotations / paging policies. The config is an input into paging, not the entire policy engine.


1) Schema proposal
This document uses JSON-ish pseudocode for clarity. The stored representation can be JSON or YAML; control plane should treat it as data (not code).

Top-level
- mapping_version: semver-ish version string for the dataset (NOT the control plane app version)
- taxonomy_version: reference string (ex: “v1”) so we can roll taxonomy revisions without ambiguity
- updated_by: optional; control plane will record actual actor in audit log
- entries: list of mapping entries; each entry is keyed by incident_type + optional subtype

Proposed schema (pseudocode)

mapping_version: "0.3.0"
taxonomy_version: "v1"
description: "Owner mapping for incident taxonomy v1"
entries: [
  {
    key: {
      incident_type: "availability"
      incident_subtype: "5xx-spike"          // optional
      customer_tier: "any"                   // optional (any|hosted|dedicated|private)
    }
    owner: {
      team: "eng-serving-runtime"            // primary accountable team
      service: "serving-gateway"             // optional: canonical service/system
      escalation_channel: "#eng-runtime-oncall" // optional convenience
    }
    secondary_teams: ["eng-sre", "eng-platform"],   // optional
    consulted_teams: ["customer-success"],           // optional
    runbooks: [
      {
        label: "Overload / 5xx runbook"
        url: "https://confluence.redwood.example/..."
      }
    ],
    dashboards: [
      {
        label: "Request errors (by model/route)"
        url: "https://grafana.redwood.example/d/..."
      }
    ],
    notes: "Primary if 5xx originates at gateway/runtime. If upstream auth rejects, reclassify auth.*",
    routing_hints: {
      pager_target: "primary"                // (primary|sre|none) optional hint for paging integration
    },
    status: "active"                          // (active|deprecated)
    effective_at: "2026-01-08"                // optional future activation
  }
]

Fields and constraints (proposed)
A) key
- incident_type (required)
  - Must be allowlisted to taxonomy v1 top-level types.
  - String format: lowercase-kebab-case.
- incident_subtype (optional)
  - Must be allowlisted for the given incident_type.
- customer_tier (optional)
  - Purpose: carve-outs for Private-specific ownership (ex: private-connectivity.*)
  - Allowed values: any | hosted | dedicated | private
  - Default: any

B) owner (required)
- team (required)
  - Must be allowlisted from a canonical set of internal team identifiers.
  - This is the “single throat to choke” for reporting.
- service (optional)
  - Canonical service/system string (control plane will validate against known service registry if available; otherwise allowlist in config).
- escalation_channel (optional)
  - Slack channel name; treated as convenience metadata (not relied upon for correctness).

C) secondary_teams / consulted_teams (optional)
- Purpose: make cross-team collaboration explicit without diluting ownership.
- secondary_teams: expected to actively help mitigate
- consulted_teams: may be consulted; optional
- Both fields are informational for v1; incident commander still decides who to pull in.

D) runbooks / dashboards (optional)
- A list of links (label + URL)
- Validation: URL must be https, label non-empty.

E) notes (optional)
- Freeform text for edge cases.
- Not used for metrics labels (avoid cardinality explosion).

F) routing_hints (optional)
- Intentionally small surface area for now.
- We should avoid embedding paging policy here; keep this as a minimal override knob.

G) status + effective_at (optional)
- status: active | deprecated
- effective_at: ISO date string; used for scheduled changes without needing exact time.
- Deprecated entries remain for historical resolution/backfill.


2) Owner resolution behavior (consumer contract)
Consumers (incident-bot, alert router, Console) will call a single endpoint:
resolve_owner(incident_type, incident_subtype?, customer_tier?) -> owner + metadata

Resolution order
1) Exact match on (type, subtype, tier)
2) Exact match on (type, subtype, any)
3) Match on (type, tier) with subtype omitted in config
4) Match on (type, any) with subtype omitted
5) If no match:
   - owner.team = "eng-sre" (fallback)
   - add resolution_status = "unmapped"
   - emit metric + log + alert (see below)

Important: subtype is optional, but if provided by the incident record and mapping exists, we should prefer it.

Output should include
- owner.team, owner.service (if any), escalation channel (if any)
- link arrays (runbooks, dashboards)
- resolution_status: mapped | fallback | unmapped
- mapping_version + taxonomy_version returned for auditability


3) Validation rules (control plane enforced)
Hard validation (reject config update)
- mapping_version must parse as x.y.z (strict) and must be greater than current version unless “emergency_override=true” (see rollout notes)
- taxonomy_version must be recognized (v1 for now)
- entries must be unique by (incident_type, incident_subtype, customer_tier)
- incident_type must be in allowlist
- incident_subtype must be in allowlist for that type (if present)
- customer_tier must be one of any|hosted|dedicated|private
- owner.team must be in team allowlist
- secondary_teams / consulted_teams must be in team allowlist; must not include owner.team
- URLs must be https; must not exceed 2KB; max 10 runbooks + 10 dashboards per entry

Soft validation (accept config update but warn + log)
- Missing runbook links for high-frequency incident types (based on last 90d incident analytics)
- Missing escalation_channel
- Excessive use of tier-specific overrides (encourage generic defaults)
- Entries with notes > 2k chars

Cardinality guardrails (non-negotiable)
- This config MUST NOT define new labels for metrics.
- Only incident_type and incident_subtype from the taxonomy allowlist are permitted.
- “Custom subtypes” are not allowed.

Audit + traceability
- Control plane records:
  - actor (user/service)
  - diff summary (# entries added/removed/changed)
  - previous mapping_version -> new mapping_version
  - approval reference (PR link or ticket)


4) Proposed allowlists (placeholders)
These should match the taxonomy v1 Confluence page and be duplicated here for validation.

Top-level incident types (draft)
- availability
- latency
- streaming
- routing-fallback
- capacity-fleet
- control-plane
- auth-rbac
- telemetry-blindness
- private-connectivity

Examples of subtypes (draft, incomplete)
- availability: 5xx-spike, overload, dependency-outage
- latency: p95-regression, tail-latency, rollout-regression
- streaming: disconnects, stalled-streams
- routing-fallback: fallback-stuck, wrong-target, policy-misconfig, health-signal-bad
- capacity-fleet: gpu-exhaustion, provisioning-lag, regional-shortage
- control-plane: api-outage, config-write-failures, rollout-bad
- auth-rbac: token-validation, rbac-policy, key-rotation
- telemetry-blindness: metrics-gap, logging-pipeline, tracing-outage
- private-connectivity: dns, tls, vpc-routing, customer-firewall

NOTE: These names are placeholders; do not treat as final taxonomy. Validation list should be sourced from the taxonomy v1 definitions once ratified.


5) Example entries (more realistic)

Example: routing fallback misbehavior
key.incident_type = "routing-fallback"
key.incident_subtype = "wrong-target"
owner.team = "eng-platform" (Smart Routing)
secondary_teams = ["eng-infra"]
consulted_teams = ["eng-sre"]
runbooks = ["Routing fallback runbook", "Region failover checklist"]
notes = "If root cause is insufficient capacity (no healthy pools), reclassify capacity-fleet.gpu-exhaustion and owner becomes eng-infra. Keep routing-fallback as contributing factor."

Example: dedicated burst leading to 429s
key.incident_type = "availability"
key.incident_subtype = "overload"
key.customer_tier = "dedicated"
owner.team = "eng-infra" (Fleet)
secondary_teams = ["eng-platform"]
notes = "If 429 is quota enforcement (expected), classify auth-rbac/rate-limit-policy instead and owner=Platform Control Plane."

Example: private DNS/TLS errors after upgrade
key.incident_type = "private-connectivity"
key.incident_subtype = "tls"
key.customer_tier = "private"
owner.team = "eng-infra" (Private networking)
secondary_teams = ["security-and-compliance"]
notes = "If customer-managed cert rotated incorrectly, still track as incident if production impact; tag as customer-caused in incident record; SLA timers may pause per policy."


6) Rollout / change management notes
Environments
- staging: accepts config pushes freely for iteration
- canary: subset of prod traffic/users (incident-bot + alert routing reads canary mapping)
- prod: default

Rollout steps (standard)
1) Update in staging (validate schema + smoke test resolve_owner)
2) Promote to canary (monitor unmapped rate, wrong-owner reports)
3) Promote to prod
4) Post-rollout check: compare incident-bot owner resolution vs manual owner for last N incidents

Rollback
- Control plane retains last N configs (N=20) and allows quick rollback by mapping_version.
- Rollback is a config pointer flip, not a redeploy.

Emergency override
- In a live incident, we may need a temporary remap.
- Proposal:
  - allow “emergency_override=true” that bypasses semver monotonic requirement but still requires:
    - actor in allowlist (SRE + Platform on-call)
    - explicit expiry (effective_until date)
    - auto-alert if expired

Change review policy (draft)
- Any change to primary owner.team for an existing (type, subtype, tier) key requires approval from both teams’ EMs (or designated delegate).
- SRE is notified on every production promotion.

Observability for the mapping
- Emit metrics:
  - owner_mapping_resolution_total{status=mapped|fallback|unmapped, consumer=incident-bot|alert-router|console}
  - owner_mapping_unmapped_keys_total{incident_type, incident_subtype?, customer_tier} BUT NOTE: we must be careful not to introduce new label cardinality; only emit allowlisted taxonomy values. If subtype is unknown/unallowlisted, emit subtype="unknown".
- Log events with the full key and returned owner for audit/debug (logs can have higher cardinality).


7) Open questions / TODO
- Should we store Slack channel IDs rather than names to avoid rename drift?
- Do we need service ownership (service field) for reporting, or is team ownership sufficient for v1?
- How do we represent “ownership is SRE during mitigation, then handoff to X for fix” (likely belongs in remediation SLA policy, not this schema)?
- Who maintains the team allowlist? (Platform suggests a small internal registry, otherwise a static list in the control plane repo)
- Should tier-specific mapping be limited to a small subset of types (private-connectivity, dedicated capacity), to avoid duplication?

Action items
- Ethan: sync this schema draft with Rafael + Allison (cardinality guardrails) and incorporate final taxonomy v1 allowlist (once finalized).
- Mina/Vanessa: provide final owners for disputed routing-vs-fleet edge cases.
- Kira: identify minimum runbook links for top 10 incident keys.

References
- Linear ENG-4825 (owner mapping config + audit)
- PR redwood#28488 (initial implementation)
- Reliability Program: incident taxonomy + ownership mapping (Confluence)

notes = "If customer-managed cert rotated incorrectly, still track as incident if production impact; tag as customer-caused in incident record; SLA timers may pause per policy."


6) Rollout / change management notes
Environments
- staging: accepts config pushes freely for iteration
- canary: subset of prod traffic/users (incident-bot + alert routing reads canary mapping)
- prod: default

Rollout steps (standard)
1) Update in staging (validate schema + smoke test resolve_owner)
2) Promote to canary (monitor unmapped rate, wrong-owner reports)
3) Promote to prod
4) Post-rollout check: compare incident-bot owner resolution vs manual owner for last N incidents

Rollback
- Control plane retains last N configs (N=20) and allows quick rollback by mapping_version.
- Rollback is a config pointer flip, not a redeploy.

Emergency override
- In a live incident, we may need a temporary remap.
- Proposal:
  - allow “emergency_override=true” that bypasses semver monotonic requirement but still requires:
    - actor in allowlist (SRE + Platform on-call)
    - explicit expiry (effective_until date)
    - auto-alert if expired

Change review policy (draft)
- Any change to primary owner.team for an existing (type, subtype, tier) key requires approval from both teams’ EMs (or designated delegate).
- SRE is notified on every production promotion.

Observability for the mapping
- Emit metrics:
  - owner_mapping_resolution_total{status=mapped|fallback|unmapped, consumer=incident-bot|alert-router|console}
  - owner_mapping_unmapped_keys_total{incident_type, incident_subtype?, customer_tier} BUT NOTE: we must be careful not to introduce new label cardinality; only emit allowlisted taxonomy values. If subtype is unknown/unallowlisted, emit subtype="unknown".
- Log events with the full key and returned owner for audit/debug (logs can have higher cardinality).


7) Open questions / TODO
- Should we store Slack channel IDs rather than names to avoid rename drift?
- Do we need service ownership (service field) for reporting, or is team ownership sufficient for v1?
- How do we represent “ownership is SRE during mitigation, then handoff to X for fix” (likely belongs in remediation SLA policy, not this schema)?
- Who maintains the team allowlist? (Platform suggests a small internal registry, otherwise a static list in the control plane repo)
- Should tier-specific mapping be limited to a small subset of types (private-connectivity, dedicated capacity), to avoid duplication?

Action items
- Ethan: sync this schema draft with Rafael + Allison (cardinality guardrails) and incorporate final taxonomy v1 allowlist (once finalized).
- Mina/Vanessa: provide final owners for disputed routing-vs-fleet edge cases.
- Kira: identify minimum runbook links for top 10 incident keys.

References
- Linear ENG-4825 (owner mapping config + audit)
- PR redwood#28488 (initial implementation)
- Reliability Program: incident taxonomy + ownership mapping (Confluence)

title: Enterprise security response playbook — RBAC v2 & log export
preview:
Purpose:
This playbook captures canonical responses and operational runbooks we give customers around RBAC v2, audit log schema, SIEM export options, and our evidence automation approach. Intended audience: security reviewers at customer orgs, Solutions Engineering, and Product Security reviewers who produce packaged artifacts for sales/CS.

Scope and intent:
- Not a formal trust-package (that’s produced by the compliance team); this is a living playbook containing ready-to-send answers, field-level log examples, and runbooks for on-demand exports.
- Focused on the pieces customers ask about most: role shapes & intent (RBAC v2), what exactly we log (audit schema), how logs are exported/ingested into Splunk/Elastic/Datadog, and what we can automate for evidence collection.

High-level RBAC v2 summary (customer-facing short):
- RBAC v2 separates: identities (users, service principals), role bindings (resource-scoped), and policies (action->permission mapping).
- Roles are first-class objects with versioned definitions; roles can be pinned per environment and can inherit via a small, explicit set of built-in base roles.
- Intended customer mapping: map your product roles -> Redwood roles via role bindings scoped to org/project/environment. Minimal principle: grant the least privilege necessary for the route / model / dataset.

RBAC v2 quick reference (for security reviewers):
- Role types: admin (org-level), operator (env-level), app-service (service principal scoped to model endpoints), auditor (read-only, log access).
- Resources: route, model, model-version, dataset, deployment, key (kms-key-ref), audit-log-export.
- Example permission: routes:invoke, routes:update, models:promote, logs:read, logs:export.
- Role binding example (JSONL):
  { "binding_id": "bind-9a1f", "principal": "svc:billing-ingestor", "role": "app-service", "scope": "org:acme/environment:prod" }

Audit log schema (recommended canonical field list):
We prefer newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) for audit exports. Each record is one event. The following fields are included in every event (fields below are present even if empty):
- timestamp: ISO-8601 utc
- event_id: uuidv4
- event_type: string (e.g., route.invoke, model.load, auth.login, role.bind)
- outcome: success | failure | throttled | partial
- actor_id: principal id (user:<id> / svc:<id>)
- actor_type: user | service | system
- actor_roles: [role_name, ...] (snapshot at time-of-event)
- action: free-form action string (semantic)
- resource_type: model | route | dataset | key | deployment | audit-config
- resource_id: canonical id (model:<model-id>)
- request_id: short-trace id (helps correlate frontend/backend traces)
- trace_id: full tracing id when available (OpenTelemetry compatible)
- ip_address: client IP if applicable
- user_agent: client UA (if present)
- region: region id of serving cluster
- model: model name used for inference (if relevant)
- model_version: specific model ver/commit/quantization tag
- tokens_in, tokens_out: integer token counts (for generation events)
- latency_ms: end-to-end latency we measured (ms)
- batch_size: integer (if batching applied)
- kv_cache_hit: boolean (if prefix cache used)
- fallback_model: optional string when a fallback was used
- error_code: internal canonical error code (if any)
- error_message: free-text (truncated)
- internal_tags: array (e.g., ["canary","dedicated-pool"])
- retention_policy: reference tag used for downstream retention pipelines

Notes:
- PII: we avoid logging full prompt/response text in standard audit logs for hosted accounts. For customers with explicit agreement (private deployments, signed DPA extensions), we can enable redaction-safe prompt logging into a secured bucket.
- Sensitive fields are redacted by default; we include token counts/latency and pointers to stored artefacts rather than raw content.

Log formatting and export formats:
- NDJSON is our canonical export. We support three export sinks out-of-the-box:
  1) HTTP(S) push to Splunk HEC (recommended for Splunk): batched NDJSON POSTs, configurable HEC token, retry/backoff.
  2) Kafka/Confluent topic: NDJSON messages keyed by org id. Good for high-throughput customers.
  3) S3/GCS batches (parquet/ndjson) for customers who prefer cloud landing zone + cloud-native ingest.
- Syslog/CEF forwarding: available via an adaptor that maps key fields to CEF; supported for SIEMs that need legacy connectors.

SIEM ingestion mapping notes (for engineers):
- Splunk: use HEC with index-time sourcetype redaction rules; recommended sourcetype: redwood:audit:ndjson. Provide sample props/transforms to extract tokens_in/tokens_out, latency_ms, actor_roles.
- Elastic: ingest NDJSON via Filebeat or ingest API; provide an ingest pipeline that normalizes timestamp and creates ECS-compatible fields: observer, event, source, user.
- Datadog: push via HTTP logs endpoint; provide mappings for attributes + tags using the org id and route id for filtering.

Evidence automation strategy (how we assemble a trust package quickly):
- Goal: produce a signed artefact containing: RBAC snapshot (JSON), sample audit logs (NDJSON) for selected window, SIEM mapping manifest (yaml), configuration exports (exported feature flags, kms-refs, network egress rules), and a verification checklist.
- Automation steps (high-level):
  1) On request, trigger an evidence run for org X via internal endpoint (ticket, Solutions Eng runbook).
  2) Orchestrator collects: current RBAC bindings, role definitions (v2), latest 7 days of audit logs (sampled), export config for SIEM sink, and a signed manifest referencing S3 URIs.
  3) Artefact is packaged into a signed tar.gz stored on a time-limited pre-signed URL and pushed to customer-owned S3/GCS if required by contract.
  4) Delivery: Solutions Eng shares signed link + verification hash; if customer requires, we run a remote verification call to validate parsers.
- Automation caveats: retention/scale limits prevent full log dumps by default; for large orgs we supply sampled windows + tail-stream examples + replay tokens to re-hydrate more events under strict controls.

Sample customer questionnaire responses (boilerplate, edit per customer):
Q: Do you provide role-based access controls for management and data plane?
A: Yes. RBAC v2 separates management-plane role bindings and data-plane permissions. We maintain role definitions centrally and support scoped role bindings per organization and environment. The auditor role allows read-only access to audit logs and configuration metadata.

Q: What events are logged for model inference?
A: For each inference request we log an event with timestamp, event_id, actor id/type/roles, route/model/model_version, tokens_in/tokens_out, latency_ms, request and trace ids, and outcome. We omit prompt/response text for hosted accounts by default; logging of raw content is available only under contract and with data handling controls.

Q: How can we ingest logs into our SIEM?
A: We support Splunk HEC, Kafka topics, and cloud landing zones (S3/GCS) by default. We provide a Splunk HEC sample config, a Filebeat pipeline for Elastic, and a minimal Fluentd config for other collectors. (See attached sample configs in the evidence package.)

On-demand evidence export runbook (quick):
- Pre-reqs: Solutions Eng request ticket JIRA-EVID-###, customer confirmation for export scope, legal approval if the export contains PII.
- Steps:
  1) Create evidence run in infra orchestration UI -> choose org and time window.
  2) Select sink: Redwood pre-signed URL OR customer S3.
  3) Trigger run and monitor job; expected time 3–15 minutes for small orgs, up to hours for large exports.
  4) Review package, redact further if requested, deliver.

Operational notes and guardrails:
- Retention: default audit log retention is 90 days for hosted accounts; Dedicated/Private can negotiate longer retention.
- Access: only 'auditor' and above roles can request exports; exports are gated by an approvals flow (pull request from Security team).
- Encryption: artifacts at rest are encrypted with KMS-managed keys (customer-managed KMS available for Private).

Unresolved questions / TODOs:
- Decide canonical minimum sample size for evidence packages for very large customers (currently we use 24h or 10k events, whichever smaller). (Owner: Samir)
- Formalize wording for support contract addendum that permits raw prompt logging under a DPA extension. (Owner: Nina)
- Add a lightweight Terraform module example for Splunk HEC onboarding (Owner: Laura).

Next steps:
- Convert this playbook into a polished one-pager for Solutions Eng with curated sample configs.
- Add automated test that validates NDJSON output against the schema above on every audit pipeline change (CI job).
- Link to final trust-package generation pipeline (CONFLUENCE-12345) and update the security runbook with the final role naming conventions.

Appendix A — sample NDJSON event (single line):
{"timestamp":"2025-12-02T18:14:32Z","event_id":"4a8b2f4e-6c7e-4d3b-9f2a-1b2c3d4e5f6a","event_type":"route.invoke","outcome":"success","actor_id":"user:U-1752","actor_type":"user","actor_roles": ["engineer"],"action":"invoke","resource_type":"route","resource_id":"route:translate-enfr-prod","request_id":"req-9c1d","trace_id":"trace-0b1c2d","ip_address":"18.205.33.12","user_agent":"Redwood-CLI/1.4.2","region":"us-west-2","model":"open-llama-13b","model_version":"v1.7-q4","tokens_in": 52,"tokens_out": 234,"latency_ms": 120,"batch_size": 1,"kv_cache_hit": false,"internal_tags": ["dedicated-pool"]}

Appendix B — quick links and references:
- RBAC v2 design doc (internal): CONFLUENCE-12345
- Audit pipeline infra PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/infra/pull/412
- Evidence automation ticket template: JIRA-EVID-REQUEST

Notes to self: keep samples short, customers prefer a 1-page summary + single sample event. This doc should be updated after the next RBAC cut (v2.1) and after the Splunk HEC pipeline changes planned for Q1-2026.
