title: Kickoff notes: Safe apply + rollback for Optimization Configs
preview:
Doc: kickoff notes + early open questions for the “Safe apply + rollback mechanism” project (Optimization Config versioning + staged rollout + one-click rollback).

Date/time
- 2025-09-12 10:00–11:00 PT

Owner
- Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane)

Attendees
- Product: Paula Mendes, Marissa Cole (partial), Naomi Feldman (partial)
- Program: Talia Benson
- Eng Platform: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Michael Nguyen, Jordan Lee
- Console: Chloe Martin, Jada Williams
- Runtime: Noah Patel (partial via notes)
- SRE/Obs: Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant
- Applied ML/Evals: Jonas Weber, Arjun Desai
- Security: Marcus Lin
- Release Eng: Connor O’Brien

Goal of kickoff
- Align on what “safe apply” + “rollback” means for Optimization Config changes (batching/caching/quant profiles).
- Establish a shared definition for blast radius + gating signals.
- Agree on minimum viable versioning fields (MVP) and how we migrate existing configs.
- Identify risks (race conditions, partial rollout states) and early API/UX shape.

Problem statement (recap)
- Optimization changes (batching heuristics, cache toggles, quantization profiles) can cause high-impact regressions (p95/p99 latency spikes, elevated 5xx, quality degradation).
- Current change path is too manual, not easily reversible, and not consistently auditable across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Non-goals (phase 1)
- Fully automated rollback without human confirmation for all customers.
- Complex transactional rollbacks across unrelated subsystems.

Working definitions
1) “Optimization Config”
- The serving-time configuration bundle that affects inference cost/perf behavior.
- Includes (not exhaustive): batching parameters/heuristics, caching toggles and cache sizing thresholds, routing knobs that affect batching/caching decisions, quantization profile selection (where supported).

2) “Version”
- Immutable record of an Optimization Config payload + metadata.
- Versions can be “validated” and then “applied” via staged rollout.
- A separate “active pointer” (per scope) determines what version the runtime should use.

3) “Safe apply”
- A controlled rollout process that:
  (a) validates the config (schema + semantic checks)
  (b) applies progressively (canary -> ramp)
  (c) gates progression on health signals (SLO metrics; plus eval/quality for quant)
  (d) is pausable/reversible

4) “Rollback”
- One-click revert of the active pointer back to a prior “known good” version for a given scope.
- Requires audit log entry + RBAC permission.
- Must be robust to partial rollout and concurrent operations (apply vs rollback).

Blast radius (early proposal)
We need a consistent way to describe “how big is this change?” to drive default canary requirements + approval requirements.

Blast radius dimensions (what we think we need to encode)
- Scope selector:
  - tenant_id (single tenant vs multiple vs global)
  - model_id / model_family (one model vs a whole catalog)
  - region (single region vs multi-region)
  - deployment_mode (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
- Rollout fraction:
  - percent-based traffic canary (e.g., 1%, 10%, 50%, 100%) OR shard-based (worker shard ring)
- Expected load level:
  - off-peak vs peak windows (ties into policy / recommended windows)

Blast radius classes (draft, for policy + UX labels)
- BR0 “No-prod”: staging/dev only (no SLO gates required beyond basic)
- BR1 “Small”: single tenant + single region + single model OR <=5% traffic canary; no global effect
- BR2 “Medium”: single tenant multi-region OR multi-tenant limited cohort OR <=25% traffic
- BR3 “Large”: multi-tenant broad rollout OR default/global scope OR >=50% traffic

Open question: do we compute blast radius from the requested scope + rollout policy automatically, or require the operator to select it (with validation)?
- Lean: compute automatically and show in UI; allow elevated approval workflow for overrides.

Minimum viable versioning fields (MVP)
We need to lock down the smallest set that still enables:
- traceability (who/what/when)
- diff view
- safe apply orchestration
- rollback pointer flip

MVP fields (proposal; names not final)
- config_version_id (opaque ID; sortable timestamp or UUID)
- scope (tenant/model/region/deployment-mode selector; stored as normalized fields)
- payload (full config JSON; immutable)
- payload_hash / checksum (to detect accidental mutation + for idempotency keys)
- created_at, created_by (actor; user + auth context)
- source (console | api | terraform | internal automation)
- change_reason (free text; required for prod scopes)
- parent_version_id (optional; enables lineage)
- status (draft | validated | applied_in_progress | active | superseded | rolled_back)
- validation_summary (store result + validator version; enough to show in UI)
- diff_summary (computed vs parent or vs currently active)
- gate_policy_ref (which gate policy to use; default per blast radius)
- rollout_policy (canary percentage, step schedule, pause rules) — question: store as part of apply “operation” vs version?

Not MVP (but likely needed soon)
- approvals (who approved; change management)
- linked_incident_id / ticket
- “known-good” flag (or inference from apply success + time window)

Key design fork (not resolved in kickoff)
- Option A: store rollout_policy on the version itself (version “encodes” how it should be rolled out)
- Option B: store rollout_policy on the apply operation (version is pure config; operations reference it)
- Leaning: Option B to keep version immutable/pure and allow re-apply with different canary.

Migration concerns (current-state -> versioned)
We currently have existing Optimization Config(s) in control plane that are mutable / updated in place.

Migration goals
- No downtime.
- Preserve currently-active behavior as “v1” or “v0 migrated”.
- Allow rollback even for the first new version after migration.

Proposed migration approach (draft)
- Step 1: introduce tables/records for immutable versions + active pointers.
- Step 2: backfill a migrated version per existing scope:
  - Create config_version_id = “migrated-<date>-<scope>” (or standard UUID) with created_by = “system:migration”
  - Mark as active via active pointer record.
- Step 3: keep compatibility layer:
  - runtime continues reading the “active pointer”; if absent, fallback to old config record during the cutover window.
- Step 4: after cutover, freeze old mutable path and gradually remove.

Migration open questions
- How do we identify “scope” for legacy configs when historically some values were implicitly global?
- Do we need to support multiple active pointers per scope (e.g., per tenant override + global default), and define precedence?
- For Dedicated: do we treat each cluster as a scope dimension (cluster_id) or map to tenant_id + region?
- For Private: how do we represent “scope” when the control plane may be local? Do we standardize on same schema but different storage/replication?

Safe apply gating signals (initial alignment, not final)
Health gates (SRE/Obs)
- 5xx rate (absolute + delta vs baseline)
- p95 and p99 latency (absolute + delta vs baseline)
- saturation signals (GPU utilization, queue depth, batching queue time, KV cache hit/miss if relevant)
- error budget burn (optional / later)

Quality gates (Applied ML / quantization)
- For quant profile changes, we need eval harness gating before promoting beyond canary.
- Proposed: run a fixed prompt set + regression thresholds (task-specific) and require “no significant degradation” vs baseline.
- Open question: noisy evals—do we use confidence intervals and require N runs? What is “fast enough” for rollout progression?

Policy question
- What is the default gating window for canary steps? (e.g., 10–15 min for SLO metrics; longer for evals)
- How do we avoid false positives causing rollout stalls during naturally volatile periods?

Rollback semantics (what “rollback succeeded” means)
We need very crisp semantics for Console + API responses.

Draft semantics
- Rollback request:
  - updates the active pointer for a given scope to target_version_id
  - triggers runtime config applier to converge workers to target_version_id
- “Rollback initiated” returns quickly with operation_id.
- “Rollback completed” means:
  - active pointer updated
  - >=X% of workers have acknowledged target_version_id (X default depends on deployment; 95%? 99%?)
  - and/or traffic-weighted convergence depending on routing mode

Edge cases
- Partial rollout: rolling back while a progressive apply is mid-ramp.
  - Need an operation lock / concurrency policy (single in-flight operation per scope).
- Concurrent apply and rollback requests.
  - Need idempotency keys + compare-and-swap pointer updates.
- Cache invalidation: rollback might change cache toggles and cause transient latency.
  - Need mitigations (staged cache flip? pre-warm? or document expected spike).

RBAC + auditability (Security)
RBAC (draft roles)
- optimization_config.viewer
- optimization_config.editor (create/validate)
- optimization_config.operator (apply/rollback)
- optimization_config.admin (promote defaults/global; manage policies)

Audit events (must-have)
- optimization_config.version_created
- optimization_config.version_validated
- optimization_config.apply_started / apply_paused / apply_completed / apply_failed
- optimization_config.promoted
- optimization_config.rollback_started / rollback_completed / rollback_failed

Minimum audit fields (draft)
- actor (user/service), actor_type
- tenant_id (if applicable), scope
- config_version_id, previous_version_id
- operation_id, request_id
- timestamp
- source_ip/user_agent (if from Console)

Console UX notes (very early)
- Timeline view by scope: shows versions + who/when + status badges.
- Diff view: highlights “risk fields” (batching max_batch_tokens, cache enabled, quant profile) with warnings.
- Rollback button:
  - requires confirmation step: “blast radius summary” + type-to-confirm for BR2/BR3
  - shows what will happen: active pointer -> target version, and expected time to converge
- Need visibility for support: “what version is currently active for tenant X / model Y / region Z?”

Decisions made in kickoff (lightweight)
- We will pursue immutable versions + active pointer model (vs fully mutable config object).
- Rollback is implemented as pointer revert + convergent runtime applier (not “undo patch”).
- We will define blast radius and use it to choose default canary + gating policies.

Open questions to resolve (tracked)
1) Scope model / precedence
- Do we allow overrides (tenant-specific) on top of a global default? What is the precedence order?
- How to represent “All models” safely without accidental wide blast radius?

2) Apply operation model
- Is apply modeled as an Operation object with steps + status, or do we encode status on the version?
- How do we persist step-level gating results for postmortem/debugging?

3) Gate defaults
- What thresholds for p95/p99 delta and 5xx delta? Do we set global defaults + allow override with elevated permission?

4) Quant gating integration
- What is the minimal eval that’s “good enough” for canary? How long does it take? How do we avoid blocking rollouts for hours?

5) Private deployments
- How do customers “pin” a version and roll back using helm/installer/terraform without creating drift?
- Do we require a local “version store” in Private or is it packaged as part of the control plane?

6) Migration
- How do we guarantee that the first rollback after migration has a known-good target?
- Should we automatically mark the migrated baseline as “protected / known-good”?

Action items (initial)
- Benji Okafor: draft API outline for version CRUD + apply + rollback + operation status (by 2025-09-19)
- Michael Nguyen: propose storage schema (versions + active pointers + operations) + migration plan (by 2025-09-19)
- Jordan Lee: RBAC permission matrix + policy bundle changes (by 2025-09-23)
- Sean Gallagher + Allison Grant: proposal for gating metrics/tags and dashboard annotations (by 2025-09-23)
- Jonas Weber + Arjun Desai: quantization gate: minimal eval set + thresholds + runtime constraints (by 2025-09-26)
- Chloe Martin + Jada Williams: Console UX wire notes for history/diff/rollback confirmation + copy requirements (by 2025-09-26)
- Connor O’Brien: release sequencing risks + feature flag plan across runtime/control-plane/console (by 2025-09-26)

Notes / parking lot
- Support escalation path: during Sev, who is authorized to rollback for a customer? (tie into on-call + RBAC break-glass?)
- Consider adding optional response headers (config_version) for debugging customer-reported regressions.
- Decide naming: “Optimization Config” vs “Optimize Policy” vs “Serving Optimization Profile”. Keep consistent with existing docs.

Next meeting
- 2025-09-19: review schema + API draft; decide Operation model; align on scope precedence and blast radius computation.
text:
Doc: kickoff notes + early open questions for the “Safe apply + rollback mechanism” project (Optimization Config versioning + staged rollout + one-click rollback).

Date/time
- 2025-09-12 10:00–11:00 PT

Owner
- Ethan Park (Platform Control Plane)

Attendees
- Product: Paula Mendes, Marissa Cole (partial), Naomi Feldman (partial)
- Program: Talia Benson
- Eng Platform: Ethan Park, Benji Okafor, Michael Nguyen, Jordan Lee
- Console: Chloe Martin, Jada Williams
- Runtime: Noah Patel (partial via notes)
- SRE/Obs: Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant
- Applied ML/Evals: Jonas Weber, Arjun Desai
- Security: Marcus Lin
- Release Eng: Connor O’Brien

Goal of kickoff
- Align on what “safe apply” + “rollback” means for Optimization Config changes (batching/caching/quant profiles).
- Establish a shared definition for blast radius + gating signals.
- Agree on minimum viable versioning fields (MVP) and how we migrate existing configs.
- Identify risks (race conditions, partial rollout states) and early API/UX shape.

Problem statement (recap)
- Optimization changes (batching heuristics, cache toggles, quantization profiles) can cause high-impact regressions (p95/p99 latency spikes, elevated 5xx, quality degradation).
- Current change path is too manual, not easily reversible, and not consistently auditable across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Non-goals (phase 1)
- Fully automated rollback without human confirmation for all customers.
- Complex transactional rollbacks across unrelated subsystems.

Working definitions
1) “Optimization Config”
- The serving-time configuration bundle that affects inference cost/perf behavior.
- Includes (not exhaustive): batching parameters/heuristics, caching toggles and cache sizing thresholds, routing knobs that affect batching/caching decisions, quantization profile selection (where supported).

2) “Version”
- Immutable record of an Optimization Config payload + metadata.
- Versions can be “validated” and then “applied” via staged rollout.
- A separate “active pointer” (per scope) determines what version the runtime should use.

3) “Safe apply”
- A controlled rollout process that:
  (a) validates the config (schema + semantic checks)
  (b) applies progressively (canary -> ramp)
  (c) gates progression on health signals (SLO metrics; plus eval/quality for quant)
  (d) is pausable/reversible

4) “Rollback”
- One-click revert of the active pointer back to a prior “known good” version for a given scope.
- Requires audit log entry + RBAC permission.
- Must be robust to partial rollout and concurrent operations (apply vs rollback).

Blast radius (early proposal)
We need a consistent way to describe “how big is this change?” to drive default canary requirements + approval requirements.

Blast radius dimensions (what we think we need to encode)
- Scope selector:
  - tenant_id (single tenant vs multiple vs global)
  - model_id / model_family (one model vs a whole catalog)
  - region (single region vs multi-region)
  - deployment_mode (Hosted / Dedicated / Private)
- Rollout fraction:
  - percent-based traffic canary (e.g., 1%, 10%, 50%, 100%) OR shard-based (worker shard ring)
- Expected load level:
  - off-peak vs peak windows (ties into policy / recommended windows)

Blast radius classes (draft, for policy + UX labels)
- BR0 “No-prod”: staging/dev only (no SLO gates required beyond basic)
- BR1 “Small”: single tenant + single region + single model OR <=5% traffic canary; no global effect
- BR2 “Medium”: single tenant multi-region OR multi-tenant limited cohort OR <=25% traffic
- BR3 “Large”: multi-tenant broad rollout OR default/global scope OR >=50% traffic

Open question: do we compute blast radius from the requested scope + rollout policy automatically, or require the operator to select it (with validation)?
- Lean: compute automatically and show in UI; allow elevated approval workflow for overrides.

Minimum viable versioning fields (MVP)
We need to lock down the smallest set that still enables:
- traceability (who/what/when)
- diff view
- safe apply orchestration
- rollback pointer flip

MVP fields (proposal; names not final)
- config_version_id (opaque ID; sortable timestamp or UUID)
- scope (tenant/model/region/deployment-mode selector; stored as normalized fields)
- payload (full config JSON; immutable)
- payload_hash / checksum (to detect accidental mutation + for idempotency keys)
- created_at, created_by (actor; user + auth context)
- source (console | api | terraform | internal automation)
- change_reason (free text; required for prod scopes)
- parent_version_id (optional; enables lineage)
- status (draft | validated | applied_in_progress | active | superseded | rolled_back)
- validation_summary (store result + validator version; enough to show in UI)
- diff_summary (computed vs parent or vs currently active)
- gate_policy_ref (which gate policy to use; default per blast radius)
- rollout_policy (canary percentage, step schedule, pause rules) — question: store as part of apply “operation” vs version?

Not MVP (but likely needed soon)
- approvals (who approved; change management)
- linked_incident_id / ticket
- “known-good” flag (or inference from apply success + time window)

Key design fork (not resolved in kickoff)
- Option A: store rollout_policy on the version itself (version “encodes” how it should be rolled out)
- Option B: store rollout_policy on the apply operation (version is pure config; operations reference it)
- Leaning: Option B to keep version immutable/pure and allow re-apply with different canary.

Migration concerns (current-state -> versioned)
We currently have existing Optimization Config(s) in control plane that are mutable / updated in place.

Migration goals
- No downtime.
- Preserve currently-active behavior as “v1” or “v0 migrated”.
- Allow rollback even for the first new version after migration.

Proposed migration approach (draft)
- Step 1: introduce tables/records for immutable versions + active pointers.
- Step 2: backfill a migrated version per existing scope:
  - Create config_version_id = “migrated-<date>-<scope>” (or standard UUID) with created_by = “system:migration”
  - Mark as active via active pointer record.
- Step 3: keep compatibility layer:
  - runtime continues reading the “active pointer”; if absent, fallback to old config record during the cutover window.
- Step 4: after cutover, freeze old mutable path and gradually remove.

Migration open questions
- How do we identify “scope” for legacy configs when historically some values were implicitly global?
- Do we need to support multiple active pointers per scope (e.g., per tenant override + global default), and define precedence?
- For Dedicated: do we treat each cluster as a scope dimension (cluster_id) or map to tenant_id + region?
- For Private: how do we represent “scope” when the control plane may be local? Do we standardize on same schema but different storage/replication?

Safe apply gating signals (initial alignment, not final)
Health gates (SRE/Obs)
- 5xx rate (absolute + delta vs baseline)
- p95 and p99 latency (absolute + delta vs baseline)
- saturation signals (GPU utilization, queue depth, batching queue time, KV cache hit/miss if relevant)
- error budget burn (optional / later)

Quality gates (Applied ML / quantization)
- For quant profile changes, we need eval harness gating before promoting beyond canary.
- Proposed: run a fixed prompt set + regression thresholds (task-specific) and require “no significant degradation” vs baseline.
- Open question: noisy evals—do we use confidence intervals and require N runs? What is “fast enough” for rollout progression?

Policy question
- What is the default gating window for canary steps? (e.g., 10–15 min for SLO metrics; longer for evals)
- How do we avoid false positives causing rollout stalls during naturally volatile periods?

Rollback semantics (what “rollback succeeded” means)
We need very crisp semantics for Console + API responses.

Draft semantics
- Rollback request:
  - updates the active pointer for a given scope to target_version_id
  - triggers runtime config applier to converge workers to target_version_id
- “Rollback initiated” returns quickly with operation_id.
- “Rollback completed” means:
  - active pointer updated
  - >=X% of workers have acknowledged target_version_id (X default depends on deployment; 95%? 99%?)
  - and/or traffic-weighted convergence depending on routing mode

Edge cases
- Partial rollout: rolling back while a progressive apply is mid-ramp.
  - Need an operation lock / concurrency policy (single in-flight operation per scope).
- Concurrent apply and rollback requests.
  - Need idempotency keys + compare-and-swap pointer updates.
- Cache invalidation: rollback might change cache toggles and cause transient latency.
  - Need mitigations (staged cache flip? pre-warm? or document expected spike).

RBAC + auditability (Security)
RBAC (draft roles)
- optimization_config.viewer
- optimization_config.editor (create/validate)
- optimization_config.operator (apply/rollback)
- optimization_config.admin (promote defaults/global; manage policies)

Audit events (must-have)
- optimization_config.version_created
- optimization_config.version_validated
- optimization_config.apply_started / apply_paused / apply_completed / apply_failed
- optimization_config.promoted
- optimization_config.rollback_started / rollback_completed / rollback_failed

Minimum audit fields (draft)
- actor (user/service), actor_type
- tenant_id (if applicable), scope
- config_version_id, previous_version_id
- operation_id, request_id
- timestamp
- source_ip/user_agent (if from Console)

Console UX notes (very early)
- Timeline view by scope: shows versions + who/when + status badges.
- Diff view: highlights “risk fields” (batching max_batch_tokens, cache enabled, quant profile) with warnings.
- Rollback button:
  - requires confirmation step: “blast radius summary” + type-to-confirm for BR2/BR3
  - shows what will happen: active pointer -> target version, and expected time to converge
- Need visibility for support: “what version is currently active for tenant X / model Y / region Z?”

Decisions made in kickoff (lightweight)
- We will pursue immutable versions + active pointer model (vs fully mutable config object).
- Rollback is implemented as pointer revert + convergent runtime applier (not “undo patch”).
- We will define blast radius and use it to choose default canary + gating policies.

Open questions to resolve (tracked)
1) Scope model / precedence
- Do we allow overrides (tenant-specific) on top of a global default? What is the precedence order?
- How to represent “All models” safely without accidental wide blast radius?

2) Apply operation model
- Is apply modeled as an Operation object with steps + status, or do we encode status on the version?
- How do we persist step-level gating results for postmortem/debugging?

3) Gate defaults
- What thresholds for p95/p99 delta and 5xx delta? Do we set global defaults + allow override with elevated permission?

4) Quant gating integration
- What is the minimal eval that’s “good enough” for canary? How long does it take? How do we avoid blocking rollouts for hours?

5) Private deployments
- How do customers “pin” a version and roll back using helm/installer/terraform without creating drift?
- Do we require a local “version store” in Private or is it packaged as part of the control plane?

6) Migration
- How do we guarantee that the first rollback after migration has a known-good target?
- Should we automatically mark the migrated baseline as “protected / known-good”?

Action items (initial)
- Benji Okafor: draft API outline for version CRUD + apply + rollback + operation status (by 2025-09-19)
- Michael Nguyen: propose storage schema (versions + active pointers + operations) + migration plan (by 2025-09-19)
- Jordan Lee: RBAC permission matrix + policy bundle changes (by 2025-09-23)
- Sean Gallagher + Allison Grant: proposal for gating metrics/tags and dashboard annotations (by 2025-09-23)
- Jonas Weber + Arjun Desai: quantization gate: minimal eval set + thresholds + runtime constraints (by 2025-09-26)
- Chloe Martin + Jada Williams: Console UX wire notes for history/diff/rollback confirmation + copy requirements (by 2025-09-26)
- Connor O’Brien: release sequencing risks + feature flag plan across runtime/control-plane/console (by 2025-09-26)

Notes / parking lot
- Support escalation path: during Sev, who is authorized to rollback for a customer? (tie into on-call + RBAC break-glass?)
- Consider adding optional response headers (config_version) for debugging customer-reported regressions.
- Decide naming: “Optimization Config” vs “Optimize Policy” vs “Serving Optimization Profile”. Keep consistent with existing docs.

Next meeting
- 2025-09-19: review schema + API draft; decide Operation model; align on scope precedence and blast radius computation.

title: Lightweight call reroute policy DSL (sketch)
preview:
Top-level goal: a tiny policy language (DSL) that client adapters can ship with to make fast, deterministic reroute/hedge decisions without pulling heavy config from control plane on every call.

Why:
- clients (edge proxies / lightweight sidecars) need predictable fallback behaviors when: regional capacity exhausted, model variant degraded, or transient infra errors (timeouts, 5xx).
- we want to avoid overloading the control plane and keep client behavior debuggable and auditable.
- policy must be small, explainable, and allow safe rollout of hedging and reroute without server-side changes.

Non-goals:
- replace server-side sophisticated routing; this is for conservative client decisions only.
- global policy orchestration (control plane will still distribute policies occasionally).

Requirements (informal):
1) Tiny syntax, ~10–30 lines typical.
2) Deterministic evaluation; no randomness.
3) Limited arithmetic (thresholds, counters, time windows).
4) Hooks into local telemetry counters (recentErrors, avgLatencyMs, tokenCostEstimate).
5) Safe defaults — if policy unparsable, fall back to simple: retry-once->use-primary.
6) Versioned (policy-v1, v2) with explicit migration semantics.

Example use-cases it should express:
- If primary region returns >=2 errors in last 30s, route to fallback region A for next 5 requests.
- Hedge: send a second request to family-B if latency > 200ms predicted and user is on premium tier.
- Cost-aware: avoid fallbacks to expensive quantized models unless cost budget allows.

DSL sketch (rough, intentionally small):
- grammar primitives: condition -> action | condition && condition | condition || condition | not(condition)
- conditions: counter(name, windowSec) OP number | metric(name) OP number | header(name) == 'value' | user_tier >= N
- actions: route(region=NAME, variant=NAME, weight=N) | hedge(to=region/variant, timeoutMs=N) | delay(ms=N) | tag(reason=STRING)

Tiny example policies: 
1) Simple region failover:
  policy-v1 {
    when counter(primary_errors, 30) >= 2 then route(region=eu-west, variant=primary-fallback) for 5 requests tag(reason="quick-failover")
    else route(region=us-west, variant=primary)
  }

2) Hedging for high-latency tails (premium only):
  policy-v1 {
    when user_tier >= 2 && metric(predicted_latency_ms) > 200 then hedge(to=variant=fast-quant, timeoutMs=120) tag(reason="tail-hedge-premium")
  }

3) Cost-aware guardrail:
  policy-v1 {
    when tokenCostEstimate > 100 && metric(budget_remaining_pct) < 0.1 then route(variant=cheap-quant)
  }

Notes on semantics and primitives (open issues):
- counter(name, window): should support sliding windows vs fixed buckets? Sliding preferable but heavier to implement client-side. For first pass use exponentially-decayed counters or simple ring buffer counters exposed through sdk.
- "for N requests" semantics: local client should decrement a short-lived token and persist in-memory across calls; safe for ephemeral processes.
- Evaluation frequency: evaluate at call start only. Avoid mid-stream changes (no dynamic switching mid-response).
- Hedging: if hedge request returns faster than primary, must cancel primary response consumption but allow server-side to complete (best-effort cancellation via existing cancellation token).
- Telemetry: every decision must emit: policy_id, policy_version, rule_matched, inputs snapshot (cond values), action_taken. This is critical for debugging and later offline eval.

Simple evaluator pseudo: 
  parsed = parse(policy_blob)
  ctx = gatherContext(callMeta, localCounters, userTier, predictedLatency, costEst)
  matched = evaluateRules(parsed, ctx)  // deterministic ordering, top-first
  emitTelemetry(matched, ctx)
  applyAction(matched.action)

Safety checks (client must enforce):
- Max hedge concurrency per call: default 1 (configurable up to 2).
- Max fallback rate per minute to expensive region/model: 50/min by default to avoid surprise bills.
- TTL for policy blobs: if older than 24 hours, fall back to last-known-safe or built-in default.

Runtime considerations:
- Parsers should be small: use a hand-rolled recursive-descent parser in Go/TS/Rust; avoid heavy parser generators in client libs.
- Cross-language spec: canonical spec in Markdown + test vectors in JSON (control plane validates before distributing).
- Policy signing: control plane signs blobs (HMAC) and clients verify; unauth policy -> reject.
- Fallback on parse error: log + increment parseFailureCounter, use built-in fallback policy.

Telemetry fields (proposal):
- policy_id, version, rule_id, rule_text, inputs: ["metric:lat=210","counter:primary_errors=3"], action: "hedge->fast-quant", decision_time_ms, caller_pid, sample_rate

Open questions / TODOs (for follow up):
- How do we express multi-step workflows (e.g., annotate and then route)? Do we allow compound actions? Risk: complexity explosion. Prefer simple atomic actions for v1.
- Can we express gradual weights (A/B) for rollouts? Might be useful (route(weight=0.1)). But requires client randomization; must be deterministic seed.
- Should policies be policy-scoped (per-tenant) or global with tenant overrides? We'll support both: global baseline + tenant overlay. Need merge semantics.
- Edge case: what if hedging doubles token usage and drives up cost badly? Need circuit breaker that tags callers and progressively disables hedging if cost spike detected.

Minimal test vectors to include in spec repo:
- policy blob that triggers on counter threshold (simulate counters) -> assert action route->fallback and telemetry emitted.
- hedging triggers and cancellation semantics: assert that fastest response is used and cancellation attempted on primary.
- oversized policy blob (line count > 200) should be rejected by client.

Implementation plan (very rough):
- week 0–2: finalize grammar + small reference parser in Go + 10 test vectors.
- week 2–4: integrate parser into proxy-lite (TS) and sidecar (Go). Add telemetry emissions.
- week 4–6: control plane: policy authoring UI + HMAC signing + distribution endpoint + validation server.
- week 6–8: dogfood on internal traffic (start with non-production buckets) and collect signals.

Backward-compat / rollout:
- policy has version field; when control plane updates, it sets rollout flag. Clients check signatures. For early rollout, set policy.mode=opt-in.

References (links/notes):
- slack thread: #ingress-routing (see thread 2025-08-05)
- jira:ENG-4821 (policy distribution + signing)
- related proto: ingress-routing/v1 (TBD)

Quick sketch of extremely tiny JSON transport format (for control plane):
  {
    "policy_id":"rl-01",
    "version":"v1",
    "blob":"(dsl text as string)",
    "signed_by":"ingress-service",
    "meta":"createdBy:EthanPark"
  }

Notes-to-self: keep the DSL intentionally constrained. The bulk of complexity should live in control-plane rule authoring and validation; clients should be simple evaluators + safe-guards.

Action items for next pass:
- pick final primitive set and write an ANTLR-ish EBNF (but implement parser by hand in clients).
- document safe default policy (explicit).
- add 8–10 unit tests to spec repo covering error modes.

Misc brain-dump ends here. Rough, messy, but actionable.

-- Ethan

(quick todo: drop sample policy into /repo/ingress/specs/policies/sample-quick-failover.txt and link in PR 317)

Runtime considerations:
- Parsers should be small: use a hand-rolled recursive-descent parser in Go/TS/Rust; avoid heavy parser generators in client libs.
- Cross-language spec: canonical spec in Markdown + test vectors in JSON (control plane validates before distributing).
- Policy signing: control plane signs blobs (HMAC) and clients verify; unauth policy -> reject.
- Fallback on parse error: log + increment parseFailureCounter, use built-in fallback policy.

Telemetry fields (proposal):
- policy_id, version, rule_id, rule_text, inputs: ["metric:lat=210","counter:primary_errors=3"], action: "hedge->fast-quant", decision_time_ms, caller_pid, sample_rate

Open questions / TODOs (for follow up):
- How do we express multi-step workflows (e.g., annotate and then route)? Do we allow compound actions? Risk: complexity explosion. Prefer simple atomic actions for v1.
- Can we express gradual weights (A/B) for rollouts? Might be useful (route(weight=0.1)). But requires client randomization; must be deterministic seed.
- Should policies be policy-scoped (per-tenant) or global with tenant overrides? We'll support both: global baseline + tenant overlay. Need merge semantics.
- Edge case: what if hedging doubles token usage and drives up cost badly? Need circuit breaker that tags callers and progressively disables hedging if cost spike detected.

Minimal test vectors to include in spec repo:
- policy blob that triggers on counter threshold (simulate counters) -> assert action route->fallback and telemetry emitted.
- hedging triggers and cancellation semantics: assert that fastest response is used and cancellation attempted on primary.
- oversized policy blob (line count > 200) should be rejected by client.

Implementation plan (very rough):
- week 0–2: finalize grammar + small reference parser in Go + 10 test vectors.
- week 2–4: integrate parser into proxy-lite (TS) and sidecar (Go). Add telemetry emissions.
- week 4–6: control plane: policy authoring UI + HMAC signing + distribution endpoint + validation server.
- week 6–8: dogfood on internal traffic (start with non-production buckets) and collect signals.

Backward-compat / rollout:
- policy has version field; when control plane updates, it sets rollout flag. Clients check signatures. For early rollout, set policy.mode=opt-in.

References (links/notes):
- slack thread: #ingress-routing (see thread 2025-08-05)
- jira:ENG-4821 (policy distribution + signing)
- related proto: ingress-routing/v1 (TBD)

Quick sketch of extremely tiny JSON transport format (for control plane):
  {
    "policy_id":"rl-01",
    "version":"v1",
    "blob":"(dsl text as string)",
    "signed_by":"ingress-service",
    "meta":"createdBy:EthanPark"
  }

Notes-to-self: keep the DSL intentionally constrained. The bulk of complexity should live in control-plane rule authoring and validation; clients should be simple evaluators + safe-guards.

Action items for next pass:
- pick final primitive set and write an ANTLR-ish EBNF (but implement parser by hand in clients).
- document safe default policy (explicit).
- add 8–10 unit tests to spec repo covering error modes.

Misc brain-dump ends here. Rough, messy, but actionable.

-- Ethan

(quick todo: drop sample policy into /repo/ingress/specs/policies/sample-quick-failover.txt and link in PR 317)

title: Thermal surface and proactive degradation policy for mixed GPU fleets
preview:
Summary:
This doc defines a lightweight, empirically calibrated 'frequency surface' model and an operational policy for proactively degrading performance to respect GPU power/thermal envelopes across mixed fleets (A100/H100/L4/MI300). The goal is to keep tail latency and availability stable during thermal excursions and mixed-workload microbursts while preserving unit-cost efficiency.

Motivation / Problem statement:
- We operate heterogeneous GPU pools with different thermal headroom and power efficiency. Workload microbursts and sustained high-util phases lead to thermal throttling or immediate frequency drops on some SKUs, which in turn causes latency amplification and instability for multi-tenant routing.
- Current reactive fallback (wait-for-throttle -> migrate -> cold start) is costly: high latencies, cache churn, and sometimes transient OOMs when KV caches are migrated.
- We need a policy that: (1) predicts when a card is approaching a thermally-triggered degradation surface; (2) gracefully reduces per-request resource footprint to avoid catastrophic drops; (3) applies consistent kernel selection and KV cache policies to keep behavior deterministic across architectures.

Definitions / concepts used in this doc:
- Frequency surface: a per-GPU (SKU) mapping from target throughput/active SM utilization to expected clock state and power draw under typical serving thermals. Represented by a small set of calibrated points plus linear interpolation.
- Headroom budget: the amount of frequency/power we keep in reserve to absorb short microbursts without entering a hard throttle. Expressed as percentage of maximum sustained power (e.g., 10%).
- Proactive degradation: preemptive lowering of a request's resource intensity (e.g., algorithmic kernel variant, kv-cache locality tradeoff, reduced concurrency) before hardware triggers an abrupt frequency clamp.
- Degradation step: an atomic policy action (e.g., switch to 8-bit quantized kernel, reduce max_batch_size for that shard, pin KV cache to host memory subset).

Assumptions / constraints:
- We assume measurable telemetry from each GPU: instantaneous clock, power draw, GPU temperature, GPU utilisation per process, and PCIe transfer rates.
- We assume the serving runtime can choose kernel variants at request time and can alter per-queue concurrency and KV cache locality settings without full model reload.
- We cannot insert long pauses or blocking migrations as primary mitigation in P95/P99 critical paths.

Design overview:
1) Per-SKU frequency surface calibration
   - For each SKU (A100 80GB, H100, L4, MI300), run automated calibration jobs across representative prompt lengths and batch sizes to measure: average clock under sustained inference, power, and latency.
   - Fit a compact piecewise-linear surface with <= 6 control points: idle->low->medium->high->sustained->saturated. Store as small JSON on the node: {sku: [ {util_pct, expected_clock_MHz, expected_power_W}, ... ] }.
   - Calibration outputs: thermal hysteresis windows (e.g., temp ranges that cause ±X MHz swings), time-to-throttle estimates under continuous load, and sensitivity factors that map incremental utilization -> delta power.

2) Runtime prediction and headroom budgeting
   - Nodes compute a short-horizon rolling forecast (5–30s) of expected utilization using recent arrival rates and queue lengths (exponential smoothing).
   - Combine forecast with the frequency surface to estimate the probability of crossing the conservative 'degradation threshold' (a control point set to keep power < (sustained_limit - headroom_budget)).
   - If probability > policy threshold (default 60%), mark node as 'at-risk' and start degradation plan for active QoS-soft flows.

3) Degradation steps (ordered, reversible)
   - Step A: soft concurrency cap — reduce per-request concurrency slots (threads/streams) for low-QoS flows (e.g., background embedding batches). This is minimal impact and no kernel change.
   - Step B: kernel dimming — switch to lower-power kernel variants (e.g., reduced-fma kernel, quantized 8-bit kernel, or block-size change that lowers SM utilisation) for eligible models. Markers: preserve top-k output fidelity where possible.
   - Step C: KV affinity shrink — reduce KV cache resident set for non-hot prefixes (evict LRU segments to host memory or compressed store). For chat workloads, keep prefix caches for pinned sessions.
   - Step D: lightweight request shaping — reject or delay low-priority micro-batches (token buckets) until utilization recovers.
   - Step E: scheduled migration — as last resort, signal orchestration to move durable state (large KV partitions) off the node and permit aggressive throttles. Migration must be asynchronous and background.

Mapping steps to hardware and kernels:
- A100: moderate thermal hysteresis. Prefer kernel dimming to reduced concurrency first for large attention-heavy models where kernel variants reduce SM occupancy efficiently. Use headroom_budget default 12%.
- H100: higher perf per watt but sometimes aggressive DVFS on thermal events. Favor early soft concurrency caps + kernel dimming; prefer quantized matmul kernels when available. headroom_budget default 8%.
- L4: low-power inference-focused SKU. Thermal hysteresis is tight; prefer KV affinity shrink earlier since memory bandwidth matters. headroom_budget default 15%.
- MI300: different thermal profile and memory subsystem; prefer multiplexing-based concurrency reductions (slice workload across fewer CUs) and avoid full KV evictions unless sustained. headroom_budget default 10%.

Scheduling policy changes for mixed pools:
- Assign a transient 'risk_score' to each node computed from forecast and surface. Global router uses risk_score along with capacity score to route. Prefer low-risk nodes for latency-sensitive routes.
- Implement fast soft-drain: if node risk_score exceeds critical threshold, mark new latency-sensitive requests as 'disallowed' for 3–10s and route to alternative replicas. Avoid wholesale draining to keep background jobs.
- Introduce a 'kernel-label' in the model catalog: models publish kernel compatibility with degradation steps (e.g., supports-8bit, supports-low-block kernels). Router and scheduler must respect that label.

KV cache behavior and mitigations:
- Partition KV into hot and warm tiers at runtime. Hot stays in GPU DRAM pinned (pinned set is small and session-critical); warm moves to compressed GPU-resident pages or host pinned pages.
- When stepping through degradation steps, eviction policy prefers warm-tier eviction first and sets per-session thresholds. For L4s, proactively compress 20–40% of warm-tier entries during high-risk states.
- Add a 'kv-session-preservation' toggle: if enabled for a customer/session, they get stronger relocation guarantees but consume more headroom. Customer billing/SLAs tied to this.

Observability and metrics:
- New per-node metrics to emit at 1s granularity: forecast_util_pct, risk_score, headroom_left_W, current_degradation_step, kernel_variant_in_use, kv_hot_ratio.
- Autobuild a short rolling causal trace for any sharp latency/throughput change that includes the sequence of applied degradation steps and node telemetry for the last 60s.

Calibration and rollout plan:
- Phase 0 (lab): run calibration pipelines on representative hardware images, generate frequency surfaces and validate forecasting accuracy across 200 synthetic workloads.
- Phase 1 (canary): enable soft concurrency caps and risk_score routing on a small canary fleet for non-critical routes. Monitor P95/P99 and unit cost delta.
- Phase 2 (kernel dimming): introduce kernel variants and enable step B for canary workloads that opt-in. Validate fidelity regressions with eval-set.
- Phase 3 (full): enable full multi-step policy, KV affinity controls, and routing changes for production pool with conservative defaults.

Open questions / trade-offs:
- How aggressive should headroom defaults be for multi-tenant nodes? We need an SLO-cost tradeoff study to choose default budgets per SKU.
- Kernel fidelity: some models respond poorly to quantized kernels; we need a per-model opt-in signal and automated eval regression checks.
- KV compression CPU overhead vs GPU warming cost: moving warm pages off-GPU may increase tail latency on cache misses.

Backward compatibility and fallbacks:
- If the node cannot apply kernel changes (older binaries), fallback to Step A and Step D and then route as needed.
- Keep a safe 'panic' route: if node experiences an unexpected OOM or hard thermal clamp, mark it NotReady and rely on cross-region fallback if available.

Next actions (owners and timelines):
- Calibration pipeline for A100/H100/L4/MI300: Maya (2 weeks)
- Forecasting & risk_score prototype in runtime: Luis (3 weeks)
- Kernel-variant tagging & catalog updates: Priya + Model-ops (2 weeks)
- KV affinity controls API: Connor (3 weeks)
- Canary plan and SLA/perf gates: Product + SRE (1 week to draft)

Appendix: quick checklist for experiments
- Perf sweep: 8 prompt lengths x 4 batch sizes x 2 concurrency patterns across each SKU
- Record: clocks, power (W), temp, latency distribution, KV resident sizes, and PCIe stall counters
- Fit: piecewise linear surface and compute residual errors; aim for <8% forecasting error for 30s horizon.

This is a working draft; expect iterations as calibration data arrives. Feedback requested on: (1) sensible headroom defaults per SKU, (2) acceptable degradation ordering for mixed workloads, and (3) telemetry dimensions we might be missing.

title: Hosted Data Residency Enforcement — Implementation Plan (Engineering)
preview:
Context / TL;DR
We are delivering “Hosted data residency enforcement” for the Hosted API as part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative. The work introduces customer-configurable region pinning (allowed region set + primary region), deterministic enforcement at request time (no cross-region routing/fallback unless explicitly allowed), and consistent error messaging + auditing.

This doc is the engineering execution plan (milestones, owners, dependencies, risks, and launch checklist). Source-of-truth for requirements + public contract is Confluence PRD + ADRs. This plan is intended to be updated as we learn during rollout.

Goals (engineering interpretation)
1) Deterministic request-time enforcement
- Given org/project/key residency policy, the system should either:
  a) route to a compliant region and continue, or
  b) block the request with a stable error contract.
- “Compliant” includes routing decisions, fallback decisions, background processing that touches customer content, and logging/tracing boundaries per ADR-021.

2) Clear errors + consistent behavior across endpoints
- Identical error payload shape + status across streaming and non-streaming.
- SDKs should surface a dedicated error class (separate workstream but contract must be stable).

3) Auditability + observability
- Emit audit log events for residency policy config changes.
- Emit decision logs/metrics for runtime enforcement outcomes (allowed/blocked; reason; selected region).

Non-goals (initial)
- Multi-region replication of customer content.
- Complex per-subsystem residency rules beyond routing/gateway/logging boundaries (we’ll harden over time).

Owners / Workstreams
- Control plane residency policy service + schema + migrations: Ethan Park (lead), Benji Okafor (impl lead), Michael Nguyen (migrations)
- Policy evaluation / precedence logic: Jordan Lee (architect)
- API key & auth integration: Kaitlyn Nguyen
- Smart routing enforcement (incl. fallback rules): Mina Haddad (lead), Ravi Menon (architect), Elena Popov (impl)
- API gateway error contract + request validation: Amara Diallo (lead), Brian Murphy (error contract owner)
- Audit logs / eventing: Logan Wright
- Metrics/dashboards: Allison Grant (standards), Sean Gallagher (SRE review)
- Security review / threat model sign-off: Marcus Lin
- Product sign-off on UX + error copy: Naomi Feldman

Milestones & Timeline (target dates are placeholders; adjust as we firm up)
Phase 0 — Discovery / alignment (complete)
- Policy model + enforcement boundary confirmed in ADR-021 (DONE)
- Public error contract approved in ADR-022 (DONE)
- Initial stakeholder sign-offs: Product/Security/SRE (DONE, pending final GA go/no-go)

Phase 1 — Design + scaffolding (Jan 27 – Feb 07)
1.1 Control plane schema + admin APIs (Ethan/Benji)
- Define policy objects (org/project/key), fields, validation rules, precedence.
- Admin endpoints + Console integration stubs.
- Deliverable: PR 21483 (merged), follow-up tasks for edge validations.

1.2 Gateway error contract wiring (Amara/Brian)
- Unified error response for residency violations; ensure streaming path does not degrade to generic 500.
- Deliverable: PR 21533 (merged), regression tests pending.

1.3 Routing enforcement design + feature flags (Mina/Ravi)
- Enforce “allowed regions” filter on primary routing.
- Disable cross-region fallback by default.
- Add structured “residency decision context” to routing logs.
- Deliverable: PR 21507 (merged), rollout plan attached below.

Phase 2 — Implementation + integration (Feb 10 – Mar 07)
2.1 Migrations + defaults (Michael)
- Backfill default “unrestricted” policy for existing orgs OR explicit null policy treated as unrestricted (final decision in ADR-021; implement exactly).
- Ensure no behavior change for customers without policies.
- Add dry-run mode to compare “would block” counts before enforcement.

2.2 End-to-end enforcement plumbing (Jordan/Ethan/Amara/Mina)
- Gateway extracts identity (org/project/key), resolves effective policy (precedence), and attaches decision context.
- Routing layer consumes decision context and restricts region selection.
- Ensure downstream systems that could log customer content remain region-scoped (per ADR-021; for now: logs/traces redaction rules and regional storage).

2.3 Audit log events (Logan)
- Emit events for create/update/delete residency policies.
- Emit events for admin overrides (if we ship a break-glass flag).
- Wire into audit-log-exporter.
- Deliverable: PR 21561 (merged), verify export + retention.

2.4 Observability + dashboards (Allison/Sean)
- Metrics: policy_eval_latency_ms, residency_block_total, residency_allow_total, cross_region_route_attempt_total, fallback_block_total.
- Dimensions: org_id (hashed), project_id (hashed), policy_id, requested_region, selected_region, endpoint, error_code, reason.
- Dashboard + alert thresholds with SRE review.
- Deliverable: observability-pack PR 803 (merged), confirm naming consistency w/ telemetry standards.

2.5 E2E tests + CI gating (TBD owner; propose: Elena Popov + QA rotation)
- E2E suite: EU-only org hitting US region; streaming and non-streaming; fallback scenarios; policy precedence (key overrides project overrides org).
- Add CI gate for routing + gateway changes.
- Deliverable: ENG-4851.

Phase 3 — Rollout (Mar 10 – Apr 04)
3.1 Staging enablement + test orgs (SRE + Platform)
- Enable feature flags in staging.
- Seed test orgs/projects/keys:
  - EU-only (primary eu-central)
  - US-only (primary us-east)
  - Multi-allowed (EU+US) with primary EU
  - Unrestricted (null policy)
- Run verification scenarios (see checklist).

3.2 Internal dogfood (Redwood apps / internal consumers)
- Enable enforcement for internal orgs in one region first.
- Monitor blocked requests and policy eval latency.

3.3 Allowlist customer rollout
- Start with 1–3 friendly enterprise accounts (Solutions Eng coordination).
- Require customer acknowledgment that cross-region fallback is disabled unless explicitly allowed.
- Provide playbook for pinning misconfig.

3.4 Progressive GA
- Gradually widen allowlist by region.
- Default remains “unrestricted” unless customer opts in.

Phase 4 — GA + hardening (Apr 07+)
- Documentation finalized + customer comms templates.
- Security evidence pack v1.
- Post-launch retro + backlog for additional subsystem hardening.

Dependency Map (what blocks what)
A) Control plane policy service (ENG-4821)
- Blocks: gateway enforcement (needs policy resolution), routing enforcement (needs effective policy), Console UX.
- Key dependency: auth model for API key-scoped policies (Kaitlyn).

B) API gateway enforcement & error contract (ENG-4833)
- Blocks: consistent customer experience; supportability.
- Depends on: ADR-022 finalization; policy resolution contract; streaming response plumbing.

C) Smart routing enforcement (ENG-4827)
- Blocks: residency actually being guaranteed in Hosted routing layer.
- Depends on: region ID mapping (infra sheet), gateway passing decision context OR routing calling policy service (we are doing “gateway resolves, routing consumes” to reduce duplication).

D) Observability/metrics (ENG-4846)
- Blocks: rollout confidence + SRE go/no-go.
- Depends on: stable error codes and block reason taxonomy; logging pipeline fields.

E) Audit logs (ENG-4840)
- Blocks: enterprise security requirement + GRC evidence.
- Depends on: policy CRUD endpoints and identity context.

F) E2E tests (ENG-4851)
- Blocks: CI confidence + regression prevention.
- Depends on: end-to-end staging environment enablement and seeded policies.

Implementation Notes / Technical Decisions (condensed)
- Precedence: API key policy > project policy > org policy > unrestricted default. If a higher-precedence object exists but is invalid, treat as configuration error (block) rather than fallback to lower level (confirm with Product/Security).
- Primary region: used as preferred target if included in allowed set. If primary not in allowed set, validation should fail at config time; if it somehow occurs at runtime, block with config_invalid.
- Fallback behavior:
  - Default: no cross-region fallback if policy is pinned (allowed regions != “any”).
  - If allowed regions includes multiple regions, fallback within allowed set is allowed.
  - If routing cannot find capacity within allowed set, return residency/capacity error as specified (do not silently route outside).
- “Customer content” boundary per ADR-021: prompts, completions, embeddings inputs/outputs, tool/function payloads. Metadata allowed cross-region must be explicitly enumerated (request_id, timing metrics, non-content error codes).

Error Codes / Block Reasons (taxonomy we will implement)
(Exact payload per ADR-022; this is engineering mapping for metrics and runbooks.)
- RESIDENCY_REGION_NOT_ALLOWED: requested/selected region not in allowed set (or policy demands EU-only and request attempted US).
- RESIDENCY_FALLBACK_BLOCKED: routing attempted cross-region fallback but policy forbids.
- RESIDENCY_POLICY_NOT_FOUND: policy reference exists but missing (should be rare; indicates data integrity issue).
- RESIDENCY_POLICY_INVALID: policy config invalid (primary not in allowed, empty allowed list, unknown region id).
- RESIDENCY_ENFORCEMENT_TEMPORARY_UNAVAILABLE: policy evaluation dependency failure (we should strongly prefer fail-closed for pinned customers; decide per tier/flag).

Feature Flags / Rollout Controls
- residency_enforcement_enabled (global)
- residency_enforcement_allowlist_orgs (list)
- residency_enforcement_dry_run (log + metrics only, no blocking)
- residency_enforcement_fail_open (emergency only; should require Security approval; emits audit event)
- residency_disable_cross_region_fallback_default (expected TRUE for pinned policies)

Risk Register (living)
1) False positives block legitimate traffic
- Likelihood: Medium (early rollout)
- Impact: High (traffic drop)
- Mitigation: dry-run mode; allowlist rollout; clear error messaging; on-call runbook; emergency fail-open (audited).
- Owner: Sean Gallagher + Amara Diallo

2) Streaming path returns generic 500 instead of policy error
- Likelihood: Medium (known regressions)
- Impact: High (support pain + poor UX)
- Mitigation: unit tests + E2E streaming coverage; ensure error contract mapping in streaming handler.
- Owner: Brian Murphy

3) Cross-region logging/tracing leaks customer content
- Likelihood: Low/Medium (depends on subsystem defaults)
- Impact: Critical (compliance breach)
- Mitigation: confirm log redaction + region-scoped sinks; block content from being attached to cross-region traces; security review.
- Owner: Marcus Lin + Logan Wright

4) Policy evaluation latency adds tail latency
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: Medium/High (SLO risk)
- Mitigation: cache effective policy at gateway (short TTL, e.g., 30–60s); precompute policy snapshot in auth token if feasible later; metrics + alert.
- Owner: Jordan Lee

5) Region mapping inconsistencies (internal region IDs vs cloud regions)
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: Medium (misroutes/blocks)
- Mitigation: single source mapping doc; validation at config-time; integration tests that assert routing uses canonical region IDs.
- Owner: Mina Haddad

6) Migration errors for existing customers (unexpected default pinning)
- Likelihood: Low (but high consequence)
- Impact: High
- Mitigation: explicit default = unrestricted; run dry-run fleet-wide to confirm block count = 0 pre-opt-in; staged migration; rollback path.
- Owner: Michael Nguyen

7) Customer expects cross-region availability semantics that are no longer true
- Likelihood: High (enterprise assumption)
- Impact: Medium/High
- Mitigation: docs + enablement + Console copy: “Pinning may reduce availability if region capacity constrained”; allow multi-allowed regions; Dedicated messaging alignment.
- Owner: Naomi Feldman + Markus Klein

Open Questions (need resolution before GA)
- Fail-open vs fail-closed behavior for pinned policies when policy service is unavailable (tiered? always fail-closed for pinned?). Proposed: fail-closed for pinned, fail-open for unrestricted; emergency override behind audited flag.
- Do we expose policy_id in public errors? (Product thread suggests yes for supportability; confirm final stance with Security.)
- Do we allow API key policy to expand allowed regions beyond org/project policy (i.e., key-level override to be less strict)? Proposed: no; key-level can only be equally or more restrictive. (Needs decision + implementation validation.)
- Dedicated alignment: do we reuse same policy model for “Dedicated Hosted Gateway” scenarios or keep separate? (Out of scope for initial but avoid painting into corner.)

Verification Scenarios (staging + prod allowlist)
- No policy (unrestricted): requests to any region behave exactly as today (no new blocks).
- Org pinned EU-only:
  - Request routed to EU primary region.
  - If EU capacity constrained: no fallback to US; returns correct error code.
  - Streaming: returns same error contract (not 500) when blocked.
- Multi-allowed (EU+US), primary EU:
  - Normal: routes to EU.
  - If EU constrained: may fallback to US (allowed) and logs decision reason.
- API key override (more restrictive than org): key pinned to EU-west only while org is EU-only (multiple EU regions). Verify key routes only EU-west.
- Policy config invalid (primary not in allowed): must be rejected at write time in control plane; if forced via DB, runtime blocks w/ config_invalid.

Launch Checklist (go/no-go)
Engineering readiness
- [ ] Control plane policy CRUD + validation deployed to prod (behind feature flags if needed)
- [ ] Gateway enforces error contract on all endpoints (streaming + non-streaming) and includes request_id
- [ ] Routing enforcement respects allowed regions and does not cross-region fallback by default for pinned policies
- [ ] Decision context propagated end-to-end (gateway -> routing -> logs/metrics)
- [ ] Dry-run enabled and reviewed for at least 48 hours on internal allowlist (block rate within expectation)
- [ ] E2E tests in CI covering pinned EU scenario incl. streaming

SRE/Observability
- [ ] Dashboards live: blocked requests, policy eval latency, cross-region attempt counters
- [ ] Alerts configured + tuned (paging thresholds reviewed by SRE)
- [ ] Runbook for false positives published (triage steps + mitigation)

Security/GRC
- [ ] Threat model reviewed and signed off (Marcus + Aisha)
- [ ] Audit log events verified end-to-end (create/update/delete + override)
- [ ] Evidence pack draft links populated (dashboards, test runs, config history)

Product/Support readiness
- [ ] Console UX copy finalized for pinning + troubleshooting view
- [ ] Support macros prepared (how to interpret error_code; how to fix config)
- [ ] Docs PR ready/merged (public region pinning docs + troubleshooting)

Rollback / Mitigation Plan
- Immediate mitigation: disable residency_enforcement_enabled (global) OR remove org from allowlist.
- For pinned customers with live traffic: use residency_enforcement_fail_open only with Security approval; must emit audit event and notify on-call channel.
- If only streaming path broken: hotfix gateway streaming handler (do not relax routing constraints).
- Rollback criteria: sustained block rate > expected threshold for allowlist orgs, or any confirmed cross-region content leak, or p95/p99 latency regression beyond SLO.

Notes to self / next edits
- Add explicit metric names as they land in code (final prefix may change).
- Add a short “Customer rollout comms checklist” once Solutions Eng confirms template.
- Align on the ‘key policy cannot be less strict than org/project’ decision and encode in validation.
text:
Context / TL;DR
We are delivering “Hosted data residency enforcement” for the Hosted API as part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative. The work introduces customer-configurable region pinning (allowed region set + primary region), deterministic enforcement at request time (no cross-region routing/fallback unless explicitly allowed), and consistent error messaging + auditing.

This doc is the engineering execution plan (milestones, owners, dependencies, risks, and launch checklist). Source-of-truth for requirements + public contract is Confluence PRD + ADRs. This plan is intended to be updated as we learn during rollout.

Goals (engineering interpretation)
1) Deterministic request-time enforcement
- Given org/project/key residency policy, the system should either:
  a) route to a compliant region and continue, or
  b) block the request with a stable error contract.
- “Compliant” includes routing decisions, fallback decisions, background processing that touches customer content, and logging/tracing boundaries per ADR-021.

2) Clear errors + consistent behavior across endpoints
- Identical error payload shape + status across streaming and non-streaming.
- SDKs should surface a dedicated error class (separate workstream but contract must be stable).

3) Auditability + observability
- Emit audit log events for residency policy config changes.
- Emit decision logs/metrics for runtime enforcement outcomes (allowed/blocked; reason; selected region).

Non-goals (initial)
- Multi-region replication of customer content.
- Complex per-subsystem residency rules beyond routing/gateway/logging boundaries (we’ll harden over time).

Owners / Workstreams
- Control plane residency policy service + schema + migrations: Ethan Park (lead), Benji Okafor (impl lead), Michael Nguyen (migrations)
- Policy evaluation / precedence logic: Jordan Lee (architect)
- API key & auth integration: Kaitlyn Nguyen
- Smart routing enforcement (incl. fallback rules): Mina Haddad (lead), Ravi Menon (architect), Elena Popov (impl)
- API gateway error contract + request validation: Amara Diallo (lead), Brian Murphy (error contract owner)
- Audit logs / eventing: Logan Wright
- Metrics/dashboards: Allison Grant (standards), Sean Gallagher (SRE review)
- Security review / threat model sign-off: Marcus Lin
- Product sign-off on UX + error copy: Naomi Feldman

Milestones & Timeline (target dates are placeholders; adjust as we firm up)
Phase 0 — Discovery / alignment (complete)
- Policy model + enforcement boundary confirmed in ADR-021 (DONE)
- Public error contract approved in ADR-022 (DONE)
- Initial stakeholder sign-offs: Product/Security/SRE (DONE, pending final GA go/no-go)

Phase 1 — Design + scaffolding (Jan 27 – Feb 07)
1.1 Control plane schema + admin APIs (Ethan/Benji)
- Define policy objects (org/project/key), fields, validation rules, precedence.
- Admin endpoints + Console integration stubs.
- Deliverable: PR 21483 (merged), follow-up tasks for edge validations.

1.2 Gateway error contract wiring (Amara/Brian)
- Unified error response for residency violations; ensure streaming path does not degrade to generic 500.
- Deliverable: PR 21533 (merged), regression tests pending.

1.3 Routing enforcement design + feature flags (Mina/Ravi)
- Enforce “allowed regions” filter on primary routing.
- Disable cross-region fallback by default.
- Add structured “residency decision context” to routing logs.
- Deliverable: PR 21507 (merged), rollout plan attached below.

Phase 2 — Implementation + integration (Feb 10 – Mar 07)
2.1 Migrations + defaults (Michael)
- Backfill default “unrestricted” policy for existing orgs OR explicit null policy treated as unrestricted (final decision in ADR-021; implement exactly).
- Ensure no behavior change for customers without policies.
- Add dry-run mode to compare “would block” counts before enforcement.

2.2 End-to-end enforcement plumbing (Jordan/Ethan/Amara/Mina)
- Gateway extracts identity (org/project/key), resolves effective policy (precedence), and attaches decision context.
- Routing layer consumes decision context and restricts region selection.
- Ensure downstream systems that could log customer content remain region-scoped (per ADR-021; for now: logs/traces redaction rules and regional storage).

2.3 Audit log events (Logan)
- Emit events for create/update/delete residency policies.
- Emit events for admin overrides (if we ship a break-glass flag).
- Wire into audit-log-exporter.
- Deliverable: PR 21561 (merged), verify export + retention.

2.4 Observability + dashboards (Allison/Sean)
- Metrics: policy_eval_latency_ms, residency_block_total, residency_allow_total, cross_region_route_attempt_total, fallback_block_total.
- Dimensions: org_id (hashed), project_id (hashed), policy_id, requested_region, selected_region, endpoint, error_code, reason.
- Dashboard + alert thresholds with SRE review.
- Deliverable: observability-pack PR 803 (merged), confirm naming consistency w/ telemetry standards.

2.5 E2E tests + CI gating (TBD owner; propose: Elena Popov + QA rotation)
- E2E suite: EU-only org hitting US region; streaming and non-streaming; fallback scenarios; policy precedence (key overrides project overrides org).
- Add CI gate for routing + gateway changes.
- Deliverable: ENG-4851.

Phase 3 — Rollout (Mar 10 – Apr 04)
3.1 Staging enablement + test orgs (SRE + Platform)
- Enable feature flags in staging.
- Seed test orgs/projects/keys:
  - EU-only (primary eu-central)
  - US-only (primary us-east)
  - Multi-allowed (EU+US) with primary EU
  - Unrestricted (null policy)
- Run verification scenarios (see checklist).

3.2 Internal dogfood (Redwood apps / internal consumers)
- Enable enforcement for internal orgs in one region first.
- Monitor blocked requests and policy eval latency.

3.3 Allowlist customer rollout
- Start with 1–3 friendly enterprise accounts (Solutions Eng coordination).
- Require customer acknowledgment that cross-region fallback is disabled unless explicitly allowed.
- Provide playbook for pinning misconfig.

3.4 Progressive GA
- Gradually widen allowlist by region.
- Default remains “unrestricted” unless customer opts in.

Phase 4 — GA + hardening (Apr 07+)
- Documentation finalized + customer comms templates.
- Security evidence pack v1.
- Post-launch retro + backlog for additional subsystem hardening.

Dependency Map (what blocks what)
A) Control plane policy service (ENG-4821)
- Blocks: gateway enforcement (needs policy resolution), routing enforcement (needs effective policy), Console UX.
- Key dependency: auth model for API key-scoped policies (Kaitlyn).

B) API gateway enforcement & error contract (ENG-4833)
- Blocks: consistent customer experience; supportability.
- Depends on: ADR-022 finalization; policy resolution contract; streaming response plumbing.

C) Smart routing enforcement (ENG-4827)
- Blocks: residency actually being guaranteed in Hosted routing layer.
- Depends on: region ID mapping (infra sheet), gateway passing decision context OR routing calling policy service (we are doing “gateway resolves, routing consumes” to reduce duplication).

D) Observability/metrics (ENG-4846)
- Blocks: rollout confidence + SRE go/no-go.
- Depends on: stable error codes and block reason taxonomy; logging pipeline fields.

E) Audit logs (ENG-4840)
- Blocks: enterprise security requirement + GRC evidence.
- Depends on: policy CRUD endpoints and identity context.

F) E2E tests (ENG-4851)
- Blocks: CI confidence + regression prevention.
- Depends on: end-to-end staging environment enablement and seeded policies.

Implementation Notes / Technical Decisions (condensed)
- Precedence: API key policy > project policy > org policy > unrestricted default. If a higher-precedence object exists but is invalid, treat as configuration error (block) rather than fallback to lower level (confirm with Product/Security).
- Primary region: used as preferred target if included in allowed set. If primary not in allowed set, validation should fail at config time; if it somehow occurs at runtime, block with config_invalid.
- Fallback behavior:
  - Default: no cross-region fallback if policy is pinned (allowed regions != “any”).
  - If allowed regions includes multiple regions, fallback within allowed set is allowed.
  - If routing cannot find capacity within allowed set, return residency/capacity error as specified (do not silently route outside).
- “Customer content” boundary per ADR-021: prompts, completions, embeddings inputs/outputs, tool/function payloads. Metadata allowed cross-region must be explicitly enumerated (request_id, timing metrics, non-content error codes).

Error Codes / Block Reasons (taxonomy we will implement)
(Exact payload per ADR-022; this is engineering mapping for metrics and runbooks.)
- RESIDENCY_REGION_NOT_ALLOWED: requested/selected region not in allowed set (or policy demands EU-only and request attempted US).
- RESIDENCY_FALLBACK_BLOCKED: routing attempted cross-region fallback but policy forbids.
- RESIDENCY_POLICY_NOT_FOUND: policy reference exists but missing (should be rare; indicates data integrity issue).
- RESIDENCY_POLICY_INVALID: policy config invalid (primary not in allowed, empty allowed list, unknown region id).
- RESIDENCY_ENFORCEMENT_TEMPORARY_UNAVAILABLE: policy evaluation dependency failure (we should strongly prefer fail-closed for pinned customers; decide per tier/flag).

Feature Flags / Rollout Controls
- residency_enforcement_enabled (global)
- residency_enforcement_allowlist_orgs (list)
- residency_enforcement_dry_run (log + metrics only, no blocking)
- residency_enforcement_fail_open (emergency only; should require Security approval; emits audit event)
- residency_disable_cross_region_fallback_default (expected TRUE for pinned policies)

Risk Register (living)
1) False positives block legitimate traffic
- Likelihood: Medium (early rollout)
- Impact: High (traffic drop)
- Mitigation: dry-run mode; allowlist rollout; clear error messaging; on-call runbook; emergency fail-open (audited).
- Owner: Sean Gallagher + Amara Diallo

2) Streaming path returns generic 500 instead of policy error
- Likelihood: Medium (known regressions)
- Impact: High (support pain + poor UX)
- Mitigation: unit tests + E2E streaming coverage; ensure error contract mapping in streaming handler.
- Owner: Brian Murphy

3) Cross-region logging/tracing leaks customer content
- Likelihood: Low/Medium (depends on subsystem defaults)
- Impact: Critical (compliance breach)
- Mitigation: confirm log redaction + region-scoped sinks; block content from being attached to cross-region traces; security review.
- Owner: Marcus Lin + Logan Wright

4) Policy evaluation latency adds tail latency
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: Medium/High (SLO risk)
- Mitigation: cache effective policy at gateway (short TTL, e.g., 30–60s); precompute policy snapshot in auth token if feasible later; metrics + alert.
- Owner: Jordan Lee

5) Region mapping inconsistencies (internal region IDs vs cloud regions)
- Likelihood: Medium
- Impact: Medium (misroutes/blocks)
- Mitigation: single source mapping doc; validation at config-time; integration tests that assert routing uses canonical region IDs.
- Owner: Mina Haddad

6) Migration errors for existing customers (unexpected default pinning)
- Likelihood: Low (but high consequence)
- Impact: High
- Mitigation: explicit default = unrestricted; run dry-run fleet-wide to confirm block count = 0 pre-opt-in; staged migration; rollback path.
- Owner: Michael Nguyen

7) Customer expects cross-region availability semantics that are no longer true
- Likelihood: High (enterprise assumption)
- Impact: Medium/High
- Mitigation: docs + enablement + Console copy: “Pinning may reduce availability if region capacity constrained”; allow multi-allowed regions; Dedicated messaging alignment.
- Owner: Naomi Feldman + Markus Klein

Open Questions (need resolution before GA)
- Fail-open vs fail-closed behavior for pinned policies when policy service is unavailable (tiered? always fail-closed for pinned?). Proposed: fail-closed for pinned, fail-open for unrestricted; emergency override behind audited flag.
- Do we expose policy_id in public errors? (Product thread suggests yes for supportability; confirm final stance with Security.)
- Do we allow API key policy to expand allowed regions beyond org/project policy (i.e., key-level override to be less strict)? Proposed: no; key-level can only be equally or more restrictive. (Needs decision + implementation validation.)
- Dedicated alignment: do we reuse same policy model for “Dedicated Hosted Gateway” scenarios or keep separate? (Out of scope for initial but avoid painting into corner.)

Verification Scenarios (staging + prod allowlist)
- No policy (unrestricted): requests to any region behave exactly as today (no new blocks).
- Org pinned EU-only:
  - Request routed to EU primary region.
  - If EU capacity constrained: no fallback to US; returns correct error code.
  - Streaming: returns same error contract (not 500) when blocked.
- Multi-allowed (EU+US), primary EU:
  - Normal: routes to EU.
  - If EU constrained: may fallback to US (allowed) and logs decision reason.
- API key override (more restrictive than org): key pinned to EU-west only while org is EU-only (multiple EU regions). Verify key routes only EU-west.
- Policy config invalid (primary not in allowed): must be rejected at write time in control plane; if forced via DB, runtime blocks w/ config_invalid.

Launch Checklist (go/no-go)
Engineering readiness
- [ ] Control plane policy CRUD + validation deployed to prod (behind feature flags if needed)
- [ ] Gateway enforces error contract on all endpoints (streaming + non-streaming) and includes request_id
- [ ] Routing enforcement respects allowed regions and does not cross-region fallback by default for pinned policies
- [ ] Decision context propagated end-to-end (gateway -> routing -> logs/metrics)
- [ ] Dry-run enabled and reviewed for at least 48 hours on internal allowlist (block rate within expectation)
- [ ] E2E tests in CI covering pinned EU scenario incl. streaming

SRE/Observability
- [ ] Dashboards live: blocked requests, policy eval latency, cross-region attempt counters
- [ ] Alerts configured + tuned (paging thresholds reviewed by SRE)
- [ ] Runbook for false positives published (triage steps + mitigation)

Security/GRC
- [ ] Threat model reviewed and signed off (Marcus + Aisha)
- [ ] Audit log events verified end-to-end (create/update/delete + override)
- [ ] Evidence pack draft links populated (dashboards, test runs, config history)

Product/Support readiness
- [ ] Console UX copy finalized for pinning + troubleshooting view
- [ ] Support macros prepared (how to interpret error_code; how to fix config)
- [ ] Docs PR ready/merged (public region pinning docs + troubleshooting)

Rollback / Mitigation Plan
- Immediate mitigation: disable residency_enforcement_enabled (global) OR remove org from allowlist.
- For pinned customers with live traffic: use residency_enforcement_fail_open only with Security approval; must emit audit event and notify on-call channel.
- If only streaming path broken: hotfix gateway streaming handler (do not relax routing constraints).
- Rollback criteria: sustained block rate > expected threshold for allowlist orgs, or any confirmed cross-region content leak, or p95/p99 latency regression beyond SLO.

Notes to self / next edits
- Add explicit metric names as they land in code (final prefix may change).

title: Onboarding Safety Attack Catalogue — Rolling Prompt Set for Regression Tests
preview: Working catalogue of adversarial and policy-sensitive prompts used for onboarding new model commits into Redwood's production serving. Focused on divergent failure modes (jailbreaks, PII exfiltration, self-harm instructions, malware creation). This doc is intentionally a compact, operational prompt bank + instructions for running nightly regression checks and triage steps.
