title: Stochastic sched + KV fuzz experiment diary
preview:
Summary:
This is a working diary of a set of experiments that combine probabilistic/sampled scheduling heuristics with aggressive KV-cache churn fuzzing. The goal: surface brittle codepaths in the scheduler + kernel selection logic that only trigger under realistic but low-frequency trace patterns. This doc collects test designs, microbench workloads, quick results, and regression artifacts for follow-up.

Motivation / Why this feels urgent:
- Multiple customer incidents (late-2024) triggered by rare sequences: long context prefixes + intermittent small requests caused the KV cache to thrash and the scheduler to choose high-latency kernels.
- Our existing probebench seeding covers common shapes but misses low-probability prefix reuse and interleaving patterns seen in production.
- We need a repeatable harness that stitches real traces with synthetic perturbations and stress-schedules to catch regressions in PRs that touch kernel selection, admission control, and KV eviction.

Primary objectives:
1) Build a harness that composes: real trace segments + synthetic churn + probabilistic admission policies.
2) Define a compact set of microbench scenarios and acceptance criteria (latency P99, median KV hit ratio, kernel-switch frequency).
3) Run nightly fuzz jobs that produce deterministic seeds for failures and attach artifacts to CI.
4) Produce clear regression tickets when flaky paths are found and a minimal repro that devs can run locally.

Hypotheses we want to test:
- H1: Stochastic admission with burst-probability p=0.02 causes kernel thrash on models with mixed seq-lens more often than deterministic round-robin when KV churn > 35%.
- H2: Small-token bursts (<8 tokens) interleaved with long prefix requests cause repeated KV evictions if epoch-based eviction is not tuned for mean request length.
- H3: A small scheduler prioritization change (preferring low-cost kernels for short requests) reduces tail latency but can increase KV miss storms for reused prefixes.

Harness design notes (implementation-level):
- Harness components:
  - Trace-store: directory of real production-derived segments (anonymized) labeled by shape: long-prefix, chat-burst, embed-batch, rerank-seq.
  - Synth-perturber: generator that inserts synthetic short requests at tunable frequency and skew (front/back of prefix).
  - Scheduler-simulator: in-process component that applies our live scheduler codepaths (admission, fairness, preempt) but driven by harness time.
  - Kernel-proxy: plumbs kernel-selection code without launching full GPU inference; returns modeled token latencies (configurable per-kernel).
  - KV-model: simplified approximation of our KV cache behavior (insert/evict counts, hot-set tracking) but deterministic for given seed.
- Determinism: all fuzz runs must accept a numeric seed. Seed -> deterministic perturbations + modeled random choices. Store seed in run metadata.
- Output artifacts per run: compressed trace (post-perturb), scheduler-event-log (text), kernel-selection-timeline (CSV), kv-metrics-summary (JSON), splunk/observability example logs (text).

Microbench scenarios (short list):
- scenario A: long-prefix warm (prefix len 1024 tokens) + jittered small requests at p=0.05 arriving at random offsets. Run for 2k requests.
  - acceptance: P99 < 120ms simulated kernel latency OR KV hit ratio >= 0.6.
- scenario B: mixed chat (short/medium) with sudden concurrency spikes (ramp to 160 qps for 10s). Measure kernel switch count per 1000 tokens.
- scenario C: embed-and-rerank mix: 80% embeds (batch=8) + 20% rerank (seq-len=512). Test quantization-kernel selection under memory pressure.
- scenario D: synthetic KV-thrash: repeated reuse of a tiny hot-set while background churn inserts new keys at rate r. Sweep r from 5% to 80% of total inserts.

Trace seeding protocol (how we pick and mutate traces):
- Start from labeled buckets (long-prefix, short-chat, multi-turn search) derived from anonymized production traces (contrib: LR-4110).
- For each run: pick base trace T, pick seed S, apply N perturbations where N ~ Poisson(lambda) (lambda configured per scenario).
- Perturbations include: random short-request insertion, token-drop (simulate client truncation), prefix-swap (replace end of prefix with another segment), and timing jitter (sub-ms to 200ms).
- Save the mutated trace and the exact perturbation list so developers can replay.

Regression capture + triage flow (lightweight playbook):
1) Job fails acceptance criteria -> CI artifact bucket gets tagged with seed + failing metric + job id.
2) On-call (eng-serving-runtime) triages within 24 hours: run local harness with seed, attempt to reduce scenario to minimal repro (binary search over perturbations).
3) If reproducible, open ticket with: failing seed, minimal trace, scheduler-event-log, and suggested suspects (PRs touching scheduler/kernel in last 30 days). Attach to INF-6723.
4) If nondeterministic, escalate to SRE with resource-level logs (must capture kernel allocation/exhaustion timeline).

Notable quick findings (experimental notes, raw):
- 2024-11-18 (seed 38219): Under stochastic admission p=0.03 we observed kernel-selection flips on every 3rd request for mixed seq workloads; root cause: admission queue timeouts were being used to prefer kernel A, which then caused higher KV miss due to different embedding layout. Repro seed saved.
- 2024-12-03 (seed 40501): Small bursts inserted at prefix tail cause consecutive evictions in epoch-evict policy if epoch window < 120s. Increasing epoch window reduced evictions but increased memory hold -> tradeoff.
- 2025-01-22 (seed 43320): Kernel-proxy latency modeling showed that switching to kernel B for short requests improved median by ~18% but increased P99 by 40% when hot-set reuse was >70%.

Observability and metrics to capture (recommended minimal set):
- scheduler.queue_wait_ms (histogram), kernel_switch_count (counter per request), kv.hit_ratio (per-request), kv.eviction_rate (per-second), kernel_churn_window (sliding count of unique kernels in last N requests), cpu/gpu_mem_pressure flags (binary).
- For each failing run include a small flamegraph of scheduler events and annotated kernel timeline.

Local repro tips for devs:
- harness binary supports: --seed, --scenario, --duration, --kernel-latency-map, --kv-policy. Example: ./harness --seed 40501 --scenario long-prefix-warm --duration 2000 --kv-policy epoch-180
- Reproducer script: scripts/repro-from-artifact.sh <bucket>/<run-id> will fetch artifacts and launch harness with saved seed/params.

Open questions / follow-ups:
- What is the right default epoch eviction window? (tradeoff: latency tail vs memory). Need cross-team discussion with infra (Marcus).
- Should kernel selection include a KV-hit feedback signal (i.e., prefer kernels that historically produced higher KV hit for a prefix)? Risk: feedback loops and oscillation. Need safe dampening strategy.
- Can we instrument a lightweight on-path KV-hit estimator to guide short-request tuning without full profiling overhead?

Next steps (2-week sprint scope):
- Build seed-to-PR link automation: when regression triggered, try to find PRs touching scheduler/kernel in 30d via GitHub API and include candidates in ticket. (owner: Asha)
- Add nightly fuzz job (scenario A & D) to run with 128 seeds/day and upload artifacts to the repro bucket (owner: Priya).
- Prototype kernel-selection feedback flag in kernel-proxy only (off-by-default) to evaluate oscillation risk (owner: Ruth).
- Schedule cross-team sync with infra and observability to finalize metric surface and retention (Marcus + Observability).

Notes to self / todo (scratchpad):
- need better naming for seeds vs runs (seed collisions confusing). Consider UUID per run but keep deterministic seed as param.
- improve harness docs: quickstart + docker image for local dev (low friction).
- correlate observed failures with customer incidents list (sales/customer-success) to prioritize fixing.

Attachments / artifacts to look at first:
- GH PR 814 (scheduler admission change) -- likely culprit for 2024-11 flip
- INF-6723 (regression owner tracking)
- LR-4110 (trace anonymization notes)

If you're reading this and want to reproduce a failure I mentioned above, ping me (Ethan) on #eng-serving-runtime with the seed id you care about and I will drop the run bundle in /repro/runs/<run-id>.

End of diary entry (draft).

title: SR Inference Candidate Scratchpad - Akash M.
preview:
Quick brain dump from interviews (loops with infra, runtime, and recruiting). Notes to self — unpolished.

Candidate: Akash Mehta (LinkedIn above)
Role applied: Senior ML Engineer, Inference Runtime
Stage: post-onsite (3 interviews)
Interviews:
  - Runtime deep-dive (Jorge) — 60m
  - Systems & scaling (Maya) — 45m
  - Product/ownership + culture (me) — 30m

Summary impressions (TL;DR):
  - Strong ML background (worked on decoder optimization + quantization).
  - Good systems intuition (cache eviction, KV management) but less exposure to our specific multi-tenant routing/fallback policies.
  - Pragmatic engineer, writes compact notes, asked good follow-ups about SLOs.

Scorecard (rough, for discussion):
  - Technical depth (inference kernels / quantization): 4/5
  - Systems design (scalability & reliability): 3.5/5
  - Ownership & product sense: 4/5
  - Communication / docs: 4/5
  - Cultural fit / collaboration: 4/5
  - Overall hire recommendation: Strong hire for infra-facing role; borderline for immediate owner of multi-tenant routing work (needs ramp on specific product).

Notes by interviewer:
  Jorge (runtime):
    - Asked about memory layout and cache management for KV cache — described approach using per-shard LRU with eviction hints, aware of shard-safety concerns.
    - Implemented custom attention kernel at previous company (reduced memory copy by pinning buffers). Shared benchmarks (claims ~20% throughput improvement). Ask for PR link.
    - Follow-up: verify claim with provided repo/PR (linked_artifacts PR above maybe related) — need to validate reproducibility.

  Maya (systems & scaling):
    - Good on autoscaling tradeoffs, suggested hybrid metrics: queue latency + GPU utilization for scaling decisions.
    - Less concrete on cross-tenant fairness: didn't propose concrete algorithms for routing under capacity constraints (e.g., token-level pacing or backpressure).
    - Suggested a staged rollout approach (canary -> weighted routing -> auto-fallback) which maps to our console rollouts; positive.

  Ethan / Product (me):
    - Discussed ownership style. Akash prefers small teams, code-first, likes shipping infra primitives.
    - Asked about SLAs and incident playbooks. Has experience owning on-call for model serving clusters.

Concrete technical takeaways / follow-ups:
  - Ask candidate for PR/benchmark artifacts for custom attention kernel (Jorge requested). If provided, run quick review and a small repro on our infra sandbox. (TODO: assign to infra-intern for quick sanity-run).
  - Get clarifying examples of latency SLOs they had to hit and how they measured tail latency (p95/p99 strategies).
  - One design question to re-run in debrief: routing by cost vs latency. He favored cost-aware routing with latency guards; need to evaluate how that interacts with our automatic fallback policy.

Risks / Gaps:
  - Limited exposure to multi-tenant billing/rate-limiting edge cases (tokens accounting, prefix caching when clients migrate).
  - Not super familiar with our specific compilation/kernel selection heuristics — has experience but not same toolchain (e.g., his work used custom CUDA kernels; we use mix of Triton/XLA/optimized kernels). Ramp required.

Red flags: none obvious. No concerning behavioral signals.

Suggested next step:
  - Proceed to call with Eng Manager (final loop) focusing on:
      * Deep dive on a past project: request code & benchmarks (attention kernel) and ask candidate to walk us through tradeoffs.
      * Scenario exercise: design a routing policy that preserves latency SLO for tiered customers while minimizing cost; specify metrics + fallback behavior.
  - Compensation band: put in the candidate band for Senior (~$180-230k range + equity) — confirm with recruiting comp grid.

Notes-to-self / admin: 
  - Put a short summary (2-3 lines) into Greenhouse/ATS: "Strong infra-oriented ML engineer. Excellent kernel-level chops; needs product ramp for multi-tenant routing but good owner. Recommend Eng Manager final loop."
  - Add PR link when candidate shares (tag: JIRA-RI-3421 in linked_artifacts).
  - Sync with Maya and Jorge tomorrow 10:00am PT for quick calibration (we may have small variance on scoring).

Open questions I want answered before final decision:
  - Can they translate micro-optimizations into reproducible gains across model sizes (small -> long-sequence)? Examples?
  - How comfortable are they with production-grade rollout practices (canary health metrics, automated rollback)? Past examples?

Random scratch ideas mentioned in interview (might be useful for product):
  - "sequence-length aware kernel selection" rule engine — small DSL to pick kernels at runtime based on seq-dist histogram
  - auto-annotating prompts to leverage prefix cache more aggressively (but safety around cache leakage)

Mood: candidate enthusiastic, curious. Likes Redwood's product stance (unified runtime across deploy modes).

TODO list:
  - [ ] Request candidate's PRs + benchmark artifacts (owner: Ethan)
  - [ ] Draft short ATS summary + send to recruiting (owner: Ethan)
  - [ ] Schedule Eng Manager final loop (owner: recruiting)
  - [ ] If PR looks good, kick off small repro on sandbox infra (owner: infra intern / Jorge)

End of scratchpad.

(Notes are rough — intended to be trimmed and formalized into ATS feedback after follow-ups.)

title: Microburst credit pools and soft preemption: PRD sketch
preview:
Context

This is a working sketch for a dedicated-capacity feature set that aims to let tenants absorb short, high-concurrency demand spikes without large long-term commit increases while preserving tenant isolation and predictable SLOs. Unlike hard evictions or purely queue-based throttles, the idea is a two-tier credit + soft-preemption model that gracefully degrades noncritical capacity, shifts to cheaper model variants, and uses shadow pools for observed microbursts.

Problem statement

- Customers want predictable latency and capacity for steady traffic but also need to support occasional high-concurrency bursts (cron spikes, flash events, promotion traffic).
- Buying large reserved capacity for rare bursts is expensive and underutilized.
- Current approaches (strict tenant quotas, aggressive queuing, or hard eviction) cause poor UX or noisy neighbor concerns.

Design goals

1) Absorb short spikes (seconds->minutes) with minimal latency regression for primary requests.
2) Keep steady-state reserved capacity small and predictable.
3) Preserve tenant isolation and avoid unbounded cross-tenant impact.
4) Make behavior transparent and controllable by tenant (config + observability).

Non-goals

- Not addressing long-term sustained increases (minutes -> hours). For that use reserved capacity top-ups or scaling commitments.
- Not replacing cost-aware routing across regions — this focuses on single-pool behavior.

Feature overview

1) Microburst Credit Pool (MCP)
   - Each tenant gets a baseline of burst credits tied to their reserved capacity tier and historical burst profile.
   - Credits are consumed for requests that exceed the tenant's baseline throughput. Consumption weight factors: model size, sequence length, concurrency slot cost.
   - Credits auto-refill at a configurable rate (e.g., 10% of baseline per 5 minutes), with a maximum cap (e.g., 3x baseline).

2) Shadow Burst Pool
   - Cluster-level ephemeral nodes that accept excess requests when MCP is available and cluster has slack. Shadow pool uses opportunistic capacity and is tagged to tenants consuming MCP.
   - Shadow nodes run prewarmed lightweight runners and can preferentially host smaller model variants or quantized kernels to reduce cost.

3) Soft preemption and graceful degradation
   - When MCP is exhausted and cluster cannot provide shadow capacity, degrade noncritical work first: asynchronous batch jobs, long-running reranks, low-priority routes.
   - For critical generate/chat requests, provide configurable fallbacks: smaller model family, lower temperature, or truncated context. Fall back policy is tenant-configurable with safe defaults.

4) Tenant isolation & fairness controls
   - Per-tenant burst throttles with leak-bucket semantics to limit instantaneous amplification.
   - Cross-tenant shard fencing: shadow pool assigns tenancy lanes so one tenant cannot monopolize all shadow nodes.

5) Economics & billing signals
   - Burst consumption recorded separately and visible in tenant billing dashboard. Option to convert burst usage into an on-demand invoice item or automatically bill from committed headroom credits.

API & configuration sketch

- Tenant config fields (example):
  - reserved_capacity_units: int
  - microburst_credit_cap: percent_of_baseline_or_absolute
  - microburst_refill_rate: per_minute
  - fallback_policy: ["smaller_model","truncate_context","queue_with_deadline"]
  - allow_shadow_pool: bool
  - burst_priority: [low,normal,high]

Operational flow (simplified)

1) Incoming request evaluated against tenant baseline throughput.
2) If over baseline, attempt to charge MCP. If credits available: route to primary pool if healthy, else try shadow pool.
3) If no credits or shadow capacity unavailable: apply fallback_policy order.
4) Emit events for observability: MCP_CONSUME, MCP_REFILL, SHADOW_ALLOC, FALLBACK_APPLIED, EVICTED_DUE_TO_LONG_SUSTAIN.

Metrics & SLOs

- Target: absorb >= 90% of microburst bursts under 2 minutes without falling back for tenants with MCP headroom.
- MCP utilization (per-tenant): wanted under 60% during typical week for predictable refill behavior.
- Shadow pool prewarm hit-rate: target 80% for accepted overflow requests.
- Failures fallback rate: <5% of burst requests for high-priority tenants.

Open tradeoffs and considerations

- Credit sizing: fixed formula (percent of baseline) vs. adaptive based on historical variance. Adaptive reduces waste but is more complex and needs safeguards against gaming.
- Fairness vs. utilization: strict fairness reduces cluster utilization; relaxed fairness boosts throughput but could risk noisy neighbor impact. Compromise: per-tenant lane quotas in shadow pool with occasional elastic overflow.
- Complexity for tenants: adding too many knobs may confuse teams. Provide sane defaults and a one-click "burst-friendly" profile.

Failure modes and mitigation

- Sustained spike longer than MCP refill -> automatic recommendation to tenant to purchase short-duration reserved capacity or temporarily increase commit. Also auto-escalate to on-call if errors cross threshold.
- Shadow pool overload -> degrade to fallback_policy queue with deadline and notify tenant.
- Billing surprises -> surface simulated forecasting charts in console before burst events and caps to prevent runaway charges.

Experiment & rollout plan

- Phase 0 (lab sims): run Monte Carlo sims against historical tenant traffic to validate refill heuristics (2 weeks).
- Phase 1 (internal beta): enable MCP + shadow pool for a handful of internal accounts and small customers, monitor metrics (4 weeks).
- Phase 2 (opt-in customers): invite customers to opt into burst profile with UI controls, collect feedback, iterate (8 weeks).
- Phase 3 (default availability): enable on by default for select commitment tiers with clear opt-out.

Runbook snippets (for SRE)

- MCP anomalies: if MCP_CONSUME rate > 3x normal for >5m, check tenant traffic source, run occupancy heatmap, consider temporary per-tenant cap until owner responds.
- Shadow node failure: remove node, redistribute prewarm cache, alert and spin replacement; reduce shadow allocation rate until pool healthy.

Open questions / TODOs

- Simulation: pick representative 30 tenants across enterprise and scale to stress-test shared shadow pools.
- Pricing model: per-credit cost vs. pay-as-you-go. Which aligns with sales incentives?
- UI: controls, thresholds, and telemetry surfaces in Redwood Console. What visual affordances reduce support load?
- Security: ensure shadow nodes do not leak KV cache between tenants; require strict cache invalidation and per-tenant encryption keys.

Notes to self

- Talk to Jesse (billing) and Sana (console) about billing visibility and UI mockups.
- Schedule sync with SRE to review observability events schema.
- Create Linear tickets for sim harness and shadow-node autoscaler prototype.
text:
Context

This is a working sketch for a dedicated-capacity feature set that aims to let tenants absorb short, high-concurrency demand spikes without large long-term commit increases while preserving tenant isolation and predictable SLOs. Unlike hard evictions or purely queue-based throttles, the idea is a two-tier credit + soft-preemption model that gracefully degrades noncritical capacity, shifts to cheaper model variants, and uses shadow pools for observed microbursts.

Problem statement

- Customers want predictable latency and capacity for steady traffic but also need to support occasional high-concurrency bursts (cron spikes, flash events, promotion traffic).
- Buying large reserved capacity for rare bursts is expensive and underutilized.
- Current approaches (strict tenant quotas, aggressive queuing, or hard eviction) cause poor UX or noisy neighbor concerns.

Design goals

1) Absorb short spikes (seconds->minutes) with minimal latency regression for primary requests.
2) Keep steady-state reserved capacity small and predictable.
3) Preserve tenant isolation and avoid unbounded cross-tenant impact.
4) Make behavior transparent and controllable by tenant (config + observability).

Non-goals

- Not addressing long-term sustained increases (minutes -> hours). For that use reserved capacity top-ups or scaling commitments.
- Not replacing cost-aware routing across regions — this focuses on single-pool behavior.

Feature overview

1) Microburst Credit Pool (MCP)
   - Each tenant gets a baseline of burst credits tied to their reserved capacity tier and historical burst profile.
   - Credits are consumed for requests that exceed the tenant's baseline throughput. Consumption weight factors: model size, sequence length, concurrency slot cost.
   - Credits auto-refill at a configurable rate (e.g., 10% of baseline per 5 minutes), with a maximum cap (e.g., 3x baseline).

2) Shadow Burst Pool
   - Cluster-level ephemeral nodes that accept excess requests when MCP is available and cluster has slack. Shadow pool uses opportunistic capacity and is tagged to tenants consuming MCP.
   - Shadow nodes run prewarmed lightweight runners and can preferentially host smaller model variants or quantized kernels to reduce cost.

3) Soft preemption and graceful degradation
   - When MCP is exhausted and cluster cannot provide shadow capacity, degrade noncritical work first: asynchronous batch jobs, long-running reranks, low-priority routes.
   - For critical generate/chat requests, provide configurable fallbacks: smaller model family, lower temperature, or truncated context. Fall back policy is tenant-configurable with safe defaults.

4) Tenant isolation & fairness controls
   - Per-tenant burst throttles with leak-bucket semantics to limit instantaneous amplification.
   - Cross-tenant shard fencing: shadow pool assigns tenancy lanes so one tenant cannot monopolize all shadow nodes.

5) Economics & billing signals
   - Burst consumption recorded separately and visible in tenant billing dashboard. Option to convert burst usage into an on-demand invoice item or automatically bill from committed headroom credits.

API & configuration sketch

- Tenant config fields (example):
  - reserved_capacity_units: int
  - microburst_credit_cap: percent_of_baseline_or_absolute
  - microburst_refill_rate: per_minute
  - fallback_policy: ["smaller_model","truncate_context","queue_with_deadline"]
  - allow_shadow_pool: bool
  - burst_priority: [low,normal,high]

Operational flow (simplified)

1) Incoming request evaluated against tenant baseline throughput.
2) If over baseline, attempt to charge MCP. If credits available: route to primary pool if healthy, else try shadow pool.
3) If no credits or shadow capacity unavailable: apply fallback_policy order.
4) Emit events for observability: MCP_CONSUME, MCP_REFILL, SHADOW_ALLOC, FALLBACK_APPLIED, EVICTED_DUE_TO_LONG_SUSTAIN.

Metrics & SLOs

- Target: absorb >= 90% of microburst bursts under 2 minutes without falling back for tenants with MCP headroom.
- MCP utilization (per-tenant): wanted under 60% during typical week for predictable refill behavior.
- Shadow pool prewarm hit-rate: target 80% for accepted overflow requests.
- Failures fallback rate: <5% of burst requests for high-priority tenants.

Open tradeoffs and considerations

- Credit sizing: fixed formula (percent of baseline) vs. adaptive based on historical variance. Adaptive reduces waste but is more complex and needs safeguards against gaming.
- Fairness vs. utilization: strict fairness reduces cluster utilization; relaxed fairness boosts throughput but could risk noisy neighbor impact. Compromise: per-tenant lane quotas in shadow pool with occasional elastic overflow.
- Complexity for tenants: adding too many knobs may confuse teams. Provide sane defaults and a one-click "burst-friendly" profile.

Failure modes and mitigation

- Sustained spike longer than MCP refill -> automatic recommendation to tenant to purchase short-duration reserved capacity or temporarily increase commit. Also auto-escalate to on-call if errors cross threshold.
- Shadow pool overload -> degrade to fallback_policy queue with deadline and notify tenant.
- Billing surprises -> surface simulated forecasting charts in console before burst events and caps to prevent runaway charges.

Experiment & rollout plan

- Phase 0 (lab sims): run Monte Carlo sims against historical tenant traffic to validate refill heuristics (2 weeks).
- Phase 1 (internal beta): enable MCP + shadow pool for a handful of internal accounts and small customers, monitor metrics (4 weeks).
- Phase 2 (opt-in customers): invite customers to opt into burst profile with UI controls, collect feedback, iterate (8 weeks).
- Phase 3 (default availability): enable on by default for select commitment tiers with clear opt-out.

Runbook snippets (for SRE)

- MCP anomalies: if MCP_CONSUME rate > 3x normal for >5m, check tenant traffic source, run occupancy heatmap, consider temporary per-tenant cap until owner responds.
- Shadow node failure: remove node, redistribute prewarm cache, alert and spin replacement; reduce shadow allocation rate until pool healthy.

Open questions / TODOs

- Simulation: pick representative 30 tenants across enterprise and scale to stress-test shared shadow pools.
- Pricing model: per-credit cost vs. pay-as-you-go. Which aligns with sales incentives?
- UI: controls, thresholds, and telemetry surfaces in Redwood Console. What visual affordances reduce support load?
- Security: ensure shadow nodes do not leak KV cache between tenants; require strict cache invalidation and per-tenant encryption keys.

Notes to self

- Talk to Jesse (billing) and Sana (console) about billing visibility and UI mockups.
- Schedule sync with SRE to review observability events schema.
- Create Linear tickets for sim harness and shadow-node autoscaler prototype.

title: region-aware-sku-variability-and-cache-thresholds
preview:
Summary:

Quick working notes from the cross-region/cross-sku runs we kicked off to understand how driver + CUDA + region placement interact with cache thresholds and observed tail noise. Goal: find stable config knobs for Dedicated and Private deployments (default kernel + cache thresholds) that generalize across AWS p4/p5 and GCP L4/compute SKUs.

What we ran (matrix):
- SKUs: AWS p4d (A100), AWS p5 (H100), GCP a2/a2-megagpu (A100 variants), GCP l4d (L40S).
- CUDA/driver combos: CUDA 11.8 / NVIDIA driver 535.xx, CUDA 12.2 / driver 545.xx, and a legacy 470.xx on A100 for historical comparison.
- Workloads: 8k-context autoregressive generation, batch sizes {1, 4, 8}, pipelined streaming (1 token at a time) vs batched token append, KV-cache enabled vs disabled, warm caches vs cold-start, and long-concat (incremental context growth up to 32k).
- Metrics captured: p50/p95/p99 per-token latency, end-to-end request latency, GPU util %, SM/Tensor op breakdown (sampled), memory margin (GB free at peak), throughput (tokens/sec), power when available, and cost-per-1M tokens using region hourly prices. Collected Nsight traces for failure cases.

Representative numbers (median across 3 runs each, batch=1 streaming, 8k context):
- A100 (AWS p4d, CUDA11.8/driver535): p50=48ms, p95=128ms, p99=305ms, throughput=1.7k tokens/s, GPU util=72%, mem_margin=8GB, est cost/1M tokens=$11.8.
- H100 (AWS p5, CUDA12.2/driver545): p50=36ms, p95=92ms, p99=248ms, throughput=2.3k tokens/s, GPU util=82%, mem_margin=6GB, est cost/1M tokens=$17.5.
- L40S (GCP l4d, CUDA12.2/driver545): p50=62ms, p95=155ms, p99=412ms, throughput=1.5k tokens/s, GPU util=66%, mem_margin=10GB, est cost/1M tokens=$8.9.

Immediate observations (working):
- H100 consistently gives the best p50/p95 and throughput for long contexts when using CUDA12.2 + driver545. Tensor-core kernels scaled well.
- A100 with older driver (535) is stable for p95 but has heavier tail amplification under mixed workloads (concurrent KV warmups + long context churn). We saw p99 spikes correlating with CPU-side kernel launch serialization in the driver.
- L40S has the best cost/1M-token but worse latency and much wider tail on p99 for streaming workloads — appears to be driven by larger kernel launch variance for small-batch streaming on L40S runtime image.
- Driver version matters: H100 on driver 535 had strange kernel fallbacks for matmul where 32-bit kernels were used instead of 8-bit math on certain fused ops, causing ~18–30% slowdown vs driver545.
- Region placement (us-east vs europe-west) introduced ~4–6ms median extra latency for requests that required cross-AZ controller hops (private control plane placement). This is network/control-plane overhead, not GPU perf, but it amplifies p99 when controller retries occur.

Anomalies we captured:
- AWS p5 with driver535: intermittent 40–80ms micro-pauses at p99; Nsight traces show occasional fallback to CPU memcpy path after kernel enqueue. Root cause hypothesis: a driver scheduling bug when concurrent CUDA streams exceed a threshold with our batching pattern.
- GCP l4d small-batch jitter: NVLINK peer-to-peer fallbacks triggered on certain host kernel sizes; when we limited host-to-device pinned buffer sizes to <16MB the jitter reduced.
- A100 legacy driver 470: very predictable p50 but very poor throughput on long-context KV cache misses (up to 35% slower).

On-cache-threshold experiments (automatic prefix caching):
- We tried three cache cutoff heuristics: (A) Always cache prefixes, (B) cache only when prefix reuse > 0.25, (C) size-aware cache where we only cache prefixes if estimated memory cost < 12% of GPU memory.
- Results: (A) best for raw latency p50 but highest memory pressure; (C) best tradeoff across SKUs (kept mem_margin >= 6GB on all SKUs, p95 increased slightly on L40S).
- Recommendation: default to size-aware cache with per-SKU memory multiplier (H100 multiplier 0.9, A100 1.0, L40S 0.8) to avoid accidental OOMs in multi-tenant Dedicated pools.

Profiling commands / quick reproducibility notes:
- Workload runner: ./tools/runner --model redwood/trl-8k --batch 1 --context 8192 --mode streaming --repeat 3 --cuda 12.2
- Trace capture (when weird p99 seen): CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 nsight-sys --capture-range=cudaProfilerApi --output trace.qdrep -- ./tools/runner ...
- Kernel list sample: ncu --target-processes all --kernel-regex "matmul|softmax" --launch-skip 5 --launch-count 200 ./tools/runner ...

Immediate action items (short term):
- (Ethan) File ENGINF ticket for p5-driver-535 micro-pause with attached nsight trace and initial hypothesis. Priority P0 for Dedicated customers on p5.
- (Maya) Add per-SKU default cache multiplier to serving config and smoke-test with 50 simultaneous connections on p4/p5/l4d images.
- (Liam) Re-run H100 on driver545 with strict CPU pinning (isolcpus) to rule out host-side scheduling interplay.
- (Team) Add a daily cron that runs lightweight latency checker (batch=1 streaming 1k context) across our supported SKUs and posts p50/p95/p99 to /internal/benchmarks. This will catch regressions from driver rollouts.

Longer-term ideas / investigation threads:
- Kernel selection tracing: instrument the runtime to log which kernel variant is chosen for matmul/softmax and surface that in the console per-model per-SKU. This would make it obvious when a driver causes a fallback.
- Autoscaler safety: when mem_margin < X across pool, automatically throttle cache or reduce batching to preserve latency SLOs.
- Cost/perf curves: generate an expanded table (region x SKU x CUDA-driver) with performance-per-dollar normalized to p50*throughput/cost. We'll use it for product guidance (hosted vs dedicated).

Notes to self (scratchpad):
- Avoid changing CUDA-major version mid-rollout without a canary on a small dedicated pool — saw 545 -> nice perf, but also introduced one regression on an obscure kernel path.
- Check RPC timeout interplay: controller retries often line up with driver micro-pauses; temporarily increasing control-plane retry backoff reduced visible p99 spikes in user-facing traces (but masks root cause).

Appendix (raw short table for quick copy):
SKU,CUDA,DRIVER,p50_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms,throughput_tok_s,gpu_util_pct,mem_margin_GB,cost_per_1M
aws-p4d,a100,11.8,48,128,305,1700,72,8,11.8
aws-p5,h100,12.2,36,92,248,2300,82,6,17.5
gcp-l4d,l40s,12.2,62,155,412,1500,66,10,8.9

Next sync: run the size-aware cache multiplier experiment across regions (us-east-1, us-west-2, europe-west1) next week and re-evaluate after driver545 is rolled to the p4 fleet.

Questions we still have / unresolved:
- Is the p5-driver-535 fallback reproducible with a synthetic microkernel-only workload? If yes, we can draft a repro for NVIDIA.
- Does L40S jitter correlate with host kernel ABI differences across GCP images? Need to test a vanilla container with our runtime stripped down.

End of notes.

(Informal — will clean and convert to a short one-pager once items are actioned.)

title: persona-message-matrix-engineering-triage
preview:
Purpose:
Quick, tactical playbook to help SDRs, AEs, and Solutions Engineers tailor short talk tracks and demo hooks to three overlapping engineering personas: ML engineers, backend engineers, and platform teams. This is intentionally compact — meant for call prep and one-pagers, not the canonical persona library.

TL;DR:
- ML engineers care about model fidelity, reproducibility, latency for interactive workflows, and tooling to iterate on prompts/evals.
- Backend engineers care about predictable latency, simple API semantics, and cost per token/unit under load.
- Platform teams care about multi-tenant controls, rollout/upgrade automation, observability, and procurement/SLA details.

Positioning thesis (one line each):
- ML engineer: \"Ship higher-quality LLM features faster by giving you a reproducible serving environment and first-class evals so model regressions become a solved workflow.\"
- Backend engineer: \"Deliver consistent latency at scale with a single API that gives you routing, caching, and predictable billing so your services stay within SLOs.\"
- Platform team: \"Standardize inference across teams with predictable capacity, governance controls, and rollout policies that integrate with your existing infra and security requirements.\"

Core persona vectors (how to prioritize messaging during a short 2–3 minute pitch):
1) ML engineer (fast path)
- Primary concerns: output quality, iteration velocity (evals/prompting), reproducibility, KV cache and context handling, experimenting with quantization/model variants.
- Key proof points: model catalog with verified performance profiles; quick eval harness; redwood Optimize recommendations; reproducible rollouts (pin model versions to experiments).
- Objections & responses:
  - \"We already run local experiments / huggingface models\": \"Great — Redwood plugs into that workflow: same model artifacts can be served with optimized kernels and automatic KV caching so you get interactive latency without rewriting your infra.\"
  - \"Quality varies across quantized variants\": \"We publish model+quantization quality deltas and provide A/B eval tooling so you can guardrails regressions before rollout.\"
- Discovery questions:
  - \"How often do you iterate prompts vs change model weights?\"
  - \"Do you have automated evals tied to CI or is it manual?\"
- Demo hooks: live eval dashboard showing a regression alert, quick 2-click canary rollback to previous model version.

2) Backend engineer (fast path)
- Primary concerns: latency tail, predictable throughput under burst, simple API integration, error handling and fallbacks.
- Key proof points: continuous batching, smart routing (fallback to cheaper model variant), token-level latency and cost breakdown in Console.
- Objections & responses:
  - \"We care about P95 latency spikes\": \"Redwood offers routing by latency SLO and autoscaling policies plus transparent per-route tracing to diagnose hot paths.\"
  - \"We already run inference in our services\": \"Consider Redwood as a predictability and ops layer: same HTTP/SDK interface but with caching, batching, and fallback policies you don't have to build and maintain.\"
- Discovery questions:
  - \"What percent of calls are < 1s vs > 1s today? Any hot keys or long-tail inputs?\"
  - \"How do you currently handle failover for model timeouts?\"
- Demo hooks: per-route latency breakdown; trace from request->model showing batching and cache hits; quick toggle of fallback policy.

3) Platform teams (fast path)
- Primary concerns: multi-tenant isolation, auditing/compliance, rollout governance, predictable cost across org, friendly procurement path (Dedicated/Private).
- Key proof points: Private deployment with customer-managed control plane, KMS/HSM integration, SLA-backed Dedicated tiers, RBAC and audit logs in Console.
- Objections & responses:
  - \"We have strict security controls and can't use hosted APIs\": \"Private offers an on-prem or VPC option with pluggable KMS/HSM plus an admin console that surfaces audit trails. We also provide a limited air-gapped bundle for regulated customers.\"
  - \"Vendor lock-in and migration risk\": \"Redwood exposes consistent APIs across hosted/dedicated/private and supports model artifacts you already use to minimize migration work.\"
- Discovery questions:
  - \"Do you have procurement constraints that require dedicated capacity contracts?\"
  - \"Which compliance frameworks are must-haves for a launch? e.g., SOC2, ISO, regional data residency?\"
- Demo hooks: show RBAC + audit trail for a sensitive model, show policy-driven routing by region/compliance, show Dedicated capacity contract UI.

Competitive quick-map (notes to salesperson):
- Managed LLM APIs (e.g., generalist cloud providers): strength = simplicity & scale; weakness = limited control over rollout, fewer model transparency guarantees. Message: emphasize Redwood’s model observability and rollout controls.
- Self-hosted runtimes (open-source runtimes / infra): strength = customizability; weakness = ops burden and unpredictable unit economics. Message: focus on \"production economics\" and offload of operational complexity while preserving model ownership.
- Inference vendors with tight hardware lock-ins: strength = optimized throughput; weakness = vendor/hardware lock and limited model support. Message: highlight our compilation/kernel selection that adapts to different hardware and route-by-policy to avoid lock-in.

Sample 90-second talk tracks (pick one per persona):
- ML engineer (90s): \"Hi — quick question: when you push new prompt sets or model fine-tunes, how confident are you that production quality won't regress? Redwood gives you a serving environment that connects to your model artifacts and runs automated evals and canary rollouts. You can see token-level diffs, quantization impact, and if anything goes wrong we automatically fall back to a safe variant — so you can iterate models faster without surprise defects in prod.\"

- Backend engineer (90s): \"Most infra teams I talk to are wrestling with unpredictable latencies when models get big. Redwood provides a single API that gives deterministic latency SLOs via batching and cache-aware serving. We also surface per-route cost and latency so you can budget and shard traffic predictably. Integration is an HTTP SDK and a few toggles for routing/fallback.\"

- Platform team (90s): \"For platform owners, your top ask is governance plus predictable ops. Redwood supports Dedicated capacity, private control planes, RBAC, audit logs, and KMS integration — and the exact same API your teams use in hosted mode. That means you can standardize inference across orgs without blocking model experimentation.\"

Playbook snippets (cheat sheet for quick prep):
- If hearing \"cost\": lead with Optimize recommendations and token-level cost UI; offer a batch-configuration review.
- If hearing \"quality/regression\": call out evals, model version pinning, and rollback tooling.
- If hearing \"security/compliance\": surface Private + audit + KMS and link to SOC2 summary doc.

Open questions / follow-ups to capture in CRM:
- Which model families do they use in prod? (gpt-style, instruction-tuned, web-scale LLMs)
- Current annual inference spend and target reduction %
- Existing compliance requirements and timelines
- Who owns procurement vs who owns technical eval/test sign-off?

References and quick links for reps:
- Confluence: performance-benchmarks-2024 (internal)
- Slack: #g2m-personas for playbook updates
- Jira: GTM-4321 (persona launch tasks)
- Demo deck PR: github.com/repo/marketing/playbooks/pulls/12

Notes for improvements (do before next iteration):
- Add 2–3 short video clips (30–45s) showing the demo hooks for each persona.

text:
Purpose:
Quick, tactical playbook to help SDRs, AEs, and Solutions Engineers tailor short talk tracks and demo hooks to three overlapping engineering personas: ML engineers, backend engineers, and platform teams. This is intentionally compact — meant for call prep and one-pagers, not the canonical persona library.

TL;DR:
- ML engineers care about model fidelity, reproducibility, latency for interactive workflows, and tooling to iterate on prompts/evals.
- Backend engineers care about predictable latency, simple API semantics, and cost per token/unit under load.
- Platform teams care about multi-tenant controls, rollout/upgrade automation, observability, and procurement/SLA details.

Positioning thesis (one line each):
- ML engineer: \"Ship higher-quality LLM features faster by giving you a reproducible serving environment and first-class evals so model regressions become a solved workflow.\"
- Backend engineer: \"Deliver consistent latency at scale with a single API that gives you routing, caching, and predictable billing so your services stay within SLOs.\"
- Platform team: \"Standardize inference across teams with predictable capacity, governance controls, and rollout policies that integrate with your existing infra and security requirements.\"

Core persona vectors (how to prioritize messaging during a short 2–3 minute pitch):
1) ML engineer (fast path)
- Primary concerns: output quality, iteration velocity (evals/prompting), reproducibility, KV cache and context handling, experimenting with quantization/model variants.
- Key proof points: model catalog with verified performance profiles; quick eval harness; redwood Optimize recommendations; reproducible rollouts (pin model versions to experiments).
- Objections & responses:
  - \"We already run local experiments / huggingface models\": \"Great — Redwood plugs into that workflow: same model artifacts can be served with optimized kernels and automatic KV caching so you get interactive latency without rewriting your infra.\"
  - \"Quality varies across quantized variants\": \"We publish model+quantization quality deltas and provide A/B eval tooling so you can guardrails regressions before rollout.\"
- Discovery questions:
  - \"How often do you iterate prompts vs change model weights?\"
  - \"Do you have automated evals tied to CI or is it manual?\"
- Demo hooks: live eval dashboard showing a regression alert, quick 2-click canary rollback to previous model version.

2) Backend engineer (fast path)
- Primary concerns: latency tail, predictable throughput under burst, simple API integration, error handling and fallbacks.
- Key proof points: continuous batching, smart routing (fallback to cheaper model variant), token-level latency and cost breakdown in Console.
- Objections & responses:
  - \"We care about P95 latency spikes\": \"Redwood offers routing by latency SLO and autoscaling policies plus transparent per-route tracing to diagnose hot paths.\"
  - \"We already run inference in our services\": \"Consider Redwood as a predictability and ops layer: same HTTP/SDK interface but with caching, batching, and fallback policies you don't have to build and maintain.\"
- Discovery questions:
  - \"What percent of calls are < 1s vs > 1s today? Any hot keys or long-tail inputs?\"
  - \"How do you currently handle failover for model timeouts?\"
- Demo hooks: per-route latency breakdown; trace from request->model showing batching and cache hits; quick toggle of fallback policy.

3) Platform teams (fast path)
- Primary concerns: multi-tenant isolation, auditing/compliance, rollout governance, predictable cost across org, friendly procurement path (Dedicated/Private).
- Key proof points: Private deployment with customer-managed control plane, KMS/HSM integration, SLA-backed Dedicated tiers, RBAC and audit logs in Console.
- Objections & responses:
  - \"We have strict security controls and can't use hosted APIs\": \"Private offers an on-prem or VPC option with pluggable KMS/HSM plus an admin console that surfaces audit trails. We also provide a limited air-gapped bundle for regulated customers.\"
  - \"Vendor lock-in and migration risk\": \"Redwood exposes consistent APIs across hosted/dedicated/private and supports model artifacts you already use to minimize migration work.\"
- Discovery questions:
  - \"Do you have procurement constraints that require dedicated capacity contracts?\"
  - \"Which compliance frameworks are must-haves for a launch? e.g., SOC2, ISO, regional data residency?\"
- Demo hooks: show RBAC + audit trail for a sensitive model, show policy-driven routing by region/compliance, show Dedicated capacity contract UI.

Competitive quick-map (notes to salesperson):
- Managed LLM APIs (e.g., generalist cloud providers): strength = simplicity & scale; weakness = limited control over rollout, fewer model transparency guarantees. Message: emphasize Redwood’s model observability and rollout controls.
- Self-hosted runtimes (open-source runtimes / infra): strength = customizability; weakness = ops burden and unpredictable unit economics. Message: focus on \"production economics\" and offload of operational complexity while preserving model ownership.
- Inference vendors with tight hardware lock-ins: strength = optimized throughput; weakness = vendor/hardware lock and limited model support. Message: highlight our compilation/kernel selection that adapts to different hardware and route-by-policy to avoid lock-in.

Sample 90-second talk tracks (pick one per persona):
- ML engineer (90s): \"Hi — quick question: when you push new prompt sets or model fine-tunes, how confident are you that production quality won't regress? Redwood gives you a serving environment that connects to your model artifacts and runs automated evals and canary rollouts. You can see token-level diffs, quantization impact, and if anything goes wrong we automatically fall back to a safe variant — so you can iterate models faster without surprise defects in prod.\"

- Backend engineer (90s): \"Most infra teams I talk to are wrestling with unpredictable latencies when models get big. Redwood provides a single API that gives deterministic latency SLOs via batching and cache-aware serving. We also surface per-route cost and latency so you can budget and shard traffic predictably. Integration is an HTTP SDK and a few toggles for routing/fallback.\"

- Platform team (90s): \"For platform owners, your top ask is governance plus predictable ops. Redwood supports Dedicated capacity, private control planes, RBAC, audit logs, and KMS integration — and the exact same API your teams use in hosted mode. That means you can standardize inference across orgs without blocking model experimentation.\"

Playbook snippets (cheat sheet for quick prep):
- If hearing \"cost\": lead with Optimize recommendations and token-level cost UI; offer a batch-configuration review.
- If hearing \"quality/regression\": call out evals, model version pinning, and rollback tooling.
- If hearing \"security/compliance\": surface Private + audit + KMS and link to SOC2 summary doc.

Open questions / follow-ups to capture in CRM:
- Which model families do they use in prod? (gpt-style, instruction-tuned, web-scale LLMs)
- Current annual inference spend and target reduction %
- Existing compliance requirements and timelines
- Who owns procurement vs who owns technical eval/test sign-off?

References and quick links for reps:
- Confluence: performance-benchmarks-2024 (internal)
- Slack: #g2m-personas for playbook updates
- Jira: GTM-4321 (persona launch tasks)
- Demo deck PR: github.com/repo/marketing/playbooks/pulls/12

- Collect concrete customer anecdotes + numbers for backend latency wins and cost reductions.
- Bake an objection library by competitor (top 5 competitors) with one-liners and data points.
